revision.text:
{{Multiple issues | refimprove = March 2016 }} [[File:Sekibou.jpg|thumb|Stone rod ({{ill|Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties|ja|奈良県立橿原考古学研究所}} Museum)]] [[File:Kitazawagawa Daisekibo.jpg|thumb|The largest stone rod in Japan, the {{ill|Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|ja|北沢大石棒}} ''''Stone rods''' (せきぼう, sekibō) are a type of polished [[stone tool]] from the [[Jōmon period]] in Japan. They are believed to represent [[phalluses]] and are associated with [[rituals]] and [[shamanistic practices]].Yamada 2019: 204
The largest known stone rod is the {{ill|Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|ja|北沢大石棒}} in {{ill|Sakuho, Nagano|ja|佐久穂町}}, measuring 223 cm in length and 25 cm in diameter.
== Overview == In a broad sense, "stone rods" encompass various rod-shaped stone artifacts, including [[stone swords]] and [[stone daggers]]. In a narrower sense, they often refer specifically to large stone rods.Tori 1924 These artifacts have been found throughout Japan, from [[Kyushu]] to [[Hokkaido]].
Phallic stone artifacts date back to the [[Upper Paleolithic]] period, such as the one found at the {{ill|Masukata Site|ja|升形遺跡}} in {{ill|Ōami-Shirasato, Chiba|ja|大羽白里市}}, dated to approximately 24,000 years ago.Harunari 1996 The specific type known as "large stone rods" emerged during the Middle Jōmon period in the [[Chūbu region]] and later spread eastward to areas such as the [[Kinki region]].Yamamoto 1979Okada 1960
Sites where stone rods were manufactured have been identified in eastern Japan, such as the {{ill|Shioya Kanesayama Shrine Site|ja|塩屋金清神社遺跡}} and the {{ill|Shima Site|ja|島遺跡}} in {{ill|Gifu Prefecture|ja|岐阜県}},Hayakawa & Hayashi 2000Miyoshi & Nagata 2012 as well as in western Japan, such as the {{ill|Mikuraoka Site|ja|見蔵岡遺跡}} in {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県}} and the {{ill|Kamizato Site|ja|上里遺跡}} in {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府}}.Matsui, Oshita & Tamura 1997Takahashi & Kondo 2011 These findings suggest that the use of stone rods as ritual objects was adopted as a cultural practice across different regions.Oshita 2001
Some stone rods have been found near the [[hearths]] of dwellings, often showing signs of heat damage or discoloration. This indicates their use in fire-related [[rituals]]. Ritual practices involving stone rods are thought to symbolically represent the phases of male genital arousal, sexual activity, and post-ejaculatory flaccidity through actions such as striking, heating, and breaking the rods. The prevalence of broken stone rods in archaeological sites has led some researchers to attribute symbolic meanings to this practice, though others remain skeptical.Gotō 1999
Stone rods were also used as burial goods, suggesting multiple [[shamanistic]] or ritualistic purposes.Yamada 2019: 206Taniguchi 2006
[[File:Hinotodake Santyô.JPG|thumb|Stone rod at the summit of {{ill|Mt. Chōkaku|ja|丁岳}} on the border of {{ill|Akita Prefecture|ja|秋田県}} and {{ill|Yamagata Prefecture|ja|山形県}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading ==
Tanaka, Taku & Sahara, Makoto (Eds.) 2002. ''Encyclopedia of Japanese Archaeology''. Sanseidō. pp. 487–488.
Tamura, Kōichi & Aida, Yoshimasa (Supervisors) 2004. ''Basic Terminology of Archaeological Exploration''. Yamakawa Publishing. p. 74.
Yamada, Yasuhiro. 2019. ''The History of the Jōmon Period''. Kodansha Modern Library.
Tori, Ryūzō. 1924. "Stone Rods and Stone Swords." ''Suwa History,'' Volume 1. Shinano Educational Association Suwa Branch.
Harunari, Hideō. 1996. "The Archaeology of Sexual Symbols." ''Bulletin of the National Museum of Japanese History,'' Issue 66.
Okada, Shigehiro. 1960. "Regional Development of Jōmon Culture in Kinki." ''The Jōmon Period'' (Japanese Archaeology II). Kawade Shobō Shinsha.
Gotō, Shinsuke. 1999. "Research on Artifacts: Stone Rods, Stone Swords, and Stone Daggers." ''The Jōmon Period,'' Issue 10, Vol. 4. The Jōmon Period Cultural Research Society.
Taniguchi, Yasuhiro. 2006. "Stone Rods and Stone Plates: Contexts of Symbolic Reproductive Acts." ''Archaeology IV.'' Masato Anzai.
Yamamoto, Teruhisa. 1979. "Evolution of Rituals with Stone Rods (Parts 1 & 2)." ''Ancient Culture,'' Vol. 31, Issues 11–12. Association for Ancient Studies.
Hayakawa, Shoichi & Hayashi, Naoki. 2000. ''Excavation Report of the Shioya Kanesayama Shrine Site (A Area).'' Gifu Prefecture & Miyagawa Village Board of Education.
Miyoshi, Seichō & Nagata, Tomoya. 2012. ''The Shima and Shioya Kanesayama Sites.'' Hida City Board of Education.
Matsui, Takayo; Oshita, Akira; Tamura, Seiichirō. 1997. ''Mikuraoka Site II.'' Takenochō Board of Education.
Takahashi, Kiyoshi & Kondo, Nao. 2010. ''The Kamizato Site I: A Jōmon Late-Period Settlement Excavation.'' Kyoto City Archaeological Research Center Report No. 24.
Oshita, Akira. 2001. "Adoption and Transformation of Large Stone Rods in the Kinki Region (Parts 1 & 2)." ''Stone Ritual Implements in the Transition from Jōmon to Yayoi Periods III.'' Spring Research Lab, National Museum of Japanese History.
== See Also ==

[[Snake deity]]

{{Multiple issues | refimprove = March 2016 }} [[File:Sekibou.jpg|thumb|Stone rod ({{ill|Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties|ja|奈良県立橿原考古学研究所}} Museum)]] [[File:Kitazawagawa Daisekibo.jpg|thumb|The largest stone rod in Japan, the {{ill|Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|ja|北沢大石棒}} ''''Stone rods''' (せきぼう, sekibō) are a type of polished [[stone tool]] from the [[Jōmon period]] in Japan. They are believed to represent [[phalluses]] and are associated with [[rituals]] and [[shamanistic practices]].Yamada 2019: 204
The largest known stone rod is the {{ill|Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|ja|北沢大石棒}} in {{ill|Sakuho, Nagano|ja|佐久穂町}}, measuring 223 cm in length and 25 cm in diameter.
== Overview == In a broad sense, "stone rods" encompass various rod-shaped stone artifacts, including [[stone swords]] and [[stone daggers]]. In a narrower sense, they often refer specifically to large stone rods.Tori 1924 These artifacts have been found throughout Japan, from [[Kyushu]] to [[Hokkaido]].
Phallic stone artifacts date back to the [[Upper Paleolithic]] period, such as the one found at the {{ill|Masukata Site|ja|升形遺跡}} in {{ill|Ōami-Shirasato, Chiba|ja|大羽白里市}}, dated to approximately 24,000 years ago.Harunari 1996 The specific type known as "large stone rods" emerged during the Middle Jōmon period in the [[Chūbu region]] and later spread eastward to areas such as the [[Kinki region]].Yamamoto 1979Okada 1960
Sites where stone rods were manufactured have been identified in eastern Japan, such as the {{ill|Shioya Kanesayama Shrine Site|ja|塩屋金清神社遺跡}} and the {{ill|Shima Site|ja|島遺跡}} in {{ill|Gifu Prefecture|ja|岐阜県}},Hayakawa & Hayashi 2000Miyoshi & Nagata 2012 as well as in western Japan, such as the {{ill|Mikuraoka Site|ja|見蔵岡遺跡}} in {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県}} and the {{ill|Kamizato Site|ja|上里遺跡}} in {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府}}.Matsui, Oshita & Tamura 1997Takahashi & Kondo 2011 These findings suggest that the use of stone rods as ritual objects was adopted as a cultural practice across different regions.Oshita 2001
Some stone rods have been found near the [[hearths]] of dwellings, often showing signs of heat damage or discoloration. This indicates their use in fire-related [[rituals]]. Ritual practices involving stone rods are thought to symbolically represent the phases of male genital arousal, sexual activity, and post-ejaculatory flaccidity through actions such as striking, heating, and breaking the rods. The prevalence of broken stone rods in archaeological sites has led some researchers to attribute symbolic meanings to this practice, though others remain skeptical.Gotō 1999
Stone rods were also used as burial goods, suggesting multiple [[shamanistic]] or ritualistic purposes.Yamada 2019: 206Taniguchi 2006
[[File:Hinotodake Santyô.JPG|thumb|Stone rod at the summit of {{ill|Mt. Chōkaku|ja|丁岳}} on the border of {{ill|Akita Prefecture|ja|秋田県}} and {{ill|Yamagata Prefecture|ja|山形県}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading ==
Tanaka, Taku & Sahara, Makoto (Eds.) 2002. ''Encyclopedia of Japanese Archaeology''. Sanseidō. pp. 487–488.
Tamura, Kōichi & Aida, Yoshimasa (Supervisors) 2004. ''Basic Terminology of Archaeological Exploration''. Yamakawa Publishing. p. 74.
Yamada, Yasuhiro. 2019. ''The History of the Jōmon Period''. Kodansha Modern Library.
Tori, Ryūzō. 1924. "Stone Rods and Stone Swords." ''Suwa History,'' Volume 1. Shinano Educational Association Suwa Branch.
Harunari, Hideō. 1996. "The Archaeology of Sexual Symbols." ''Bulletin of the National Museum of Japanese History,'' Issue 66.
Okada, Shigehiro. 1960. "Regional Development of Jōmon Culture in Kinki." ''The Jōmon Period'' (Japanese Archaeology II). Kawade Shobō Shinsha.
Gotō, Shinsuke. 1999. "Research on Artifacts: Stone Rods, Stone Swords, and Stone Daggers." ''The Jōmon Period,'' Issue 10, Vol. 4. The Jōmon Period Cultural Research Society.
Taniguchi, Yasuhiro. 2006. "Stone Rods and Stone Plates: Contexts of Symbolic Reproductive Acts." ''Archaeology IV.'' Masato Anzai.
Yamamoto, Teruhisa. 1979. "Evolution of Rituals with Stone Rods (Parts 1 & 2)." ''Ancient Culture,'' Vol. 31, Issues 11–12. Association for Ancient Studies.
Hayakawa, Shoichi & Hayashi, Naoki. 2000. ''Excavation Report of the Shioya Kanesayama Shrine Site (A Area).'' Gifu Prefecture & Miyagawa Village Board of Education.
Miyoshi, Seichō & Nagata, Tomoya. 2012. ''The Shima and Shioya Kanesayama Sites.'' Hida City Board of Education.
Matsui, Takayo; Oshita, Akira; Tamura, Seiichirō. 1997. ''Mikuraoka Site II.'' Takenochō Board of Education.
Takahashi, Kiyoshi & Kondo, Nao. 2010. ''The Kamizato Site I: A Jōmon Late-Period Settlement Excavation.'' Kyoto City Archaeological Research Center Report No. 24.
Oshita, Akira. 2001. "Adoption and Transformation of Large Stone Rods in the Kinki Region (Parts 1 & 2)." ''Stone Ritual Implements in the Transition from Jōmon to Yayoi Periods III.'' Spring Research Lab, National Museum of Japanese History.

{{Multiple issues | refimprove = March 2016 }} [[File:Sekibou.jpg|thumb|Stone rod ({{ill|Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties|ja|奈良県立橿原考古学研究所}} Museum)]] [[File:Kitazawagawa Daisekibo.jpg|thumb|The largest stone rod in Japan, the {{ill|Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|ja|北沢大石棒}} ''''Stone rods''' (せきぼう, sekibō) are a type of polished [[stone tool]] from the [[Jōmon period]] in Japan. They are believed to represent [[phalluses]] and are associated with [[rituals]] and [[shamanistic practices]].Yamada 2019: 204
The largest known stone rod is the {{ill|Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|ja|北沢大石棒}} in {{ill|Sakuho, Nagano|ja|佐久穂町}}, measuring 223 cm in length and 25 cm in diameter.
== Overview == In a broad sense, "stone rods" encompass various rod-shaped stone artifacts, including [[stone swords]] and [[stone daggers]]. In a narrower sense, they often refer specifically to large stone rods.Tori 1924 These artifacts have been found throughout Japan, from [[Kyushu]] to [[Hokkaido]].
Phallic stone artifacts date back to the [[Upper Paleolithic]] period, such as the one found at the {{ill|Masukata Site|ja|升形遺跡}} in {{ill|Ōami-Shirasato, Chiba|ja|大羽白里市}}, dated to approximately 24,000 years ago.Harunari 1996 The specific type known as "large stone rods" emerged during the Middle Jōmon period in the [[Chūbu region]] and later spread eastward to areas such as the [[Kinki region]].Yamamoto 1979Okada 1960
Sites where stone rods were manufactured have been identified in eastern Japan, such as the {{ill|Shioya Kanesayama Shrine Site|ja|塩屋金清神社遺跡}} and the {{ill|Shima Site|ja|島遺跡}} in {{ill|Gifu Prefecture|ja|岐阜県}},Hayakawa & Hayashi 2000Miyoshi & Nagata 2012 as well as in western Japan, such as the {{ill|Mikuraoka Site|ja|見蔵岡遺跡}} in {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県}} and the {{ill|Kamizato Site|ja|上里遺跡}} in {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府}}.Matsui, Oshita & Tamura 1997Takahashi & Kondo 2011 These findings suggest that the use of stone rods as ritual objects was adopted as a cultural practice across different regions.Oshita 2001
Some stone rods have been found near the [[hearths]] of dwellings, often showing signs of heat damage or discoloration. This indicates their use in fire-related [[rituals]]. Ritual practices involving stone rods are thought to symbolically represent the phases of male genital arousal, sexual activity, and post-ejaculatory flaccidity through actions such as striking, heating, and breaking the rods. The prevalence of broken stone rods in archaeological sites has led some researchers to attribute symbolic meanings to this practice, though others remain skeptical.Gotō 1999
Stone rods were also used as burial goods, suggesting multiple [[shamanistic]] or ritualistic purposes.Yamada 2019: 206Taniguchi 2006
[[File:Hinotodake Santyô.JPG|thumb|Stone rod at the summit of {{ill|Mt. Chōkaku|ja|丁岳}} on the border of {{ill|Akita Prefecture|ja|秋田県}} and {{ill|Yamagata Prefecture|ja|山形県}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading ==
Tanaka, Taku & Sahara, Makoto (Eds.) 2002. ''Encyclopedia of Japanese Archaeology''. Sanseidō. pp. 487–488.
Tamura, Kōichi & Aida, Yoshimasa (Supervisors) 2004. ''Basic Terminology of Archaeological Exploration''. Yamakawa Publishing. p. 74.
Yamada, Yasuhiro. 2019. ''The History of the Jōmon Period''. Kodansha Modern Library.
Tori, Ryūzō. 1924. "Stone Rods and Stone Swords." ''Suwa History,'' Volume 1. Shinano Educational Association Suwa Branch.
Harunari, Hideō. 1996. "The Archaeology of Sexual Symbols." ''Bulletin of the National Museum of Japanese History,'' Issue 66.
Okada, Shigehiro. 1960. "Regional Development of Jōmon Culture in Kinki." ''The Jōmon Period'' (Japanese Archaeology II). Kawade Shobō Shinsha.
Gotō, Shinsuke. 1999. "Research on Artifacts: Stone Rods, Stone Swords, and Stone Daggers." ''The Jōmon Period,'' Issue 10, Vol. 4. The Jōmon Period Cultural Research Society.
Taniguchi, Yasuhiro. 2006. "Stone Rods and Stone Plates: Contexts of Symbolic Reproductive Acts." ''Archaeology IV.'' Masato Anzai.
Yamamoto, Teruhisa. 1979. "Evolution of Rituals with Stone Rods (Parts 1 & 2)." ''Ancient Culture,'' Vol. 31, Issues 11–12. Association for Ancient Studies.
Hayakawa, Shoichi & Hayashi, Naoki. 2000. ''Excavation Report of the Shioya Kanesayama Shrine Site (A Area).'' Gifu Prefecture & Miyagawa Village Board of Education.
Miyoshi, Seichō & Nagata, Tomoya. 2012. ''The Shima and Shioya Kanesayama Sites.'' Hida City Board of Education.
Matsui, Takayo; Oshita, Akira; Tamura, Seiichirō. 1997. ''Mikuraoka Site II.'' Takenochō Board of Education.
Takahashi, Kiyoshi & Kondo, Nao. 2010. ''The Kamizato Site I: A Jōmon Late-Period Settlement Excavation.'' Kyoto City Archaeological Research Center Report No. 24.
Oshita, Akira. 2001. "Adoption and Transformation of Large Stone Rods in the Kinki Region (Parts 1 & 2)." ''Stone Ritual Implements in the Transition from Jōmon to Yayoi Periods III.'' Spring Research Lab, National Museum of Japanese History.

[[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[en:Ishibou]][[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:bump 1-26]] [[Category:refreshing redirects 1-27]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Ishibou]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Ishibou]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:pgoodbye $1]][[Category:null category]][[Category:Ishibou]][[Category:pgoodbye $1]][[Category:null category]][[Category:deprecated-qqs]]<!---->[[Category:qq deprecated close]] [[en:Ishibou]][[en:Ishibou]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:SuQqqqqq]] [[Category:qqqq]] [[sn:Ishibou]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Ishibou]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Ishibou]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Ishibou]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Ishibou]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Null category]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Ishibou]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Ishibou]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Removing this excess]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:if these are dates then move them to completion tier 5]][[sn:Ishibou]][[Category:if these are dates then move them to completion tier 5]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Ishibou]][[Category:qq]]

{{Multiple issues | refimprove = March 2016 }} [[File:Sekibou.jpg|thumb|Stone rod ({{ill|Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties|ja|奈良県立橿原考古学研究所}} Museum)]] [[File:Kitazawagawa Daisekibo.jpg|thumb|The largest stone rod in Japan, the {{ill|Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|ja|北沢大石棒}} ''''Stone rods''' (せきぼう, sekibō) are a type of polished [[stone tool]] from the [[Jōmon period]] in Japan. They are believed to represent [[phalluses]] and are associated with [[rituals]] and [[shamanistic practices]].Yamada 2019: 204
The largest known stone rod is the {{ill|Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|ja|北沢大石棒}} in {{ill|Sakuho, Nagano|ja|佐久穂町}}, measuring 223 cm in length and 25 cm in diameter.
== Overview == In a broad sense, "stone rods" encompass various rod-shaped stone artifacts, including [[stone swords]] and [[stone daggers]]. In a narrower sense, they often refer specifically to large stone rods.Tori 1924 These artifacts have been found throughout Japan, from [[Kyushu]] to [[Hokkaido]].
Phallic stone artifacts date back to the [[Upper Paleolithic]] period, such as the one found at the {{ill|Masukata Site|ja|升形遺跡}} in {{ill|Ōami-Shirasato, Chiba|ja|大羽白里市}}, dated to approximately 24,000 years ago.Harunari 1996 The specific type known as "large stone rods" emerged during the Middle Jōmon period in the [[Chūbu region]] and later spread eastward to areas such as the [[Kinki region]].Yamamoto 1979Okada 1960
Sites where stone rods were manufactured have been identified in eastern Japan, such as the {{ill|Shioya Kanesayama Shrine Site|ja|塩屋金清神社遺跡}} and the {{ill|Shima Site|ja|島遺跡}} in {{ill|Gifu Prefecture|ja|岐阜県}},Hayakawa & Hayashi 2000Miyoshi & Nagata 2012 as well as in western Japan, such as the {{ill|Mikuraoka Site|ja|見蔵岡遺跡}} in {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県}} and the {{ill|Kamizato Site|ja|上里遺跡}} in {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府}}.Matsui, Oshita & Tamura 1997Takahashi & Kondo 2011 These findings suggest that the use of stone rods as ritual objects was adopted as a cultural practice across different regions.Oshita 2001
Some stone rods have been found near the [[hearths]] of dwellings, often showing signs of heat damage or discoloration. This indicates their use in fire-related [[rituals]]. Ritual practices involving stone rods are thought to symbolically represent the phases of male genital arousal, sexual activity, and post-ejaculatory flaccidity through actions such as striking, heating, and breaking the rods. The prevalence of broken stone rods in archaeological sites has led some researchers to attribute symbolic meanings to this practice, though others remain skeptical.Gotō 1999
Stone rods were also used as burial goods, suggesting multiple [[shamanistic]] or ritualistic purposes.Yamada 2019: 206Taniguchi 2006
[[File:Hinotodake Santyô.JPG|thumb|Stone rod at the summit of {{ill|Mt. Chōkaku|ja|丁岳}} on the border of {{ill|Akita Prefecture|ja|秋田県}} and {{ill|Yamagata Prefecture|ja|山形県}}

Tamura, Kōichi & Aida, Yoshimasa (Supervisors) 2004. ''Basic Terminology of Archaeological Exploration''. Yamakawa Publishing. p. 74.
Yamada, Yasuhiro. 2019. ''The History of the Jōmon Period''. Kodansha Modern Library.
Tori, Ryūzō. 1924. "Stone Rods and Stone Swords." ''Suwa History,'' Volume 1. Shinano Educational Association Suwa Branch.
Harunari, Hideō. 1996. "The Archaeology of Sexual Symbols." ''Bulletin of the National Museum of Japanese History,'' Issue 66.
Okada, Shigehiro. 1960. "Regional Development of Jōmon Culture in Kinki." ''The Jōmon Period'' (Japanese Archaeology II). Kawade Shobō Shinsha.

Taniguchi, Yasuhiro. 2006. "Stone Rods and Stone Plates: Contexts of Symbolic Reproductive Acts." ''Archaeology IV.'' Masato Anzai.
Yamamoto, Teruhisa. 1979. "Evolution of Rituals with Stone Rods (Parts 1 & 2)." ''Ancient Culture,'' Vol. 31, Issues 11–12. Association for Ancient Studies.
Hayakawa, Shoichi & Hayashi, Naoki. 2000. ''Excavation Report of the Shioya Kanesayama Shrine Site (A Area).'' Gifu Prefecture & Miyagawa Village Board of Education.
Miyoshi, Seichō & Nagata, Tomoya. 2012. ''The Shima and Shioya Kanesayama Sites.'' Hida City Board of Education.
Matsui, Takayo; Oshita, Akira; Tamura, Seiichirō. 1997. ''Mikuraoka Site II.'' Takenochō Board of Education.

[[Category:SuQqqqqq]] [[Category:qqqq]] [[sn:Ishibou]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Ishibou]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Ishibou]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Ishibou]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Ishibou]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Null category]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Ishibou]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Ishibou]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Removing this excess]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:if these are dates then move them to completion tier 5]][[sn:Ishibou]][[Category:if these are dates then move them to completion tier 5]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Ishibou]][[Category:qq]]

{{Multiple issues | refimprove = March 2016 }} [[File:Sekibou.jpg|thumb|Stone rod ({{ill|Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties|ja|奈良県立橿原考古学研究所}} Museum)]] [[File:Kitazawagawa Daisekibo.jpg|thumb|The largest stone rod in Japan, the {{ill|Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|ja|北沢大石棒}}]] ''''Stone rods''' (せきぼう, sekibō) are a type of polished [[stone tool]] from the [[Jōmon period]] in Japan. They are believed to represent [[phalluses]] and are associated with [[rituals]] and [[shamanistic practices]].Yamada 2019: 204
The largest known stone rod is the {{ill|Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|ja|北沢大石棒}} in {{ill|Sakuho, Nagano|ja|佐久穂町}}, measuring 223 cm in length and 25 cm in diameter.
== Overview == In a broad sense, "stone rods" encompass various rod-shaped stone artifacts, including [[stone swords]] and [[stone daggers]]. In a narrower sense, they often refer specifically to large stone rods.Tori 1924 These artifacts have been found throughout Japan, from [[Kyushu]] to [[Hokkaido]].
Phallic stone artifacts date back to the [[Upper Paleolithic]] period, such as the one found at the {{ill|Masukata Site|ja|升形遺跡}} in {{ill|Ōami-Shirasato, Chiba|ja|大羽白里市}}, dated to approximately 24,000 years ago.Harunari 1996 The specific type known as "large stone rods" emerged during the Middle Jōmon period in the [[Chūbu region]] and later spread eastward to areas such as the [[Kinki region]].Yamamoto 1979Okada 1960
Sites where stone rods were manufactured have been identified in eastern Japan, such as the {{ill|Shioya Kanesayama Shrine Site|ja|塩屋金清神社遺跡}} and the {{ill|Shima Site|ja|島遺跡}} in {{ill|Gifu Prefecture|ja|岐阜県}},Hayakawa & Hayashi 2000Miyoshi & Nagata 2012 as well as in western Japan, such as the {{ill|Mikuraoka Site|ja|見蔵岡遺跡}} in {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県}} and the {{ill|Kamizato Site|ja|上里遺跡}} in {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府}}.Matsui, Oshita & Tamura 1997Takahashi & Kondo 2011 These findings suggest that the use of stone rods as ritual objects was adopted as a cultural practice across different regions.Oshita 2001
Some stone rods have been found near the [[hearths]] of dwellings, often showing signs of heat damage or discoloration. This indicates their use in fire-related [[rituals]]. Ritual practices involving stone rods are thought to symbolically represent the phases of male genital arousal, sexual activity, and post-ejaculatory flaccidity through actions such as striking, heating, and breaking the rods. The prevalence of broken stone rods in archaeological sites has led some researchers to attribute symbolic meanings to this practice, though others remain skeptical.Gotō 1999
Stone rods were also used as burial goods, suggesting multiple [[shamanistic]] or ritualistic purposes.Yamada 2019: 206Taniguchi 2006
[[File:Hinotodake Santyô.JPG|thumb|Stone rod at the summit of {{ill|Mt. Chōkaku|ja|丁岳}} on the border of {{ill|Akita Prefecture|ja|秋田県}} and {{ill|Yamagata Prefecture|ja|山形県}}]]

Miyoshi, Seichō & Nagata, Tomoya. 2012. ''The Shima and Shioya Kanesayama Sites.'' Hida City Board of Education.

[[Category:SuQqqqqq]] [[Category:qqqq]] [[sn:Ishibou]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Ishibou]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Ishibou]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Ishibou]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Ishibou]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Null category]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Ishibou]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Ishibou]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Removing this excess]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:if these are dates then move them to completion tier 5]][[sn:Ishibou]][[Category:if these are dates then move them to completion tier 5]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Ishibou]][[Category:qq]]

{{Multiple issues | refimprove = March 2016 }} [[File:Sekibou.jpg|thumb|Stone rod ({{ill|Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties|ja|奈良県立橿原考古学研究所}} Museum)]] [[File:Kitazawagawa Daisekibo.jpg|thumb|The largest stone rod in Japan, the {{ill|Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|ja|北沢大石棒}}]] ''''Stone rods''' (せきぼう, sekibō) are a type of polished [[stone tool]] from the [[Jōmon period]] in Japan. They are believed to represent [[phalluses]] and are associated with [[rituals]] and [[shamanistic practices]].Yamada 2019: 204
The largest known stone rod is the {{ill|Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|ja|北沢大石棒}} in {{ill|Sakuho, Nagano|ja|佐久穂町}}, measuring 223 cm in length and 25 cm in diameter.
== Overview == In a broad sense, "stone rods" encompass various rod-shaped stone artifacts, including [[stone swords]] and [[stone daggers]]. In a narrower sense, they often refer specifically to large stone rods.Tori 1924 These artifacts have been found throughout Japan, from [[Kyushu]] to [[Hokkaido]].
Phallic stone artifacts date back to the [[Upper Paleolithic]] period, such as the one found at the {{ill|Masukata Site|ja|升形遺跡}} in {{ill|Ōami-Shirasato, Chiba|ja|大羽白里市}}, dated to approximately 24,000 years ago.Harunari 1996 The specific type known as "large stone rods" emerged during the Middle Jōmon period in the [[Chūbu region]] and later spread eastward to areas such as the [[Kinki region]].Yamamoto 1979Okada 1960
Sites where stone rods were manufactured have been identified in eastern Japan, such as the {{ill|Shioya Kanesayama Shrine Site|ja|塩屋金清神社遺跡}} and the {{ill|Shima Site|ja|島遺跡}} in {{ill|Gifu Prefecture|ja|岐阜県}},Hayakawa & Hayashi 2000Miyoshi & Nagata 2012 as well as in western Japan, such as the {{ill|Mikuraoka Site|ja|見蔵岡遺跡}} in {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県}} and the {{ill|Kamizato Site|ja|上里遺跡}} in {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府}}.Matsui, Oshita & Tamura 1997Takahashi & Kondo 2011 These findings suggest that the use of stone rods as ritual objects was adopted as a cultural practice across different regions.Oshita 2001
Some stone rods have been found near the [[hearths]] of dwellings, often showing signs of heat damage or discoloration. This indicates their use in fire-related [[rituals]]. Ritual practices involving stone rods are thought to symbolically represent the phases of male genital arousal, sexual activity, and post-ejaculatory flaccidity through actions such as striking, heating, and breaking the rods. The prevalence of broken stone rods in archaeological sites has led some researchers to attribute symbolic meanings to this practice, though others remain skeptical.Gotō 1999
Stone rods were also used as burial goods, suggesting multiple [[shamanistic]] or ritualistic purposes.Yamada 2019: 206Taniguchi 2006
[[File:Hinotodake Santyô.JPG|thumb|Stone rod at the summit of {{ill|Mt. Chōkaku|ja|丁岳}} on the border of {{ill|Akita Prefecture|ja|秋田県}} and {{ill|Yamagata Prefecture|ja|山形県}}]]
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading ==
Tanaka, Taku & Sahara, Makoto (Eds.) 2002. ''Encyclopedia of Japanese Archaeology''. Sanseidō. pp. 487–488.
Tamura, Kōichi & Aida, Yoshimasa (Supervisors) 2004. ''Basic Terminology of Archaeological Exploration''. Yamakawa Publishing. p. 74.
Yamada, Yasuhiro. 2019. ''The History of the Jōmon Period''. Kodansha Modern Library.
Tori, Ryūzō. 1924. "Stone Rods and Stone Swords." ''Suwa History,'' Volume 1. Shinano Educational Association Suwa Branch.
Harunari, Hideō. 1996. "The Archaeology of Sexual Symbols." ''Bulletin of the National Museum of Japanese History,'' Issue 66.
Okada, Shigehiro. 1960. "Regional Development of Jōmon Culture in Kinki." ''The Jōmon Period'' (Japanese Archaeology II). Kawade Shobō Shinsha.
Gotō, Shinsuke. 1999. "Research on Artifacts: Stone Rods, Stone Swords, and Stone Daggers." ''The Jōmon Period,'' Issue 10, Vol. 4. The Jōmon Period Cultural Research Society.
Taniguchi, Yasuhiro. 2006. "Stone Rods and Stone Plates: Contexts of Symbolic Reproductive Acts." ''Archaeology IV.'' Masato Anzai.
Yamamoto, Teruhisa. 1979. "Evolution of Rituals with Stone Rods (Parts 1 & 2)." ''Ancient Culture,'' Vol. 31, Issues 11–12. Association for Ancient Studies.
Hayakawa, Shoichi & Hayashi, Naoki. 2000. ''Excavation Report of the Shioya Kanesayama Shrine Site (A Area).'' Gifu Prefecture & Miyagawa Village Board of Education.
Miyoshi, Seichō & Nagata, Tomoya. 2012. ''The Shima and Shioya Kanesayama Sites.'' Hida City Board of Education.
Matsui, Takayo; Oshita, Akira; Tamura, Seiichirō. 1997. ''Mikuraoka Site II.'' Takenochō Board of Education.
Takahashi, Kiyoshi & Kondo, Nao. 2010. ''The Kamizato Site I: A Jōmon Late-Period Settlement Excavation.'' Kyoto City Archaeological Research Center Report No. 24.
Oshita, Akira. 2001. "Adoption and Transformation of Large Stone Rods in the Kinki Region (Parts 1 & 2)." ''Stone Ritual Implements in the Transition from Jōmon to Yayoi Periods III.'' Spring Research Lab, National Museum of Japanese History.

{{Multiple issues | refimprove = March 2016 }} [[File:Sekibou.jpg|thumb|Stone rod ({{ill|Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties|ja|奈良県立橿原考古学研究所}} Museum)]] [[File:Kitazawagawa Daisekibo.jpg|thumb|The largest stone rod in Japan, the {{ill|Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|ja|北沢大石棒}}]] ''''Stone rods''' (せきぼう, sekibō) are a type of polished [[stone tool]] from the [[Jōmon period]] in Japan. They are believed to represent [[phalluses]] and are associated with [[rituals]] and [[shamanistic practices]].Yamada 2019: 204
The largest known stone rod is the {{ill|Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|ja|北沢大石棒}} in {{ill|Sakuho, Nagano|ja|佐久穂町}}, measuring 223 cm in length and 25 cm in diameter.
== Overview == In a broad sense, "stone rods" encompass various rod-shaped stone artifacts, including [[stone swords]] and [[stone daggers]]. In a narrower sense, they often refer specifically to large stone rods.Tori 1924 These artifacts have been found throughout Japan, from [[Kyushu]] to [[Hokkaido]].
Phallic stone artifacts date back to the [[Upper Paleolithic]] period, such as the one found at the {{ill|Masukata Site|ja|升形遺跡}} in {{ill|Ōami-Shirasato, Chiba|ja|大羽白里市}}, dated to approximately 24,000 years ago.Harunari 1996 The specific type known as "large stone rods" emerged during the Middle Jōmon period in the [[Chūbu region]] and later spread eastward to areas such as the [[Kinki region]].Yamamoto 1979Okada 1960
Sites where stone rods were manufactured have been identified in eastern Japan, such as the {{ill|Shioya Kanesayama Shrine Site|ja|塩屋金清神社遺跡}} and the {{ill|Shima Site|ja|島遺跡}} in {{ill|Gifu Prefecture|ja|岐阜県}},Hayakawa & Hayashi 2000Miyoshi & Nagata 2012 as well as in western Japan, such as the {{ill|Mikuraoka Site|ja|見蔵岡遺跡}} in {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県}} and the {{ill|Kamizato Site|ja|上里遺跡}} in {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府}}.Matsui, Oshita & Tamura 1997Takahashi & Kondo 2011 These findings suggest that the use of stone rods as ritual objects was adopted as a cultural practice across different regions.Oshita 2001
Some stone rods have been found near the [[hearths]] of dwellings, often showing signs of heat damage or discoloration. This indicates their use in fire-related [[rituals]]. Ritual practices involving stone rods are thought to symbolically represent the phases of male genital arousal, sexual activity, and post-ejaculatory flaccidity through actions such as striking, heating, and breaking the rods. The prevalence of broken stone rods in archaeological sites has led some researchers to attribute symbolic meanings to this practice, though others remain skeptical.Gotō 1999
Stone rods were also used as burial goods, suggesting multiple [[shamanistic]] or ritualistic purposes.Yamada 2019: 206Taniguchi 2006
[[File:Hinotodake Santyô.JPG|thumb|Stone rod at the summit of {{ill|Mt. Chōkaku|ja|丁岳}} on the border of {{ill|Akita Prefecture|ja|秋田県}} and {{ill|Yamagata Prefecture|ja|山形県}}]]
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading ==
Tanaka, Taku & Sahara, Makoto (Eds.) 2002. ''Encyclopedia of Japanese Archaeology''. Sanseidō. pp. 487–488.
Tamura, Kōichi & Aida, Yoshimasa (Supervisors) 2004. ''Basic Terminology of Archaeological Exploration''. Yamakawa Publishing. p. 74.
Yamada, Yasuhiro. 2019. ''The History of the Jōmon Period''. Kodansha Modern Library.
Tori, Ryūzō. 1924. "Stone Rods and Stone Swords." ''Suwa History,'' Volume 1. Shinano Educational Association Suwa Branch.
Harunari, Hideō. 1996. "The Archaeology of Sexual Symbols." ''Bulletin of the National Museum of Japanese History,'' Issue 66.
Okada, Shigehiro. 1960. "Regional Development of Jōmon Culture in Kinki." ''The Jōmon Period'' (Japanese Archaeology II). Kawade Shobō Shinsha.
Gotō, Shinsuke. 1999. "Research on Artifacts: Stone Rods, Stone Swords, and Stone Daggers." ''The Jōmon Period,'' Issue 10, Vol. 4. The Jōmon Period Cultural Research Society.
Taniguchi, Yasuhiro. 2006. "Stone Rods and Stone Plates: Contexts of Symbolic Reproductive Acts." ''Archaeology IV.'' Masato Anzai.
Yamamoto, Teruhisa. 1979. "Evolution of Rituals with Stone Rods (Parts 1 & 2)." ''Ancient Culture,'' Vol. 31, Issues 11–12. Association for Ancient Studies.
Hayakawa, Shoichi & Hayashi, Naoki. 2000. ''Excavation Report of the Shioya Kanesayama Shrine Site (A Area).'' Gifu Prefecture & Miyagawa Village Board of Education.
Miyoshi, Seichō & Nagata, Tomoya. 2012. ''The Shima and Shioya Kanesayama Sites.'' Hida City Board of Education.
Matsui, Takayo; Oshita, Akira; Tamura, Seiichirō. 1997. ''Mikuraoka Site II.'' Takenochō Board of Education.
Takahashi, Kiyoshi & Kondo, Nao. 2010. ''The Kamizato Site I: A Jōmon Late-Period Settlement Excavation.'' Kyoto City Archaeological Research Center Report No. 24.
Oshita, Akira. 2001. "Adoption and Transformation of Large Stone Rods in the Kinki Region (Parts 1 & 2)." ''Stone Ritual Implements in the Transition from Jōmon to Yayoi Periods III.'' Spring Research Lab, National Museum of Japanese History.

{{Multiple issues | refimprove = March 2016 }} [[File:Sekibou.jpg|thumb|Stone rod ({{ill|Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties|ja|奈良県立橿原考古学研究所}} Museum)]] [[File:Kitazawagawa Daisekibo.jpg|thumb|The largest stone rod in Japan, the {{ill|Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|ja|北沢大石棒}}]] ''''Stone rods''' (せきぼう, sekibō) are a type of polished [[stone tool]] from the [[Jōmon period]] in Japan. They are believed to represent [[phalluses]] and are associated with [[rituals]] and [[shamanistic practices]].Yamada 2019: 204
The largest known stone rod is the {{ill|Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|ja|北沢大石棒}} in {{ill|Sakuho, Nagano|ja|佐久穂町}}, measuring 223 cm in length and 25 cm in diameter.
== Overview == In a broad sense, "stone rods" encompass various rod-shaped stone artifacts, including [[stone swords]] and [[stone daggers]]. In a narrower sense, they often refer specifically to large stone rods.Tori 1924 These artifacts have been found throughout Japan, from [[Kyushu]] to [[Hokkaido]].
Phallic stone artifacts date back to the [[Upper Paleolithic]] period, such as the one found at the {{ill|Masukata Site|ja|升形遺跡}} in {{ill|Ōami-Shirasato, Chiba|ja|大羽白里市}}, dated to approximately 24,000 years ago.Harunari 1996 The specific type known as "large stone rods" emerged during the Middle Jōmon period in the [[Chūbu region]] and later spread eastward to areas such as the [[Kinki region]].Yamamoto 1979Okada 1960
Sites where stone rods were manufactured have been identified in eastern Japan, such as the {{ill|Shioya Kanesayama Shrine Site|ja|塩屋金清神社遺跡}} and the {{ill|Shima Site|ja|島遺跡}} in {{ill|Gifu Prefecture|ja|岐阜県}},Hayakawa & Hayashi 2000Miyoshi & Nagata 2012 as well as in western Japan, such as the {{ill|Mikuraoka Site|ja|見蔵岡遺跡}} in {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県}} and the {{ill|Kamizato Site|ja|上里遺跡}} in {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府}}.Matsui, Oshita & Tamura 1997Takahashi & Kondo 2011 These findings suggest that the use of stone rods as ritual objects was adopted as a cultural practice across different regions.Oshita 2001
Some stone rods have been found near the [[hearths]] of dwellings, often showing signs of heat damage or discoloration. This indicates their use in fire-related [[rituals]]. Ritual practices involving stone rods are thought to symbolically represent the phases of male genital arousal, sexual activity, and post-ejaculatory flaccidity through actions such as striking, heating, and breaking the rods. The prevalence of broken stone rods in archaeological sites has led some researchers to attribute symbolic meanings to this practice, though others remain skeptical.Gotō 1999
Stone rods were also used as burial goods, suggesting multiple [[shamanistic]] or ritualistic purposes.Yamada 2019: 206Taniguchi 2006
[[File:Hinotodake Santyô.JPG|thumb|Stone rod at the summit of {{ill|Mt. Chōkaku|ja|丁岳}} on the border of {{ill|Akita Prefecture|ja|秋田県}} and {{ill|Yamagata Prefecture|ja|山形県}}]]

== Further Reading ==
Tanaka, Taku & Sahara, Makoto (Eds.) 2002. ''Encyclopedia of Japanese Archaeology''. Sanseidō. pp. 487–488.
Tamura, Kōichi & Aida, Yoshimasa (Supervisors) 2004. ''Basic Terminology of Archaeological Exploration''. Yamakawa Publishing. p. 74.
Yamada, Yasuhiro. 2019. ''The History of the Jōmon Period''. Kodansha Modern Library.
Tori, Ryūzō. 1924. "Stone Rods and Stone Swords." ''Suwa History,'' Volume 1. Shinano Educational Association Suwa Branch.
Harunari, Hideō. 1996. "The Archaeology of Sexual Symbols." ''Bulletin of the National Museum of Japanese History,'' Issue 66.
Okada, Shigehiro. 1960. "Regional Development of Jōmon Culture in Kinki." ''The Jōmon Period'' (Japanese Archaeology II). Kawade Shobō Shinsha.
Gotō, Shinsuke. 1999. "Research on Artifacts: Stone Rods, Stone Swords, and Stone Daggers." ''The Jōmon Period,'' Issue 10, Vol. 4. The Jōmon Period Cultural Research Society.
Taniguchi, Yasuhiro. 2006. "Stone Rods and Stone Plates: Contexts of Symbolic Reproductive Acts." ''Archaeology IV.'' Masato Anzai.
Yamamoto, Teruhisa. 1979. "Evolution of Rituals with Stone Rods (Parts 1 & 2)." ''Ancient Culture,'' Vol. 31, Issues 11–12. Association for Ancient Studies.
Hayakawa, Shoichi & Hayashi, Naoki. 2000. ''Excavation Report of the Shioya Kanesayama Shrine Site (A Area).'' Gifu Prefecture & Miyagawa Village Board of Education.
Miyoshi, Seichō & Nagata, Tomoya. 2012. ''The Shima and Shioya Kanesayama Sites.'' Hida City Board of Education.
Matsui, Takayo; Oshita, Akira; Tamura, Seiichirō. 1997. ''Mikuraoka Site II.'' Takenochō Board of Education.
Takahashi, Kiyoshi & Kondo, Nao. 2010. ''The Kamizato Site I: A Jōmon Late-Period Settlement Excavation.'' Kyoto City Archaeological Research Center Report No. 24.
Oshita, Akira. 2001. "Adoption and Transformation of Large Stone Rods in the Kinki Region (Parts 1 & 2)." ''Stone Ritual Implements in the Transition from Jōmon to Yayoi Periods III.'' Spring Research Lab, National Museum of Japanese History.

{{Multiple issues | refimprove = March 2016 }} [[File:Sekibou.jpg|thumb|Stone rod ({{ill|Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties|ja|奈良県立橿原考古学研究所}} Museum)]] [[File:Kitazawagawa Daisekibo.jpg|thumb|The largest stone rod in Japan, the {{ill|Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|ja|北沢大石棒}}]] ''''Stone rods''' (せきぼう, sekibō) are a type of polished [[stone tool]] from the [[Jōmon period]] in Japan. They are believed to represent [[phalluses]] and are associated with [[rituals]] and [[shamanistic practices]].Yamada 2019: 204
The largest known stone rod is the {{ill|Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|ja|北沢大石棒}} in {{ill|Sakuho, Nagano|ja|佐久穂町}}, measuring 223 cm in length and 25 cm in diameter.
== Overview == In a broad sense, "stone rods" encompass various rod-shaped stone artifacts, including [[stone swords]] and [[stone daggers]]. In a narrower sense, they often refer specifically to large stone rods.Tori 1924 These artifacts have been found throughout Japan, from [[Kyushu]] to [[Hokkaido]].
Phallic stone artifacts date back to the [[Upper Paleolithic]] period, such as the one found at the {{ill|Masukata Site|ja|升形遺跡}} in {{ill|Ōami-Shirasato, Chiba|ja|大羽白里市}}, dated to approximately 24,000 years ago.Harunari 1996 The specific type known as "large stone rods" emerged during the Middle Jōmon period in the [[Chūbu region]] and later spread eastward to areas such as the [[Kinki region]].Yamamoto 1979Okada 1960
Sites where stone rods were manufactured have been identified in eastern Japan, such as the {{ill|Shioya Kanesayama Shrine Site|ja|塩屋金清神社遺跡}} and the {{ill|Shima Site|ja|島遺跡}} in {{ill|Gifu Prefecture|ja|岐阜県}},Hayakawa & Hayashi 2000Miyoshi & Nagata 2012 as well as in western Japan, such as the {{ill|Mikuraoka Site|ja|見蔵岡遺跡}} in {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県}} and the {{ill|Kamizato Site|ja|上里遺跡}} in {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府}}.Matsui, Oshita & Tamura 1997Takahashi & Kondo 2011 These findings suggest that the use of stone rods as ritual objects was adopted as a cultural practice across different regions.Oshita 2001
Some stone rods have been found near the [[hearths]] of dwellings, often showing signs of heat damage or discoloration. This indicates their use in fire-related [[rituals]]. Ritual practices involving stone rods are thought to symbolically represent the phases of male genital arousal, sexual activity, and post-ejaculatory flaccidity through actions such as striking, heating, and breaking the rods. The prevalence of broken stone rods in archaeological sites has led some researchers to attribute symbolic meanings to this practice, though others remain skeptical.Gotō 1999
Stone rods were also used as burial goods, suggesting multiple [[shamanistic]] or ritualistic purposes.Yamada 2019: 206Taniguchi 2006
[[File:Hinotodake Santyô.JPG|thumb|Stone rod at the summit of {{ill|Mt. Chōkaku|ja|丁岳}} on the border of {{ill|Akita Prefecture|ja|秋田県}} and {{ill|Yamagata Prefecture|ja|山形県}}]]
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading ==
Tanaka, Taku & Sahara, Makoto (Eds.) 2002. ''Encyclopedia of Japanese Archaeology''. Sanseidō. pp. 487–488.
Tamura, Kōichi & Aida, Yoshimasa (Supervisors) 2004. ''Basic Terminology of Archaeological Exploration''. Yamakawa Publishing. p. 74.
Yamada, Yasuhiro. 2019. ''The History of the Jōmon Period''. Kodansha Modern Library.
Tori, Ryūzō. 1924. "Stone Rods and Stone Swords." ''Suwa History,'' Volume 1. Shinano Educational Association Suwa Branch.
Harunari, Hideō. 1996. "The Archaeology of Sexual Symbols." ''Bulletin of the National Museum of Japanese History,'' Issue 66.
Okada, Shigehiro. 1960. "Regional Development of Jōmon Culture in Kinki." ''The Jōmon Period'' (Japanese Archaeology II). Kawade Shobō Shinsha.
Gotō, Shinsuke. 1999. "Research on Artifacts: Stone Rods, Stone Swords, and Stone Daggers." ''The Jōmon Period,'' Issue 10, Vol. 4. The Jōmon Period Cultural Research Society.
Taniguchi, Yasuhiro. 2006. "Stone Rods and Stone Plates: Contexts of Symbolic Reproductive Acts." ''Archaeology IV.'' Masato Anzai.
Yamamoto, Teruhisa. 1979. "Evolution of Rituals with Stone Rods (Parts 1 & 2)." ''Ancient Culture,'' Vol. 31, Issues 11–12. Association for Ancient Studies.
Hayakawa, Shoichi & Hayashi, Naoki. 2000. ''Excavation Report of the Shioya Kanesayama Shrine Site (A Area).'' Gifu Prefecture & Miyagawa Village Board of Education.
Miyoshi, Seichō & Nagata, Tomoya. 2012. ''The Shima and Shioya Kanesayama Sites.'' Hida City Board of Education.
Matsui, Takayo; Oshita, Akira; Tamura, Seiichirō. 1997. ''Mikuraoka Site II.'' Takenochō Board of Education.
Takahashi, Kiyoshi & Kondo, Nao. 2010. ''The Kamizato Site I: A Jōmon Late-Period Settlement Excavation.'' Kyoto City Archaeological Research Center Report No. 24.
Oshita, Akira. 2001. "Adoption and Transformation of Large Stone Rods in the Kinki Region (Parts 1 & 2)." ''Stone Ritual Implements in the Transition from Jōmon to Yayoi Periods III.'' Spring Research Lab, National Museum of Japanese History.

In a narrower sense, they often refer specifically to large stone rods.Tori 1924 These artifacts have been found throughout Japan, from [[Kyushu]] to [[Hokkaido]].
Phallic stone artifacts date back to the [[Upper Paleolithic]] period, such as the one found at the {{ill|Masukata Site|ja|升形遺跡}} in {{ill|Ōami-Shirasato, Chiba|ja|大羽白里市}}, dated to approximately 24,000 years ago.Harunari 1996 The specific type known as "large stone rods" emerged during the Middle Jōmon period in the [[Chūbu region]] and later spread eastward to areas such as the [[Kinki region]].Yamamoto 1979Okada 1960
Sites where stone rods were manufactured have been identified in eastern Japan, such as the {{ill|Shioya Kanesayama Shrine Site|ja|塩屋金清神社遺跡}} and the {{ill|Shima Site|ja|島遺跡}} in {{ill|Gifu Prefecture|ja|岐阜県}},Hayakawa & Hayashi 2000Miyoshi & Nagata 2012 as well as in western Japan, such as the {{ill|Mikuraoka Site|ja|見蔵岡遺跡}} in {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県}} and the {{ill|Kamizato Site|ja|上里遺跡}} in {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府}}.Matsui, Oshita & Tamura 1997Takahashi & Kondo 2011 These findings suggest that the use of stone rods as ritual objects was adopted as a cultural practice across different regions.Oshita 2001
Some stone rods have been found near the [[hearths]] of dwellings, often showing signs of heat damage or discoloration. This indicates their use in fire-related [[rituals]]. Ritual practices involving stone rods are thought to symbolically represent the phases of male genital arousal, sexual activity, and post-ejaculatory flaccidity through actions such as striking, heating, and breaking the rods. The prevalence of broken stone rods in archaeological sites has led some researchers to attribute symbolic meanings to this practice, though others remain skeptical.Gotō 1999
Stone rods were also used as burial goods, suggesting multiple [[shamanistic]] or ritualistic purposes.Yamada 2019: 206Taniguchi 2006
[[File:Hinotodake Santyô.JPG|thumb|Stone rod at the summit of {{ill|Mt. Chōkaku|ja|丁岳}} on the border of {{ill|Akita Prefecture|ja|秋田県}} and {{ill|Yamagata Prefecture|ja|山形県}}]]
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading ==

Tamura, Kōichi & Aida, Yoshimasa (Supervisors) 2004. ''Basic Terminology of Archaeological Exploration''. Yamakawa Publishing. p. 74.

Gotō, Shinsuke. 1999. "Research on Artifacts: Stone Rods, Stone Swords, and Stone Daggers." ''The Jōmon Period,'' Issue 10, Vol. 4. The Jōmon Period Cultural Research Society.
Taniguchi, Yasuhiro. 2006. "Stone Rods and Stone Plates: Contexts of Symbolic Reproductive Acts." ''Archaeology IV.'' Masato Anzai.
Yamamoto, Teruhisa. 1979. "Evolution of Rituals with Stone Rods (Parts 1 & 2)." ''Ancient Culture,'' Vol. 31, Issues 11–12. Association for Ancient Studies.
Hayakawa, Shoichi & Hayashi, Naoki. 2000. ''Excavation Report of the Shioya Kanesayama Shrine Site (A Area).'' Gifu Prefecture & Miyagawa Village Board of Education.
Miyoshi, Seichō & Nagata, Tomoya. 2012. ''The Shima and Shioya Kanesayama Sites.'' Hida City Board of Education.
Matsui, Takayo; Oshita, Akira; Tamura, Seiichirō. 1997. ''Mikuraoka Site II.'' Takenochō Board of Education.
Takahashi, Kiyoshi & Kondo, Nao. 2010. ''The Kamizato Site I: A Jōmon Late-Period Settlement Excavation.'' Kyoto City Archaeological Research Center Report No. 24.
Oshita, Akira. 2001. "Adoption and Transformation of Large Stone Rods in the Kinki Region (Parts 1 & 2)." ''Stone Ritual Implements in the Transition from Jōmon to Yayoi Periods III.'' Spring Research Lab, National Museum of Japanese History.

{{Multiple issues | refimprove = March 2016 }} [[File:Sekibou.jpg|thumb|Stone rod ({{ill|Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties|ja|奈良県立橿原考古学研究所}} Museum)]] [[File:Kitazawagawa Daisekibo.jpg|thumb|The largest stone rod in Japan, the {{ill|Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|ja|北沢大石棒}}]] ''''Stone rods''' (せきぼう, sekibō) are a type of polished [[stone tool]] from the [[Jōmon period]] in Japan. They are believed to represent [[phalluses]] and are associated with [[rituals]] and [[shamanistic practices]].Yamada 2019: 204
The largest known stone rod is the {{ill|Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|ja|北沢大石棒}} in {{ill|Sakuho, Nagano|ja|佐久穂町}}, measuring 223 cm in length and 25 cm in diameter.
== Overview == In a broad sense, "stone rods" encompass various rod-shaped stone artifacts, including [[stone swords]] and [[stone daggers]]. In a narrower sense, they often refer specifically to large stone rods.Tori 1924 These artifacts have been found throughout Japan, from [[Kyushu]] to [[Hokkaido]].
Phallic stone artifacts date back to the [[Upper Paleolithic]] period, such as the one found at the {{ill|Masukata Site|ja|升形遺跡}} in {{ill|Ōami-Shirasato, Chiba|ja|大羽白里市}}, dated to approximately 24,000 years ago.Harunari 1996 The specific type known as "large stone rods" emerged during the Middle Jōmon period in the [[Chūbu region]] and later spread eastward to areas such as the [[Kinki region]].Yamamoto 1979Okada 1960
Sites where stone rods were manufactured have been identified in eastern Japan, such as the {{ill|Shioya Kanesayama Shrine Site|ja|塩屋金清神社遺跡}} and the {{ill|Shima Site|ja|島遺跡}} in {{ill|Gifu Prefecture|ja|岐阜県}},Hayakawa & Hayashi 2000Miyoshi & Nagata 2012 as well as in western Japan, such as the {{ill|Mikuraoka Site|ja|見蔵岡遺跡}} in {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県}} and the {{ill|Kamizato Site|ja|上里遺跡}} in {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府}}.Matsui, Oshita & Tamura 1997Takahashi & Kondo 2011 These findings suggest that the use of stone rods as ritual objects was adopted as a cultural practice across different regions.Oshita 2001
Some stone rods have been found near the [[hearths]] of dwellings, often showing signs of heat damage or discoloration. This indicates their use in fire-related [[rituals]]. Ritual practices involving stone rods are thought to symbolically represent the phases of male genital arousal, sexual activity, and post-ejaculatory flaccidity through actions such as striking, heating, and breaking the rods. The prevalence of broken stone rods in archaeological sites has led some researchers to attribute symbolic meanings to this practice, though others remain skeptical.Gotō 1999
Stone rods were also used as burial goods, suggesting multiple [[shamanistic]] or ritualistic purposes.Yamada 2019: 206Taniguchi 2006
[[File:Hinotodake Santyô.JPG|thumb|Stone rod at the summit of {{ill|Mt. Chōkaku|ja|丁岳}} on the border of {{ill|Akita Prefecture|ja|秋田県}} and {{ill|Yamagata Prefecture|ja|山形県}}]]

[[en:Ishibou]][[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:bump 1-26]] [[Category:refreshing redirects 1-27]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Ishibou]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Ishibou]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:Ishibou]]<!----> [[en:Ishibou]][[en:Ishibou]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]]
[[Category:11]] [[sn:Ishibou]][[Category:11]][[sn:Ishibou]][[Category:11]][[sn:Ishibou]][[Category:11]][[sn:Ishibou]][[Category:11]][[sn:Ishibou]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Ishibou]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Ishibou]][[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] [[sn:Ishibou]][[Category:11]][[sn:Ishibou]][[Category:11]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Ishibou]][[Category:qq]]

{{Multiple issues | refimprove = March 2016 }} [[File:Sekibou.jpg|thumb|Stone rod ({{ill|Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties|ja|奈良県立橿原考古学研究所}} Museum)]] [[File:Kitazawagawa Daisekibo.jpg|thumb|The largest stone rod in Japan, the {{ill|Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|ja|北沢大石棒}}]] ''''Stone rods''' (せきぼう, sekibō) are a type of polished [[stone tool]] from the [[Jōmon period]] in Japan. They are believed to represent [[phalluses]] and are associated with [[rituals]] and [[shamanistic practices]].Yamada 2019: 204
The largest known stone rod is the {{ill|Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|ja|北沢大石棒}} in {{ill|Sakuho, Nagano|ja|佐久穂町}}, measuring 223 cm in length and 25 cm in diameter.
== Overview == In a broad sense, "stone rods" encompass various rod-shaped stone artifacts, including [[stone swords]] and [[stone daggers]]. In a narrower sense, they often refer specifically to large stone rods.Tori 1924 These artifacts have been found throughout Japan, from [[Kyushu]] to [[Hokkaido]].
Phallic stone artifacts date back to the [[Upper Paleolithic]] period, such as the one found at the {{ill|Masukata Site|ja|升形遺跡}} in {{ill|Ōami-Shirasato, Chiba|ja|大羽白里市}}, dated to approximately 24,000 years ago.Harunari 1996 The specific type known as "large stone rods" emerged during the Middle Jōmon period in the [[Chūbu region]] and later spread eastward to areas such as the [[Kinki region]].Yamamoto 1979Okada 1960
Sites where stone rods were manufactured have been identified in eastern Japan, such as the {{ill|Shioya Kanesayama Shrine Site|ja|塩屋金清神社遺跡}} and the {{ill|Shima Site|ja|島遺跡}} in {{ill|Gifu Prefecture|ja|岐阜県}},Hayakawa & Hayashi 2000Miyoshi & Nagata 2012 as well as in western Japan, such as the {{ill|Mikuraoka Site|ja|見蔵岡遺跡}} in {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県}} and the {{ill|Kamizato Site|ja|上里遺跡}} in {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府}}.Matsui, Oshita & Tamura 1997Takahashi & Kondo 2011 These findings suggest that the use of stone rods as ritual objects was adopted as a cultural practice across different regions.Oshita 2001
Some stone rods have been found near the [[hearths]] of dwellings, often showing signs of heat damage or discoloration. This indicates their use in fire-related [[rituals]]. Ritual practices involving stone rods are thought to symbolically represent the phases of male genital arousal, sexual activity, and post-ejaculatory flaccidity through actions such as striking, heating, and breaking the rods. The prevalence of broken stone rods in archaeological sites has led some researchers to attribute symbolic meanings to this practice, though others remain skeptical.Gotō 1999
Stone rods were also used as burial goods, suggesting multiple [[shamanistic]] or ritualistic purposes.Yamada 2019: 206Taniguchi 2006
[[File:Hinotodake Santyô.JPG|thumb|Stone rod at the summit of {{ill|Mt. Chōkaku|ja|丁岳}} on the border of {{ill|Akita Prefecture|ja|秋田県}} and {{ill|Yamagata Prefecture|ja|山形県}}]]

revision.comment: Text replacement - "↵↵== See Also ==" to "== See Also =="
revision.text:
{{Multiple issues | refimprove = March 2016 }} [[File:Sekibou.jpg|thumb|Stone rod ({{ill|Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties|ja|奈良県立橿原考古学研究所}} Museum)]] [[File:Kitazawagawa Daisekibo.jpg|thumb|The largest stone rod in Japan, the {{ill|Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|ja|北沢大石棒}}]] ''''Stone rods''' (せきぼう, sekibō) are a type of polished [[stone tool]] from the [[Jōmon period]] in Japan. They are believed to represent [[phalluses]] and are associated with [[rituals]] and [[shamanistic practices]].Yamada 2019: 204
The largest known stone rod is the {{ill|Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|ja|北沢大石棒}} in {{ill|Sakuho, Nagano|ja|佐久穂町}}, measuring 223 cm in length and 25 cm in diameter.
== Overview == In a broad sense, "stone rods" encompass various rod-shaped stone artifacts, including [[stone swords]] and [[stone daggers]]. In a narrower sense, they often refer specifically to large stone rods.Tori 1924 These artifacts have been found throughout Japan, from [[Kyushu]] to [[Hokkaido]].
Phallic stone artifacts date back to the [[Upper Paleolithic]] period, such as the one found at the {{ill|Masukata Site|ja|升形遺跡}} in {{ill|Ōami-Shirasato, Chiba|ja|大羽白里市}}, dated to approximately 24,000 years ago.Harunari 1996 The specific type known as "large stone rods" emerged during the Middle Jōmon period in the [[Chūbu region]] and later spread eastward to areas such as the [[Kinki region]].Yamamoto 1979Okada 1960
Sites where stone rods were manufactured have been identified in eastern Japan, such as the {{ill|Shioya Kanesayama Shrine Site|ja|塩屋金清神社遺跡}} and the {{ill|Shima Site|ja|島遺跡}} in {{ill|Gifu Prefecture|ja|岐阜県}},Hayakawa & Hayashi 2000Miyoshi & Nagata 2012 as well as in western Japan, such as the {{ill|Mikuraoka Site|ja|見蔵岡遺跡}} in {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県}} and the {{ill|Kamizato Site|ja|上里遺跡}} in {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府}}.Matsui, Oshita & Tamura 1997Takahashi & Kondo 2011 These findings suggest that the use of stone rods as ritual objects was adopted as a cultural practice across different regions.Oshita 2001
Some stone rods have been found near the [[hearths]] of dwellings, often showing signs of heat damage or discoloration. This indicates their use in fire-related [[rituals]]. Ritual practices involving stone rods are thought to symbolically represent the phases of male genital arousal, sexual activity, and post-ejaculatory flaccidity through actions such as striking, heating, and breaking the rods. The prevalence of broken stone rods in archaeological sites has led some researchers to attribute symbolic meanings to this practice, though others remain skeptical.Gotō 1999
Stone rods were also used as burial goods, suggesting multiple [[shamanistic]] or ritualistic purposes.Yamada 2019: 206Taniguchi 2006
[[File:Hinotodake Santyô.JPG|thumb|Stone rod at the summit of {{ill|Mt. Chōkaku|ja|丁岳}} on the border of {{ill|Akita Prefecture|ja|秋田県}} and {{ill|Yamagata Prefecture|ja|山形県}}]]
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading ==
Tanaka, Taku & Sahara, Makoto (Eds.) 2002. ''Encyclopedia of Japanese Archaeology''. Sanseidō. pp. 487–488.
Tamura, Kōichi & Aida, Yoshimasa (Supervisors) 2004. ''Basic Terminology of Archaeological Exploration''. Yamakawa Publishing. p. 74.
Yamada, Yasuhiro. 2019. ''The History of the Jōmon Period''. Kodansha Modern Library.
Tori, Ryūzō. 1924. "Stone Rods and Stone Swords." ''Suwa History,'' Volume 1. Shinano Educational Association Suwa Branch.
Harunari, Hideō. 1996. "The Archaeology of Sexual Symbols." ''Bulletin of the National Museum of Japanese History,'' Issue 66.
Okada, Shigehiro. 1960. "Regional Development of Jōmon Culture in Kinki." ''The Jōmon Period'' (Japanese Archaeology II). Kawade Shobō Shinsha.
Gotō, Shinsuke. 1999. "Research on Artifacts: Stone Rods, Stone Swords, and Stone Daggers." ''The Jōmon Period,'' Issue 10, Vol. 4. The Jōmon Period Cultural Research Society.
Taniguchi, Yasuhiro. 2006. "Stone Rods and Stone Plates: Contexts of Symbolic Reproductive Acts." ''Archaeology IV.'' Masato Anzai.
Yamamoto, Teruhisa. 1979. "Evolution of Rituals with Stone Rods (Parts 1 & 2)." ''Ancient Culture,'' Vol. 31, Issues 11–12. Association for Ancient Studies.
Hayakawa, Shoichi & Hayashi, Naoki. 2000. ''Excavation Report of the Shioya Kanesayama Shrine Site (A Area).'' Gifu Prefecture & Miyagawa Village Board of Education.
Miyoshi, Seichō & Nagata, Tomoya. 2012. ''The Shima and Shioya Kanesayama Sites.'' Hida City Board of Education.
Matsui, Takayo; Oshita, Akira; Tamura, Seiichirō. 1997. ''Mikuraoka Site II.'' Takenochō Board of Education.
Takahashi, Kiyoshi & Kondo, Nao. 2010. ''The Kamizato Site I: A Jōmon Late-Period Settlement Excavation.'' Kyoto City Archaeological Research Center Report No. 24.
Oshita, Akira. 2001. "Adoption and Transformation of Large Stone Rods in the Kinki Region (Parts 1 & 2)." ''Stone Ritual Implements in the Transition from Jōmon to Yayoi Periods III.'' Spring Research Lab, National Museum of Japanese History.== See Also ==

revision.text:
{{Multiple issues | refimprove = March 2016 }} [[File:Sekibou.jpg|thumb|Stone rod ({{ill|Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties|ja|奈良県立橿原考古学研究所}} Museum)]] [[File:Kitazawagawa Daisekibo.jpg|thumb|The largest stone rod in Japan, the {{ill|Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|ja|北沢大石棒}}]] ''''Stone rods''' (せきぼう, sekibō) are a type of polished [[stone tool]] from the [[Jōmon period]] in Japan. They are believed to represent [[phalluses]] and are associated with [[rituals]] and [[shamanistic practices]].Yamada 2019: 204
The largest known stone rod is the {{ill|Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|ja|北沢大石棒}} in {{ill|Sakuho, Nagano|ja|佐久穂町}}, measuring 223 cm in length and 25 cm in diameter.
== Overview == In a broad sense, "stone rods" encompass various rod-shaped stone artifacts, including [[stone swords]] and [[stone daggers]]. In a narrower sense, they often refer specifically to large stone rods.Tori 1924 These artifacts have been found throughout Japan, from [[Kyushu]] to [[Hokkaido]].
Phallic stone artifacts date back to the [[Upper Paleolithic]] period, such as the one found at the {{ill|Masukata Site|ja|升形遺跡}} in {{ill|Ōami-Shirasato, Chiba|ja|大羽白里市}}, dated to approximately 24,000 years ago.Harunari 1996 The specific type known as "large stone rods" emerged during the Middle Jōmon period in the [[Chūbu region]] and later spread eastward to areas such as the [[Kinki region]].Yamamoto 1979Okada 1960
Sites where stone rods were manufactured have been identified in eastern Japan, such as the {{ill|Shioya Kanesayama Shrine Site|ja|塩屋金清神社遺跡}} and the {{ill|Shima Site|ja|島遺跡}} in {{ill|Gifu Prefecture|ja|岐阜県}},Hayakawa & Hayashi 2000Miyoshi & Nagata 2012 as well as in western Japan, such as the {{ill|Mikuraoka Site|ja|見蔵岡遺跡}} in {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県}} and the {{ill|Kamizato Site|ja|上里遺跡}} in {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府}}.Matsui, Oshita & Tamura 1997Takahashi & Kondo 2011 These findings suggest that the use of stone rods as ritual objects was adopted as a cultural practice across different regions.Oshita 2001
Some stone rods have been found near the [[hearths]] of dwellings, often showing signs of heat damage or discoloration. This indicates their use in fire-related [[rituals]]. Ritual practices involving stone rods are thought to symbolically represent the phases of male genital arousal, sexual activity, and post-ejaculatory flaccidity through actions such as striking, heating, and breaking the rods. The prevalence of broken stone rods in archaeological sites has led some researchers to attribute symbolic meanings to this practice, though others remain skeptical.Gotō 1999
Stone rods were also used as burial goods, suggesting multiple [[shamanistic]] or ritualistic purposes.Yamada 2019: 206Taniguchi 2006
[[File:Hinotodake Santyô.JPG|thumb|Stone rod at the summit of {{ill|Mt. Chōkaku|ja|丁岳}} on the border of {{ill|Akita Prefecture|ja|秋田県}} and {{ill|Yamagata Prefecture|ja|山形県}}]]
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading ==
Tanaka, Taku & Sahara, Makoto (Eds.) 2002. ''Encyclopedia of Japanese Archaeology''. Sanseidō. pp. 487–488.
Tamura, Kōichi & Aida, Yoshimasa (Supervisors) 2004. ''Basic Terminology of Archaeological Exploration''. Yamakawa Publishing. p. 74.
Yamada, Yasuhiro. 2019. ''The History of the Jōmon Period''. Kodansha Modern Library.
Tori, Ryūzō. 1924. "Stone Rods and Stone Swords." ''Suwa History,'' Volume 1. Shinano Educational Association Suwa Branch.
Harunari, Hideō. 1996. "The Archaeology of Sexual Symbols." ''Bulletin of the National Museum of Japanese History,'' Issue 66.
Okada, Shigehiro. 1960. "Regional Development of Jōmon Culture in Kinki." ''The Jōmon Period'' (Japanese Archaeology II). Kawade Shobō Shinsha.
Gotō, Shinsuke. 1999. "Research on Artifacts: Stone Rods, Stone Swords, and Stone Daggers." ''The Jōmon Period,'' Issue 10, Vol. 4. The Jōmon Period Cultural Research Society.
Taniguchi, Yasuhiro. 2006. "Stone Rods and Stone Plates: Contexts of Symbolic Reproductive Acts." ''Archaeology IV.'' Masato Anzai.
Yamamoto, Teruhisa. 1979. "Evolution of Rituals with Stone Rods (Parts 1 & 2)." ''Ancient Culture,'' Vol. 31, Issues 11–12. Association for Ancient Studies.
Hayakawa, Shoichi & Hayashi, Naoki. 2000. ''Excavation Report of the Shioya Kanesayama Shrine Site (A Area).'' Gifu Prefecture & Miyagawa Village Board of Education.
Miyoshi, Seichō & Nagata, Tomoya. 2012. ''The Shima and Shioya Kanesayama Sites.'' Hida City Board of Education.
Matsui, Takayo; Oshita, Akira; Tamura, Seiichirō. 1997. ''Mikuraoka Site II.'' Takenochō Board of Education.
Takahashi, Kiyoshi & Kondo, Nao. 2010. ''The Kamizato Site I: A Jōmon Late-Period Settlement Excavation.'' Kyoto City Archaeological Research Center Report No. 24.
Oshita, Akira. 2001. "Adoption and Transformation of Large Stone Rods in the Kinki Region (Parts 1 & 2)." ''Stone Ritual Implements in the Transition from Jōmon to Yayoi Periods III.'' Spring Research Lab, National Museum of Japanese History. == See Also ==
[[Stone sword]]
[[Stone tools]]
[[Kin]]
[[Kōjin]]
[[Vajra]]
[[Mara Stone|Phallic stone of Asuka]]
[[Mishaguji]] — An ancient belief system originating in the Jōmon period, centered around stone rods and trees as sacred objects, prevalent in {{ill|Suwa|ja|諏訪市}} and surrounding regions.
[[Snake deity]]
== External Links ==

revision.comment: Adding [[Category:qq]][[sn:{{subst:PAGENAME}}]][[Category:pages which might possibly be incomplete but are most likely complete]][[Category:I think I cleared all the incomplete or blank pages earlier]][[Category:qq]] on (CatAlot)
revision.text:
{{Multiple issues | refimprove = March 2016 }} [[File:Sekibou.jpg|thumb|Stone rod ({{ill|Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties|ja|奈良県立橿原考古学研究所}} Museum)]] [[File:Kitazawagawa Daisekibo.jpg|thumb|The largest stone rod in Japan, the {{ill|Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|ja|北沢大石棒}}]] ''''Stone rods''' (せきぼう, sekibō) are a type of polished [[stone tool]] from the [[Jōmon period]] in Japan. They are believed to represent [[phalluses]] and are associated with [[rituals]] and [[shamanistic practices]].Yamada 2019: 204
The largest known stone rod is the {{ill|Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|ja|北沢大石棒}} in {{ill|Sakuho, Nagano|ja|佐久穂町}}, measuring 223 cm in length and 25 cm in diameter.
== Overview == In a broad sense, "stone rods" encompass various rod-shaped stone artifacts, including [[stone swords]] and [[stone daggers]]. In a narrower sense, they often refer specifically to large stone rods.Tori 1924 These artifacts have been found throughout Japan, from [[Kyushu]] to [[Hokkaido]].
Phallic stone artifacts date back to the [[Upper Paleolithic]] period, such as the one found at the {{ill|Masukata Site|ja|升形遺跡}} in {{ill|Ōami-Shirasato, Chiba|ja|大羽白里市}}, dated to approximately 24,000 years ago.Harunari 1996 The specific type known as "large stone rods" emerged during the Middle Jōmon period in the [[Chūbu region]] and later spread eastward to areas such as the [[Kinki region]].Yamamoto 1979Okada 1960
Sites where stone rods were manufactured have been identified in eastern Japan, such as the {{ill|Shioya Kanesayama Shrine Site|ja|塩屋金清神社遺跡}} and the {{ill|Shima Site|ja|島遺跡}} in {{ill|Gifu Prefecture|ja|岐阜県}},Hayakawa & Hayashi 2000Miyoshi & Nagata 2012 as well as in western Japan, such as the {{ill|Mikuraoka Site|ja|見蔵岡遺跡}} in {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県}} and the {{ill|Kamizato Site|ja|上里遺跡}} in {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府}}.Matsui, Oshita & Tamura 1997Takahashi & Kondo 2011 These findings suggest that the use of stone rods as ritual objects was adopted as a cultural practice across different regions.Oshita 2001
Some stone rods have been found near the [[hearths]] of dwellings, often showing signs of heat damage or discoloration. This indicates their use in fire-related [[rituals]]. Ritual practices involving stone rods are thought to symbolically represent the phases of male genital arousal, sexual activity, and post-ejaculatory flaccidity through actions such as striking, heating, and breaking the rods. The prevalence of broken stone rods in archaeological sites has led some researchers to attribute symbolic meanings to this practice, though others remain skeptical.Gotō 1999
Stone rods were also used as burial goods, suggesting multiple [[shamanistic]] or ritualistic purposes.Yamada 2019: 206Taniguchi 2006
[[File:Hinotodake Santyô.JPG|thumb|Stone rod at the summit of {{ill|Mt. Chōkaku|ja|丁岳}} on the border of {{ill|Akita Prefecture|ja|秋田県}} and {{ill|Yamagata Prefecture|ja|山形県}}]]
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading ==
Tanaka, Taku & Sahara, Makoto (Eds.) 2002. ''Encyclopedia of Japanese Archaeology''. Sanseidō. pp. 487–488.
Tamura, Kōichi & Aida, Yoshimasa (Supervisors) 2004. ''Basic Terminology of Archaeological Exploration''. Yamakawa Publishing. p. 74.
Yamada, Yasuhiro. 2019. ''The History of the Jōmon Period''. Kodansha Modern Library.
Tori, Ryūzō. 1924. "Stone Rods and Stone Swords." ''Suwa History,'' Volume 1. Shinano Educational Association Suwa Branch.
Harunari, Hideō. 1996. "The Archaeology of Sexual Symbols." ''Bulletin of the National Museum of Japanese History,'' Issue 66.
Okada, Shigehiro. 1960. "Regional Development of Jōmon Culture in Kinki." ''The Jōmon Period'' (Japanese Archaeology II). Kawade Shobō Shinsha.
Gotō, Shinsuke. 1999. "Research on Artifacts: Stone Rods, Stone Swords, and Stone Daggers." ''The Jōmon Period,'' Issue 10, Vol. 4. The Jōmon Period Cultural Research Society.
Taniguchi, Yasuhiro. 2006. "Stone Rods and Stone Plates: Contexts of Symbolic Reproductive Acts." ''Archaeology IV.'' Masato Anzai.
Yamamoto, Teruhisa. 1979. "Evolution of Rituals with Stone Rods (Parts 1 & 2)." ''Ancient Culture,'' Vol. 31, Issues 11–12. Association for Ancient Studies.
Hayakawa, Shoichi & Hayashi, Naoki. 2000. ''Excavation Report of the Shioya Kanesayama Shrine Site (A Area).'' Gifu Prefecture & Miyagawa Village Board of Education.
Miyoshi, Seichō & Nagata, Tomoya. 2012. ''The Shima and Shioya Kanesayama Sites.'' Hida City Board of Education.
Matsui, Takayo; Oshita, Akira; Tamura, Seiichirō. 1997. ''Mikuraoka Site II.'' Takenochō Board of Education.
Takahashi, Kiyoshi & Kondo, Nao. 2010. ''The Kamizato Site I: A Jōmon Late-Period Settlement Excavation.'' Kyoto City Archaeological Research Center Report No. 24.
Oshita, Akira. 2001. "Adoption and Transformation of Large Stone Rods in the Kinki Region (Parts 1 & 2)." ''Stone Ritual Implements in the Transition from Jōmon to Yayoi Periods III.'' Spring Research Lab, National Museum of Japanese History. == See Also ==
[[Stone sword]]
[[Stone tools]]
[[Kin]]
[[Kōjin]]
[[Vajra]]
[[Mara Stone|Phallic stone of Asuka]]
[[Mishaguji]] — An ancient belief system originating in the Jōmon period, centered around stone rods and trees as sacred objects, prevalent in {{ill|Suwa|ja|諏訪市}} and surrounding regions.
[[Snake deity]]
== External Links ==

{{Multiple issues | refimprove = March 2016 }} [[File:Sekibou.jpg|thumb|Stone rod ({{ill|Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties|ja|奈良県立橿原考古学研究所}} Museum)]] [[File:Kitazawagawa Daisekibo.jpg|thumb|The largest stone rod in Japan, the {{ill|Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|ja|北沢大石棒}}]] ''''Stone rods''' (せきぼう, sekibō) are a type of polished [[stone tool]] from the [[Jōmon period]] in Japan. They are believed to represent [[phalluses]] and are associated with [[rituals]] and [[shamanistic practices]].Yamada 2019: 204
The largest known stone rod is the {{ill|Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|ja|北沢大石棒}} in {{ill|Sakuho, Nagano|ja|佐久穂町}}, measuring 223 cm in length and 25 cm in diameter.
== Overview == In a broad sense, "stone rods" encompass various rod-shaped stone artifacts, including [[stone swords]] and [[stone daggers]]. In a narrower sense, they often refer specifically to large stone rods.Tori 1924 These artifacts have been found throughout Japan, from [[Kyushu]] to [[Hokkaido]].
Phallic stone artifacts date back to the [[Upper Paleolithic]] period, such as the one found at the {{ill|Masukata Site|ja|升形遺跡}} in {{ill|Ōami-Shirasato, Chiba|ja|大羽白里市}}, dated to approximately 24,000 years ago.Harunari 1996 The specific type known as "large stone rods" emerged during the Middle Jōmon period in the [[Chūbu region]] and later spread eastward to areas such as the [[Kinki region]].Yamamoto 1979Okada 1960
Sites where stone rods were manufactured have been identified in eastern Japan, such as the {{ill|Shioya Kanesayama Shrine Site|ja|塩屋金清神社遺跡}} and the {{ill|Shima Site|ja|島遺跡}} in {{ill|Gifu Prefecture|ja|岐阜県}},Hayakawa & Hayashi 2000Miyoshi & Nagata 2012 as well as in western Japan, such as the {{ill|Mikuraoka Site|ja|見蔵岡遺跡}} in {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県}} and the {{ill|Kamizato Site|ja|上里遺跡}} in {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府}}.Matsui, Oshita & Tamura 1997Takahashi & Kondo 2011 These findings suggest that the use of stone rods as ritual objects was adopted as a cultural practice across different regions.Oshita 2001
Some stone rods have been found near the [[hearths]] of dwellings, often showing signs of heat damage or discoloration. This indicates their use in fire-related [[rituals]]. Ritual practices involving stone rods are thought to symbolically represent the phases of male genital arousal, sexual activity, and post-ejaculatory flaccidity through actions such as striking, heating, and breaking the rods. The prevalence of broken stone rods in archaeological sites has led some researchers to attribute symbolic meanings to this practice, though others remain skeptical.Gotō 1999
Stone rods were also used as burial goods, suggesting multiple [[shamanistic]] or ritualistic purposes.Yamada 2019: 206Taniguchi 2006
[[File:Hinotodake Santyô.JPG|thumb|Stone rod at the summit of {{ill|Mt. Chōkaku|ja|丁岳}} on the border of {{ill|Akita Prefecture|ja|秋田県}} and {{ill|Yamagata Prefecture|ja|山形県}}]]
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading ==
Tanaka, Taku & Sahara, Makoto (Eds.) 2002. ''Encyclopedia of Japanese Archaeology''. Sanseidō. pp. 487–488.
Tamura, Kōichi & Aida, Yoshimasa (Supervisors) 2004. ''Basic Terminology of Archaeological Exploration''. Yamakawa Publishing. p. 74.
Yamada, Yasuhiro. 2019. ''The History of the Jōmon Period''. Kodansha Modern Library.
Tori, Ryūzō. 1924. "Stone Rods and Stone Swords." ''Suwa History,'' Volume 1. Shinano Educational Association Suwa Branch.
Harunari, Hideō. 1996. "The Archaeology of Sexual Symbols." ''Bulletin of the National Museum of Japanese History,'' Issue 66.
Okada, Shigehiro. 1960. "Regional Development of Jōmon Culture in Kinki." ''The Jōmon Period'' (Japanese Archaeology II). Kawade Shobō Shinsha.
Gotō, Shinsuke. 1999. "Research on Artifacts: Stone Rods, Stone Swords, and Stone Daggers." ''The Jōmon Period,'' Issue 10, Vol. 4. The Jōmon Period Cultural Research Society.
Taniguchi, Yasuhiro. 2006. "Stone Rods and Stone Plates: Contexts of Symbolic Reproductive Acts." ''Archaeology IV.'' Masato Anzai.
Yamamoto, Teruhisa. 1979. "Evolution of Rituals with Stone Rods (Parts 1 & 2)." ''Ancient Culture,'' Vol. 31, Issues 11–12. Association for Ancient Studies.
Hayakawa, Shoichi & Hayashi, Naoki. 2000. ''Excavation Report of the Shioya Kanesayama Shrine Site (A Area).'' Gifu Prefecture & Miyagawa Village Board of Education.
Miyoshi, Seichō & Nagata, Tomoya. 2012. ''The Shima and Shioya Kanesayama Sites.'' Hida City Board of Education.
Matsui, Takayo; Oshita, Akira; Tamura, Seiichirō. 1997. ''Mikuraoka Site II.'' Takenochō Board of Education.
Takahashi, Kiyoshi & Kondo, Nao. 2010. ''The Kamizato Site I: A Jōmon Late-Period Settlement Excavation.'' Kyoto City Archaeological Research Center Report No. 24.
Oshita, Akira. 2001. "Adoption and Transformation of Large Stone Rods in the Kinki Region (Parts 1 & 2)." ''Stone Ritual Implements in the Transition from Jōmon to Yayoi Periods III.'' Spring Research Lab, National Museum of Japanese History. == See Also ==

Sites where stone rods were manufactured have been identified in eastern Japan, such as the {{ill|Shioya Kanesayama Shrine Site|ja|塩屋金清神社遺跡|12=draft}} and the {{ill|Shima Site|ja|島遺跡|12=draft}} in {{ill|Gifu Prefecture|ja|岐阜県|12=draft}},Hayakawa & Hayashi 2000Miyoshi & Nagata 2012 as well as in western Japan, such as the {{ill|Mikuraoka Site|ja|見蔵岡遺跡|12=draft}} in {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県|12=draft}} and the {{ill|Kamizato Site|ja|上里遺跡|12=draft}} in {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府|12=draft}}.Matsui, Oshita & Tamura 1997Takahashi & Kondo 2011 These findings suggest that the use of stone rods as ritual objects was adopted as a cultural practice across different regions.Oshita 2001
Some stone rods have been found near the [[hearths]] of dwellings, often showing signs of heat damage or discoloration. This indicates their use in fire-related [[rituals]]. Ritual practices involving stone rods are thought to symbolically represent the phases of male genital arousal, sexual activity, and post-ejaculatory flaccidity through actions such as striking, heating, and breaking the rods. The prevalence of broken stone rods in archaeological sites has led some researchers to attribute symbolic meanings to this practice, though others remain skeptical.Gotō 1999
Stone rods were also used as burial goods, suggesting multiple [[shamanistic]] or ritualistic purposes.Yamada 2019: 206Taniguchi 2006
[[File:Hinotodake Santyô.JPG|thumb|Stone rod at the summit of {{ill|Mt. Chōkaku|ja|丁岳|12=draft}} on the border of {{ill|Akita Prefecture|ja|秋田県|12=draft}} and {{ill|Yamagata Prefecture|ja|山形県|12=draft}}]]

Ritual practices involving stone rods are thought to symbolically represent the phases of male genital arousal, sexual activity, and post-ejaculatory flaccidity through actions such as striking, heating, and breaking the rods. The prevalence of broken stone rods in archaeological sites has led some researchers to attribute symbolic meanings to this practice, though others remain skeptical.Gotō 1999
