revision.comment: Removing from [[Category:Pages which might possibly be incomplete but are most likely complete]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{Multiple issues | refimprove = March 2016 }} [[File:Sekibou.jpg|thumb|Stone rod ({{ill|Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties|ja|奈良県立橿原考古学研究所|12=draft}} Museum)]] [[File:Kitazawagawa Daisekibo.jpg|thumb|The largest stone rod in Japan, the {{ill|Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|ja|北沢大石棒|12=draft}}]] ''''Stone rods''' (せきぼう, sekibō) are a type of polished [[stone tool]] from the [[Jōmon period]] in Japan. They are believed to represent [[phalluses]] and are associated with [[rituals]] and [[shamanistic practices]].Yamada 2019: 204
The largest known stone rod is the {{ill|Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|ja|北沢大石棒|12=draft}} in {{ill|Sakuho, Nagano|ja|佐久穂町|12=draft}}, measuring 223 cm in length and 25 cm in diameter.
== Overview == In a broad sense, "stone rods" encompass various rod-shaped stone artifacts, including [[stone swords]] and [[stone daggers]]. In a narrower sense, they often refer specifically to large stone rods.Tori 1924 These artifacts have been found throughout Japan, from [[Kyushu]] to [[Hokkaido]].
Phallic stone artifacts date back to the [[Upper Paleolithic]] period, such as the one found at the {{ill|Masukata Site|ja|升形遺跡|12=draft}} in {{ill|Ōami-Shirasato, Chiba|ja|大羽白里市|12=draft}}, dated to approximately 24,000 years ago.Harunari 1996 The specific type known as "large stone rods" emerged during the Middle Jōmon period in the [[Chūbu region]] and later spread eastward to areas such as the [[Kinki region]].Yamamoto 1979Okada 1960
Sites where stone rods were manufactured have been identified in eastern Japan, such as the {{ill|Shioya Kanesayama Shrine Site|ja|塩屋金清神社遺跡|12=draft}} and the {{ill|Shima Site|ja|島遺跡|12=draft}} in {{ill|Gifu Prefecture|ja|岐阜県|12=draft}},Hayakawa & Hayashi 2000Miyoshi & Nagata 2012 as well as in western Japan, such as the {{ill|Mikuraoka Site|ja|見蔵岡遺跡|12=draft}} in {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県|12=draft}} and the {{ill|Kamizato Site|ja|上里遺跡|12=draft}} in {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府|12=draft}}.Matsui, Oshita & Tamura 1997Takahashi & Kondo 2011 These findings suggest that the use of stone rods as ritual objects was adopted as a cultural practice across different regions.Oshita 2001
Some stone rods have been found near the [[hearths]] of dwellings, often showing signs of heat damage or discoloration. This indicates their use in fire-related [[rituals]]. Ritual practices involving stone rods are thought to symbolically represent the phases of male genital arousal, sexual activity, and post-ejaculatory flaccidity through actions such as striking, heating, and breaking the rods. The prevalence of broken stone rods in archaeological sites has led some researchers to attribute symbolic meanings to this practice, though others remain skeptical.Gotō 1999
Stone rods were also used as burial goods, suggesting multiple [[shamanistic]] or ritualistic purposes.Yamada 2019: 206Taniguchi 2006
[[File:Hinotodake Santyô.JPG|thumb|Stone rod at the summit of {{ill|Mt. Chōkaku|ja|丁岳|12=draft}} on the border of {{ill|Akita Prefecture|ja|秋田県|12=draft}} and {{ill|Yamagata Prefecture|ja|山形県|12=draft}}]]
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading ==
Tanaka, Taku & Sahara, Makoto (Eds.) 2002. ''Encyclopedia of Japanese Archaeology''. Sanseidō. pp. 487–488.
Tamura, Kōichi & Aida, Yoshimasa (Supervisors) 2004. ''Basic Terminology of Archaeological Exploration''. Yamakawa Publishing. p. 74.
Yamada, Yasuhiro. 2019. ''The History of the Jōmon Period''. Kodansha Modern Library.
Tori, Ryūzō. 1924. "Stone Rods and Stone Swords." ''Suwa History,'' Volume 1. Shinano Educational Association Suwa Branch.
Harunari, Hideō. 1996. "The Archaeology of Sexual Symbols." ''Bulletin of the National Museum of Japanese History,'' Issue 66.
Okada, Shigehiro. 1960. "Regional Development of Jōmon Culture in Kinki." ''The Jōmon Period'' (Japanese Archaeology II). Kawade Shobō Shinsha.
Gotō, Shinsuke. 1999. "Research on Artifacts: Stone Rods, Stone Swords, and Stone Daggers." ''The Jōmon Period,'' Issue 10, Vol. 4. The Jōmon Period Cultural Research Society.
Taniguchi, Yasuhiro. 2006. "Stone Rods and Stone Plates: Contexts of Symbolic Reproductive Acts." ''Archaeology IV.'' Masato Anzai.
Yamamoto, Teruhisa. 1979. "Evolution of Rituals with Stone Rods (Parts 1 & 2)." ''Ancient Culture,'' Vol. 31, Issues 11–12. Association for Ancient Studies.
Hayakawa, Shoichi & Hayashi, Naoki. 2000. ''Excavation Report of the Shioya Kanesayama Shrine Site (A Area).'' Gifu Prefecture & Miyagawa Village Board of Education.
Miyoshi, Seichō & Nagata, Tomoya. 2012. ''The Shima and Shioya Kanesayama Sites.'' Hida City Board of Education.
Matsui, Takayo; Oshita, Akira; Tamura, Seiichirō. 1997. ''Mikuraoka Site II.'' Takenochō Board of Education.
Takahashi, Kiyoshi & Kondo, Nao. 2010. ''The Kamizato Site I: A Jōmon Late-Period Settlement Excavation.'' Kyoto City Archaeological Research Center Report No. 24.
Oshita, Akira. 2001. "Adoption and Transformation of Large Stone Rods in the Kinki Region (Parts 1 & 2)." ''Stone Ritual Implements in the Transition from Jōmon to Yayoi Periods III.'' Spring Research Lab, National Museum of Japanese History. == See Also ==
[[Stone sword]]
[[Stone tools]]
[[Kin]]
[[Kōjin]]
[[Vajra]]
[[Mara Stone|Phallic stone of Asuka]]
[[Mishaguji]] — An ancient belief system originating in the Jōmon period, centered around stone rods and trees as sacred objects, prevalent in {{ill|Suwa|ja|諏訪市|12=draft}} and surrounding regions.
[[Snake deity]]
== External Links ==
[http://www.tamagawa.ac.jp/museum/archive/2001/119_1.html Stone rods at Tamagawa University Education Museum]
[http://www.city.kunitachi.tokyo.jp/dbps_data/material/localhost/100000/101000/pdf/2012.07.10midorikawahigasiiseki.pdf Stone rods discovered at the Midorikawa East Site (PDF) – Kunitachi City]

Matsui, Takayo; Oshita, Akira; Tamura, Seiichirō. 1997. ''Mikuraoka Site II.'' Takenochō Board of Education.
Takahashi, Kiyoshi & Kondo, Nao. 2010. ''The Kamizato Site I: A Jōmon Late-Period Settlement Excavation.'' Kyoto City Archaeological Research Center Report No. 24.
Oshita, Akira. 2001. "Adoption and Transformation of Large Stone Rods in the Kinki Region (Parts 1 & 2)." ''Stone Ritual Implements in the Transition from Jōmon to Yayoi Periods III.'' Spring Research Lab, National Museum of Japanese History. == See Also ==

revision.comment: Removing from [[Category:Return the see also]] using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{Multiple issues | refimprove = March 2016 }} [[File:Sekibou.jpg|thumb|Stone rod ({{ill|Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties|ja|奈良県立橿原考古学研究所|12=draft}} Museum)]] [[File:Kitazawagawa Daisekibo.jpg|thumb|The largest stone rod in Japan, the {{ill|Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|ja|北沢大石棒|12=draft}}]] ''''Stone rods''' (せきぼう, sekibō) are a type of polished [[stone tool]] from the [[Jōmon period]] in Japan. They are believed to represent [[phalluses]] and are associated with [[rituals]] and [[shamanistic practices]].Yamada 2019: 204
The largest known stone rod is the {{ill|Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|ja|北沢大石棒|12=draft}} in {{ill|Sakuho, Nagano|ja|佐久穂町|12=draft}}, measuring 223 cm in length and 25 cm in diameter.
== Overview == In a broad sense, "stone rods" encompass various rod-shaped stone artifacts, including [[stone swords]] and [[stone daggers]]. In a narrower sense, they often refer specifically to large stone rods.Tori 1924 These artifacts have been found throughout Japan, from [[Kyushu]] to [[Hokkaido]].
Phallic stone artifacts date back to the [[Upper Paleolithic]] period, such as the one found at the {{ill|Masukata Site|ja|升形遺跡|12=draft}} in {{ill|Ōami-Shirasato, Chiba|ja|大羽白里市|12=draft}}, dated to approximately 24,000 years ago.Harunari 1996 The specific type known as "large stone rods" emerged during the Middle Jōmon period in the [[Chūbu region]] and later spread eastward to areas such as the [[Kinki region]].Yamamoto 1979Okada 1960
Sites where stone rods were manufactured have been identified in eastern Japan, such as the {{ill|Shioya Kanesayama Shrine Site|ja|塩屋金清神社遺跡|12=draft}} and the {{ill|Shima Site|ja|島遺跡|12=draft}} in {{ill|Gifu Prefecture|ja|岐阜県|12=draft}},Hayakawa & Hayashi 2000Miyoshi & Nagata 2012 as well as in western Japan, such as the {{ill|Mikuraoka Site|ja|見蔵岡遺跡|12=draft}} in {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県|12=draft}} and the {{ill|Kamizato Site|ja|上里遺跡|12=draft}} in {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府|12=draft}}.Matsui, Oshita & Tamura 1997Takahashi & Kondo 2011 These findings suggest that the use of stone rods as ritual objects was adopted as a cultural practice across different regions.Oshita 2001
Some stone rods have been found near the [[hearths]] of dwellings, often showing signs of heat damage or discoloration. This indicates their use in fire-related [[rituals]]. Ritual practices involving stone rods are thought to symbolically represent the phases of male genital arousal, sexual activity, and post-ejaculatory flaccidity through actions such as striking, heating, and breaking the rods. The prevalence of broken stone rods in archaeological sites has led some researchers to attribute symbolic meanings to this practice, though others remain skeptical.Gotō 1999
Stone rods were also used as burial goods, suggesting multiple [[shamanistic]] or ritualistic purposes.Yamada 2019: 206Taniguchi 2006
[[File:Hinotodake Santyô.JPG|thumb|Stone rod at the summit of {{ill|Mt. Chōkaku|ja|丁岳|12=draft}} on the border of {{ill|Akita Prefecture|ja|秋田県|12=draft}} and {{ill|Yamagata Prefecture|ja|山形県|12=draft}}]]
== References == {{Reflist}}

revision.text:
{{Multiple issues | refimprove = March 2016 }} [[File:Sekibou.jpg|thumb|Stone rod ({{ill|Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties|ja|奈良県立橿原考古学研究所|12=draft|12=draft}} Museum)]] [[File:Kitazawagawa Daisekibo.jpg|thumb|The largest stone rod in Japan, the {{ill|Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|ja|北沢大石棒|12=draft|12=draft}}]] ''''Stone rods''' (せきぼう, sekibō) are a type of polished [[stone tool]] from the [[Jōmon period]] in Japan. They are believed to represent [[phalluses]] and are associated with [[rituals]] and [[shamanistic practices]].Yamada 2019: 204
The largest known stone rod is the {{ill|Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|ja|北沢大石棒|12=draft|12=draft}} in {{ill|Sakuho, Nagano|ja|佐久穂町|12=draft|12=draft}}, measuring 223 cm in length and 25 cm in diameter.
== Overview == In a broad sense, "stone rods" encompass various rod-shaped stone artifacts, including [[stone swords]] and [[stone daggers]]. In a narrower sense, they often refer specifically to large stone rods.Tori 1924 These artifacts have been found throughout Japan, from [[Kyushu]] to [[Hokkaido]].
Phallic stone artifacts date back to the [[Upper Paleolithic]] period, such as the one found at the {{ill|Masukata Site|ja|升形遺跡|12=draft|12=draft}} in {{ill|Ōami-Shirasato, Chiba|ja|大羽白里市|12=draft|12=draft}}, dated to approximately 24,000 years ago.Harunari 1996 The specific type known as "large stone rods" emerged during the Middle Jōmon period in the [[Chūbu region]] and later spread eastward to areas such as the [[Kinki region]].Yamamoto 1979Okada 1960
Sites where stone rods were manufactured have been identified in eastern Japan, such as the {{ill|Shioya Kanesayama Shrine Site|ja|塩屋金清神社遺跡|12=draft|12=draft}} and the {{ill|Shima Site|ja|島遺跡|12=draft|12=draft}} in {{ill|Gifu Prefecture|ja|岐阜県|12=draft|12=draft}},Hayakawa & Hayashi 2000Miyoshi & Nagata 2012 as well as in western Japan, such as the {{ill|Mikuraoka Site|ja|見蔵岡遺跡|12=draft|12=draft}} in {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県|12=draft|12=draft}} and the {{ill|Kamizato Site|ja|上里遺跡|12=draft|12=draft}} in {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府|12=draft|12=draft}}.Matsui, Oshita & Tamura 1997Takahashi & Kondo 2011 These findings suggest that the use of stone rods as ritual objects was adopted as a cultural practice across different regions.Oshita 2001
Some stone rods have been found near the [[hearths]] of dwellings, often showing signs of heat damage or discoloration. This indicates their use in fire-related [[rituals]]. Ritual practices involving stone rods are thought to symbolically represent the phases of male genital arousal, sexual activity, and post-ejaculatory flaccidity through actions such as striking, heating, and breaking the rods. The prevalence of broken stone rods in archaeological sites has led some researchers to attribute symbolic meanings to this practice, though others remain skeptical.Gotō 1999
Stone rods were also used as burial goods, suggesting multiple [[shamanistic]] or ritualistic purposes.Yamada 2019: 206Taniguchi 2006
[[File:Hinotodake Santyô.JPG|thumb|Stone rod at the summit of {{ill|Mt. Chōkaku|ja|丁岳|12=draft|12=draft}} on the border of {{ill|Akita Prefecture|ja|秋田県|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Yamagata Prefecture|ja|山形県|12=draft|12=draft}}]]

== Further Reading ==

revision.comment: 43 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:石棒 up to rev 102197693
revision.text:
{{脚注の不足|date=2016年3月}} [[ファイル:Sekibou.jpg|thumb|石棒（[[奈良県立橿原考古学研究所#附属博物館|奈良県立橿原考古学研究所附属博物館]]）]] [[ファイル:Kitazawagawa Daisekibo.jpg|thumb|日本最大の石棒・[[北沢大石棒]]]] '''石棒'''（せきぼう）は、[[縄文時代]]の[[磨製石器]]の一つである。[[男根]]を模したと考えられる[[呪術]]・[[祭祀]]に関連した特殊な道具とみられる<ref name="#1">山田 2019：204</ref>。
最大の石棒は[[長野県]][[佐久穂町]]にある[[北沢大石棒]]で、長さは223cm、直径は25cmである。
== 概要 == 石棒は広義には[[石剣|石刀]]や[[石剣]]を含む棒状の石製品を総じて指し、狭義にはいわゆる[[大型石棒]]を指す場合が多い<ref>鳥居1924</ref>。広義の石棒は[[九州]]から[[北海道]]までほぼ[[全国]]に存在する。
男根を模した石製品としては、[[千葉県]][[大網白里市]][[升形遺跡]]出土の[[旧石器時代]]後期(約24000年前)のものまで遡れる<ref>春成1996</ref>。いわゆる大型石棒は、縄文時代中期に中部高地で出現したと考えられ、その後[[近畿地方]]以東を中心に広がったと考えられる<ref>山本1979</ref><ref>岡田1960</ref>。ただし、岐阜県の塩屋金清神社遺跡や島遺跡<ref>早川・林ほか2000</ref><ref>三好・長田2012</ref>など、いくつかの石棒製作遺跡が東日本を中心に確認されている一方で、西日本でも兵庫県見蔵岡遺跡<ref>松井・大下・田村1997</ref>や京都府上里遺跡<ref>高橋・近藤2011</ref>などの石棒製作遺跡が確認されている。これは決して東日本で製作された製品を西日本の人間が手にしただけではなく、1つの祭祀形態として受容していたことを示している<ref>大下2001</ref>。
住居内の[[炉]]の側で出土する事例が見られ、[[火熱]]による損壊変色があることから、石棒は火と関連する[[祭祀]]で用いられた祭祀具と考えられる<ref name="#1"/>。石棒の祭祀では、「勃起→性行為→射精→その後の萎縮」という男性器の一連の状態が「摩擦→叩打→被熱→破壊」として疑似的に見立てていたとされるものもある<ref name="#1"/>。このように破損して出土する事例が非常に多いため、そこに意味を求める説があるが、それに懐疑的な意見もある<ref>後藤1999</ref>。また、[[墓]]に副葬された石棒もあるように、幾通りもの呪術的機能を持っていたことが推定される<ref>山田 2019：206</ref><ref>谷口2006</ref>。
[[ファイル:Hinotodake Santyô.JPG|thumb|秋田県・山形県の[[丁岳]]山頂にある石棒]]
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== 参考文献 == *[[田中琢]]・[[佐原真]]（編集） 2002 『日本考古学事典』[[三省堂]]、487－488頁、 * [[田村晃一]]・合田芳正（監修） 2004 『考古学探訪の基礎用語』[[山川出版社]]、74頁 * [[山田康弘 (考古学者)|山田康弘]] 2019 『縄文時代の歴史』[[講談社現代新書]] *[[鳥居龍蔵]] 1924 「石棒、石剣」『諏訪史』第1巻 信濃教育会諏訪部会 *[[春成秀爾]] 1996 「性象徴の考古学」『国立歴史民俗博物館研究報告』66号 [[国立歴史民俗博物館]] *[[岡田茂弘]] 1960 「縄文文化の発展の地域性 近畿」『縄文時代』(日本の考古学Ⅱ) [[河出書房新社]] *[[後藤信祐]] 1999 「遺物研究 石棒・石剣・石刀」『縄文時代』第10号第4分冊 縄文時代文化研究会 *[[谷口康浩]] 2006 「石棒と石皿-象徴的生殖行為のコンテクスト-」『考古学』Ⅳ [[安斎正人]] *山本暉久 1979 「石棒祭祀の変遷(上・下)」『古代文化』31巻11・12号 [[古代学協会]] *早川正一・林直樹2000『岐阜県吉城郡宮川村 塩屋金清神社遺跡（Ａ地点）発掘調査報告書』岐阜県・宮川村教育委員会 *三好清超・長田友也2012『島遺跡2・塩屋金清神社遺跡3』(飛騨市文化財調査報告書4) 飛騨市教育委員会 *松井敬代・大下明・田村清一郎1997『見蔵岡遺跡その2』(竹野町文化財調査報告書11) 竹野町教育委員会 *高橋潔・近藤奈央2010『上里遺跡１-縄文時代晩期集落遺跡の調査-』(京都市埋蔵文化財研究所調査報告第24冊)京都市埋蔵文化財研究センター *大下明2001「近畿地方における大型石棒の受容と展開(上)－頭部笠状二段大型石棒の創出－」『縄文・弥生移行期の石製呪術具3』国立歴史民俗博物館春成研究室
== 関連項目 == * [[石剣]] * [[石器]] * [[砧]] * [[金精神]] * [[金剛杵]] * [[飛鳥の石造物#マラ石（明日香村祝戸）|マラ石]] * [[ミシャグジ]] - [[諏訪市|諏訪]]を中心に中部地方全域、関東地方の一部、近畿地方の一部まで広がる縄文中期頃を起源とした古代信仰の一つで、石棒や樹木を神体とする。 * [[蛇神]]
== 外部リンク == * [http://www.tamagawa.ac.jp/museum/archive/2001/119_1.html 石棒 玉川大学教育博物館] * [http://www.city.kunitachi.tokyo.jp/dbps_data/_material_/localhost/100000/101000/pdf/2012.07.10midorikawahigasiiseki.pdf 緑川東遺跡検出の敷石住居址と石棒について（PDF） 国立市]

revision.comment: Immanuelle moved page [[石棒]] to [[Sekibō]] without leaving a redirect: Bot: merge import
revision.text:
{{脚注の不足|date=2016年3月}} [[ファイル:Sekibou.jpg|thumb|石棒（[[奈良県立橿原考古学研究所#附属博物館|奈良県立橿原考古学研究所附属博物館]]）]] [[ファイル:Kitazawagawa Daisekibo.jpg|thumb|日本最大の石棒・[[北沢大石棒]]]] '''石棒'''（せきぼう）は、[[縄文時代]]の[[磨製石器]]の一つである。[[男根]]を模したと考えられる[[呪術]]・[[祭祀]]に関連した特殊な道具とみられる<ref name="#1">山田 2019：204</ref>。
最大の石棒は[[長野県]][[佐久穂町]]にある[[北沢大石棒]]で、長さは223cm、直径は25cmである。
== 概要 == 石棒は広義には[[石剣|石刀]]や[[石剣]]を含む棒状の石製品を総じて指し、狭義にはいわゆる[[大型石棒]]を指す場合が多い<ref>鳥居1924</ref>。広義の石棒は[[九州]]から[[北海道]]までほぼ[[全国]]に存在する。
男根を模した石製品としては、[[千葉県]][[大網白里市]][[升形遺跡]]出土の[[旧石器時代]]後期(約24000年前)のものまで遡れる<ref>春成1996</ref>。いわゆる大型石棒は、縄文時代中期に中部高地で出現したと考えられ、その後[[近畿地方]]以東を中心に広がったと考えられる<ref>山本1979</ref><ref>岡田1960</ref>。ただし、岐阜県の塩屋金清神社遺跡や島遺跡<ref>早川・林ほか2000</ref><ref>三好・長田2012</ref>など、いくつかの石棒製作遺跡が東日本を中心に確認されている一方で、西日本でも兵庫県見蔵岡遺跡<ref>松井・大下・田村1997</ref>や京都府上里遺跡<ref>高橋・近藤2011</ref>などの石棒製作遺跡が確認されている。これは決して東日本で製作された製品を西日本の人間が手にしただけではなく、1つの祭祀形態として受容していたことを示している<ref>大下2001</ref>。
住居内の[[炉]]の側で出土する事例が見られ、[[火熱]]による損壊変色があることから、石棒は火と関連する[[祭祀]]で用いられた祭祀具と考えられる<ref name="#1"/>。石棒の祭祀では、「勃起→性行為→射精→その後の萎縮」という男性器の一連の状態が「摩擦→叩打→被熱→破壊」として疑似的に見立てていたとされるものもある<ref name="#1"/>。このように破損して出土する事例が非常に多いため、そこに意味を求める説があるが、それに懐疑的な意見もある<ref>後藤1999</ref>。また、[[墓]]に副葬された石棒もあるように、幾通りもの呪術的機能を持っていたことが推定される<ref>山田 2019：206</ref><ref>谷口2006</ref>。
[[ファイル:Hinotodake Santyô.JPG|thumb|秋田県・山形県の[[丁岳]]山頂にある石棒]]
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== 参考文献 == *[[田中琢]]・[[佐原真]]（編集） 2002 『日本考古学事典』[[三省堂]]、487－488頁、 * [[田村晃一]]・合田芳正（監修） 2004 『考古学探訪の基礎用語』[[山川出版社]]、74頁 * [[山田康弘 (考古学者)|山田康弘]] 2019 『縄文時代の歴史』[[講談社現代新書]] *[[鳥居龍蔵]] 1924 「石棒、石剣」『諏訪史』第1巻 信濃教育会諏訪部会 *[[春成秀爾]] 1996 「性象徴の考古学」『国立歴史民俗博物館研究報告』66号 [[国立歴史民俗博物館]] *[[岡田茂弘]] 1960 「縄文文化の発展の地域性 近畿」『縄文時代』(日本の考古学Ⅱ) [[河出書房新社]] *[[後藤信祐]] 1999 「遺物研究 石棒・石剣・石刀」『縄文時代』第10号第4分冊 縄文時代文化研究会 *[[谷口康浩]] 2006 「石棒と石皿-象徴的生殖行為のコンテクスト-」『考古学』Ⅳ [[安斎正人]] *山本暉久 1979 「石棒祭祀の変遷(上・下)」『古代文化』31巻11・12号 [[古代学協会]] *早川正一・林直樹2000『岐阜県吉城郡宮川村 塩屋金清神社遺跡（Ａ地点）発掘調査報告書』岐阜県・宮川村教育委員会 *三好清超・長田友也2012『島遺跡2・塩屋金清神社遺跡3』(飛騨市文化財調査報告書4) 飛騨市教育委員会 *松井敬代・大下明・田村清一郎1997『見蔵岡遺跡その2』(竹野町文化財調査報告書11) 竹野町教育委員会 *高橋潔・近藤奈央2010『上里遺跡１-縄文時代晩期集落遺跡の調査-』(京都市埋蔵文化財研究所調査報告第24冊)京都市埋蔵文化財研究センター *大下明2001「近畿地方における大型石棒の受容と展開(上)－頭部笠状二段大型石棒の創出－」『縄文・弥生移行期の石製呪術具3』国立歴史民俗博物館春成研究室
== 関連項目 == * [[石剣]] * [[石器]] * [[砧]] * [[金精神]] * [[金剛杵]] * [[飛鳥の石造物#マラ石（明日香村祝戸）|マラ石]] * [[ミシャグジ]] - [[諏訪市|諏訪]]を中心に中部地方全域、関東地方の一部、近畿地方の一部まで広がる縄文中期頃を起源とした古代信仰の一つで、石棒や樹木を神体とする。 * [[蛇神]]
== 外部リンク == * [http://www.tamagawa.ac.jp/museum/archive/2001/119_1.html 石棒 玉川大学教育博物館] * [http://www.city.kunitachi.tokyo.jp/dbps_data/_material_/localhost/100000/101000/pdf/2012.07.10midorikawahigasiiseki.pdf 緑川東遺跡検出の敷石住居址と石棒について（PDF） 国立市]
{{Japanese-history-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:せきほう}} [[Category:石器]] [[Category:祭器]] [[Category:遺物]] [[Category:宗教と性]] [[Category:縄文時代]]

{{Multiple issues | refimprove = March 2016 }} [[File:Sekibou.jpg|thumb|Stone rod ({{ill|Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties|ja|奈良県立橿原考古学研究所|qq=draft|qq=draft}} Museum)]] [[File:Kitazawagawa Daisekibo.jpg|thumb|The largest stone rod in Japan, the {{ill|Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|ja|北沢大石棒|qq=draft|qq=draft}}]] ''''Stone rods''' (せきぼう, sekibō) are a type of polished [[stone tool]] from the [[Jōmon period]] in Japan. They are believed to represent [[phalluses]] and are associated with [[rituals]] and [[shamanistic practices]].Yamada 2019: 204
The largest known stone rod is the {{ill|Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|ja|北沢大石棒|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in {{ill|Sakuho, Nagano|ja|佐久穂町|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, measuring 223 cm in length and 25 cm in diameter.
== Overview == In a broad sense, "stone rods" encompass various rod-shaped stone artifacts, including [[stone swords]] and [[stone daggers]]. In a narrower sense, they often refer specifically to large stone rods.Tori 1924 These artifacts have been found throughout Japan, from [[Kyushu]] to [[Hokkaido]].
Phallic stone artifacts date back to the [[Upper Paleolithic]] period, such as the one found at the {{ill|Masukata Site|ja|升形遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in {{ill|Ōami-Shirasato, Chiba|ja|大羽白里市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, dated to approximately 24,000 years ago.Harunari 1996 The specific type known as "large stone rods" emerged during the Middle Jōmon period in the [[Chūbu region]] and later spread eastward to areas such as the [[Kinki region]].Yamamoto 1979Okada 1960
Sites where stone rods were manufactured have been identified in eastern Japan, such as the {{ill|Shioya Kanesayama Shrine Site|ja|塩屋金清神社遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and the {{ill|Shima Site|ja|島遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in {{ill|Gifu Prefecture|ja|岐阜県|qq=draft|qq=draft}},Hayakawa & Hayashi 2000Miyoshi & Nagata 2012 as well as in western Japan, such as the {{ill|Mikuraoka Site|ja|見蔵岡遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and the {{ill|Kamizato Site|ja|上里遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.Matsui, Oshita & Tamura 1997Takahashi & Kondo 2011 These findings suggest that the use of stone rods as ritual objects was adopted as a cultural practice across different regions.Oshita 2001
Some stone rods have been found near the [[hearths]] of dwellings, often showing signs of heat damage or discoloration. This indicates their use in fire-related [[rituals]]. Ritual practices involving stone rods are thought to symbolically represent the phases of male genital arousal, sexual activity, and post-ejaculatory flaccidity through actions such as striking, heating, and breaking the rods. The prevalence of broken stone rods in archaeological sites has led some researchers to attribute symbolic meanings to this practice, though others remain skeptical.Gotō 1999
Stone rods were also used as burial goods, suggesting multiple [[shamanistic]] or ritualistic purposes.Yamada 2019: 206Taniguchi 2006
[[File:Hinotodake Santyô.JPG|thumb|Stone rod at the summit of {{ill|Mt. Chōkaku|ja|丁岳|qq=draft|qq=draft}} on the border of {{ill|Akita Prefecture|ja|秋田県|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Yamagata Prefecture|ja|山形県|qq=draft|qq=draft}}]]
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading ==
Tanaka, Taku & Sahara, Makoto (Eds.) 2002. ''Encyclopedia of Japanese Archaeology''. Sanseidō. pp. 487–488.
Tamura, Kōichi & Aida, Yoshimasa (Supervisors) 2004. ''Basic Terminology of Archaeological Exploration''. Yamakawa Publishing. p. 74.
Yamada, Yasuhiro. 2019. ''The History of the Jōmon Period''. Kodansha Modern Library.
Tori, Ryūzō. 1924. "Stone Rods and Stone Swords." ''Suwa History,'' Volume 1. Shinano Educational Association Suwa Branch.
Harunari, Hideō. 1996. "The Archaeology of Sexual Symbols." ''Bulletin of the National Museum of Japanese History,'' Issue 66.
Okada, Shigehiro. 1960. "Regional Development of Jōmon Culture in Kinki." ''The Jōmon Period'' (Japanese Archaeology II). Kawade Shobō Shinsha.
Gotō, Shinsuke. 1999. "Research on Artifacts: Stone Rods, Stone Swords, and Stone Daggers." ''The Jōmon Period,'' Issue 10, Vol. 4. The Jōmon Period Cultural Research Society.
Taniguchi, Yasuhiro. 2006. "Stone Rods and Stone Plates: Contexts of Symbolic Reproductive Acts." ''Archaeology IV.'' Masato Anzai.
Yamamoto, Teruhisa. 1979. "Evolution of Rituals with Stone Rods (Parts 1 & 2)." ''Ancient Culture,'' Vol. 31, Issues 11–12. Association for Ancient Studies.
Hayakawa, Shoichi & Hayashi, Naoki. 2000. ''Excavation Report of the Shioya Kanesayama Shrine Site (A Area).'' Gifu Prefecture & Miyagawa Village Board of Education.
Miyoshi, Seichō & Nagata, Tomoya. 2012. ''The Shima and Shioya Kanesayama Sites.'' Hida City Board of Education.
Matsui, Takayo; Oshita, Akira; Tamura, Seiichirō. 1997. ''Mikuraoka Site II.'' Takenochō Board of Education.
Takahashi, Kiyoshi & Kondo, Nao. 2010. ''The Kamizato Site I: A Jōmon Late-Period Settlement Excavation.'' Kyoto City Archaeological Research Center Report No. 24.
Oshita, Akira. 2001. "Adoption and Transformation of Large Stone Rods in the Kinki Region (Parts 1 & 2)." ''Stone Ritual Implements in the Transition from Jōmon to Yayoi Periods III.'' Spring Research Lab, National Museum of Japanese History. == See Also ==

Miyoshi, Seichō & Nagata, Tomoya. 2012. ''The Shima and Shioya Kanesayama Sites.'' Hida City Board of Education.
Matsui, Takayo; Oshita, Akira; Tamura, Seiichirō. 1997. ''Mikuraoka Site II.'' Takenochō Board of Education.
Takahashi, Kiyoshi & Kondo, Nao. 2010. ''The Kamizato Site I: A Jōmon Late-Period Settlement Excavation.'' Kyoto City Archaeological Research Center Report No. 24.
Oshita, Akira. 2001. "Adoption and Transformation of Large Stone Rods in the Kinki Region (Parts 1 & 2)." ''Stone Ritual Implements in the Transition from Jōmon to Yayoi Periods III.'' Spring Research Lab, National Museum of Japanese History. == See Also ==

[[en:Sekibō]] [[sn:Ishibou]] [[ja:石棒]] [[pl:Sekibō]] [[ru:Сэкибо]] [[ru:Sekibo]] [[en:Ishibou]] [[sn:Sekibō]]
<!--Ishibou--><!--Ishibou-->

{{Multiple issues | refimprove = March 2016 }} [[File:Sekibou.jpg|thumb|Stone rod ({{ill|Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties|ja|奈良県立橿原考古学研究所|qq=draft|qq=draft}} Museum)]] [[File:Kitazawagawa Daisekibo.jpg|thumb|The largest stone rod in Japan, the {{ill|Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|ja|北沢大石棒|qq=draft|qq=draft}}]] ''''Stone rods''' (せきぼう, sekibō) are a type of polished [[stone tool]] from the [[Jōmon period]] in Japan. They are believed to represent [[phalluses]] and are associated with [[rituals]] and [[shamanistic practices]].Yamada 2019: 204
The largest known stone rod is the {{ill|Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|ja|北沢大石棒|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in {{ill|Sakuho, Nagano|ja|佐久穂町|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, measuring 223 cm in length and 25 cm in diameter.
== Overview == In a broad sense, "stone rods" encompass various rod-shaped stone artifacts, including [[stone swords]] and [[stone daggers]]. In a narrower sense, they often refer specifically to large stone rods.Tori 1924 These artifacts have been found throughout Japan, from [[Kyushu]] to [[Hokkaido]].
Phallic stone artifacts date back to the [[Upper Paleolithic]] period, such as the one found at the {{ill|Masukata Site|ja|升形遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in {{ill|Ōami-Shirasato, Chiba|ja|大羽白里市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, dated to approximately 24,000 years ago.Harunari 1996 The specific type known as "large stone rods" emerged during the Middle Jōmon period in the [[Chūbu region]] and later spread eastward to areas such as the [[Kinki region]].Yamamoto 1979Okada 1960
Sites where stone rods were manufactured have been identified in eastern Japan, such as the {{ill|Shioya Kanesayama Shrine Site|ja|塩屋金清神社遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and the {{ill|Shima Site|ja|島遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in {{ill|Gifu Prefecture|ja|岐阜県|qq=draft|qq=draft}},Hayakawa & Hayashi 2000Miyoshi & Nagata 2012 as well as in western Japan, such as the {{ill|Mikuraoka Site|ja|見蔵岡遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and the {{ill|Kamizato Site|ja|上里遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.Matsui, Oshita & Tamura 1997Takahashi & Kondo 2011 These findings suggest that the use of stone rods as ritual objects was adopted as a cultural practice across different regions.Oshita 2001
Some stone rods have been found near the [[hearths]] of dwellings, often showing signs of heat damage or discoloration. This indicates their use in fire-related [[rituals]]. Ritual practices involving stone rods are thought to symbolically represent the phases of male genital arousal, sexual activity, and post-ejaculatory flaccidity through actions such as striking, heating, and breaking the rods. The prevalence of broken stone rods in archaeological sites has led some researchers to attribute symbolic meanings to this practice, though others remain skeptical.Gotō 1999
Stone rods were also used as burial goods, suggesting multiple [[shamanistic]] or ritualistic purposes.Yamada 2019: 206Taniguchi 2006
[[File:Hinotodake Santyô.JPG|thumb|Stone rod at the summit of {{ill|Mt. Chōkaku|ja|丁岳|qq=draft|qq=draft}} on the border of {{ill|Akita Prefecture|ja|秋田県|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Yamagata Prefecture|ja|山形県|qq=draft|qq=draft}}]]

In a narrower sense, they often refer specifically to large stone rods.Tori 1924 These artifacts have been found throughout Japan, from [[Kyushu]] to [[Hokkaido]].
Phallic stone artifacts date back to the [[Upper Paleolithic]] period, such as the one found at the {{ill|Masukata Site|ja|升形遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Masukata Site|qq=}} in {{ill|Ōami-Shirasato, Chiba|ja|大羽白里市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōami-Shirasato, Chiba|qq=}}, dated to approximately 24,000 years ago.Harunari 1996 The specific type known as "large stone rods" emerged during the Middle Jōmon period in the [[Chūbu region]] and later spread eastward to areas such as the [[Kinki region]].Yamamoto 1979Okada 1960
Sites where stone rods were manufactured have been identified in eastern Japan, such as the {{ill|Shioya Kanesayama Shrine Site|ja|塩屋金清神社遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shioya Kanesayama Shrine Site|qq=}} and the {{ill|Shima Site|ja|島遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shima Site|qq=}} in {{ill|Gifu Prefecture|ja|岐阜県|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gifu Prefecture|qq=}},Hayakawa & Hayashi 2000Miyoshi & Nagata 2012 as well as in western Japan, such as the {{ill|Mikuraoka Site|ja|見蔵岡遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikuraoka Site|qq=}} in {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyōgo Prefecture|qq=}} and the {{ill|Kamizato Site|ja|上里遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamizato Site|qq=}} in {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto Prefecture|qq=}}.Matsui, Oshita & Tamura 1997Takahashi & Kondo 2011 These findings suggest that the use of stone rods as ritual objects was adopted as a cultural practice across different regions.Oshita 2001
Some stone rods have been found near the [[hearths]] of dwellings, often showing signs of heat damage or discoloration. This indicates their use in fire-related [[rituals]]. Ritual practices involving stone rods are thought to symbolically represent the phases of male genital arousal, sexual activity, and post-ejaculatory flaccidity through actions such as striking, heating, and breaking the rods. The prevalence of broken stone rods in archaeological sites has led some researchers to attribute symbolic meanings to this practice, though others remain skeptical.Gotō 1999
Stone rods were also used as burial goods, suggesting multiple [[shamanistic]] or ritualistic purposes.Yamada 2019: 206Taniguchi 2006
[[File:Hinotodake Santyô.JPG|thumb|Stone rod at the summit of {{ill|Mt. Chōkaku|ja|丁岳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mt. Chōkaku|qq=}} on the border of {{ill|Akita Prefecture|ja|秋田県|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akita Prefecture|qq=}} and {{ill|Yamagata Prefecture|ja|山形県|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamagata Prefecture|qq=}}]]

Harunari, Hideō. 1996. "The Archaeology of Sexual Symbols." ''Bulletin of the National Museum of Japanese History,'' Issue 66.

Oshita, Akira. 2001. "Adoption and Transformation of Large Stone Rods in the Kinki Region (Parts 1 & 2)." ''Stone Ritual Implements in the Transition from Jōmon to Yayoi Periods III.'' Spring Research Lab, National Museum of Japanese History. == See Also ==

{{Multiple issues | refimprove = March 2016 }} [[File:Sekibou.jpg|thumb|Stone rod ({{ill|Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties|ja|奈良県立橿原考古学研究所|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties|qq=}} Museum)]] [[File:Kitazawagawa Daisekibo.jpg|thumb|The largest stone rod in Japan, the {{ill|Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|ja|北沢大石棒|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|qq=}}]] ''''Stone rods''' (せきぼう, sekibō) are a type of polished [[stone tool]] from the [[Jōmon period]] in Japan. They are believed to represent [[phalluses]] and are associated with [[rituals]] and [[shamanistic practices]].Yamada 2019: 204
The largest known stone rod is the {{ill|Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|ja|北沢大石棒|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|qq=}} in {{ill|Sakuho, Nagano|ja|佐久穂町|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakuho, Nagano|qq=}}, measuring 223 cm in length and 25 cm in diameter.
== Overview == In a broad sense, "stone rods" encompass various rod-shaped stone artifacts, including [[stone swords]] and [[stone daggers]]. In a narrower sense, they often refer specifically to large stone rods.Tori 1924 These artifacts have been found throughout Japan, from [[Kyushu]] to [[Hokkaido]].
Phallic stone artifacts date back to the [[Upper Paleolithic]] period, such as the one found at the {{ill|Masukata Site|ja|升形遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Masukata Site|qq=}} in {{ill|Ōami-Shirasato, Chiba|ja|大羽白里市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōami-Shirasato, Chiba|qq=}}, dated to approximately 24,000 years ago.Harunari 1996 The specific type known as "large stone rods" emerged during the Middle Jōmon period in the [[Chūbu region]] and later spread eastward to areas such as the [[Kinki region]].Yamamoto 1979Okada 1960
Sites where stone rods were manufactured have been identified in eastern Japan, such as the {{ill|Shioya Kanesayama Shrine Site|ja|塩屋金清神社遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shioya Kanesayama Shrine Site|qq=}} and the {{ill|Shima Site|ja|島遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shima Site|qq=}} in {{ill|Gifu Prefecture|ja|岐阜県|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gifu Prefecture|qq=}},Hayakawa & Hayashi 2000Miyoshi & Nagata 2012 as well as in western Japan, such as the {{ill|Mikuraoka Site|ja|見蔵岡遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikuraoka Site|qq=}} in {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyōgo Prefecture|qq=}} and the {{ill|Kamizato Site|ja|上里遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamizato Site|qq=}} in {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto Prefecture|qq=}}.Matsui, Oshita & Tamura 1997Takahashi & Kondo 2011 These findings suggest that the use of stone rods as ritual objects was adopted as a cultural practice across different regions.Oshita 2001
Some stone rods have been found near the [[hearths]] of dwellings, often showing signs of heat damage or discoloration. This indicates their use in fire-related [[rituals]]. Ritual practices involving stone rods are thought to symbolically represent the phases of male genital arousal, sexual activity, and post-ejaculatory flaccidity through actions such as striking, heating, and breaking the rods. The prevalence of broken stone rods in archaeological sites has led some researchers to attribute symbolic meanings to this practice, though others remain skeptical.Gotō 1999
Stone rods were also used as burial goods, suggesting multiple [[shamanistic]] or ritualistic purposes.Yamada 2019: 206Taniguchi 2006
[[File:Hinotodake Santyô.JPG|thumb|Stone rod at the summit of {{ill|Mt. Chōkaku|ja|丁岳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mt. Chōkaku|qq=}} on the border of {{ill|Akita Prefecture|ja|秋田県|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akita Prefecture|qq=}} and {{ill|Yamagata Prefecture|ja|山形県|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamagata Prefecture|qq=}}]]

In a narrower sense, they often refer specifically to large stone rods.Tori 1924 These artifacts have been found throughout Japan, from [[Kyushu]] to [[Hokkaido]].
Phallic stone artifacts date back to the [[Upper Paleolithic]] period, such as the one found at the {{ill|Masukata Site|ja|升形遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Masukata Site|qq=}} in {{ill|Ōami-Shirasato, Chiba|ja|大羽白里市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōami-Shirasato, Chiba|qq=}}, dated to approximately 24,000 years ago.Harunari 1996 The specific type known as "large stone rods" emerged during the Middle Jōmon period in the [[Chūbu region]] and later spread eastward to areas such as the [[Kinki region]].Yamamoto 1979Okada 1960
Sites where stone rods were manufactured have been identified in eastern Japan, such as the {{ill|Shioya Kanesayama Shrine Site|ja|塩屋金清神社遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shioya Kanesayama Shrine Site|qq=}} and the {{ill|Shima Site|ja|島遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shima Site|qq=}} in {{ill|Gifu Prefecture|ja|岐阜県|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gifu Prefecture|qq=}},Hayakawa & Hayashi 2000Miyoshi & Nagata 2012 as well as in western Japan, such as the {{ill|Mikuraoka Site|ja|見蔵岡遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikuraoka Site|qq=}} in {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyōgo Prefecture|qq=}} and the {{ill|Kamizato Site|ja|上里遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamizato Site|qq=}} in {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto Prefecture|qq=}}.Matsui, Oshita & Tamura 1997Takahashi & Kondo 2011 These findings suggest that the use of stone rods as ritual objects was adopted as a cultural practice across different regions.Oshita 2001
Some stone rods have been found near the [[hearths]] of dwellings, often showing signs of heat damage or discoloration. This indicates their use in fire-related [[rituals]]. Ritual practices involving stone rods are thought to symbolically represent the phases of male genital arousal, sexual activity, and post-ejaculatory flaccidity through actions such as striking, heating, and breaking the rods. The prevalence of broken stone rods in archaeological sites has led some researchers to attribute symbolic meanings to this practice, though others remain skeptical.Gotō 1999
Stone rods were also used as burial goods, suggesting multiple [[shamanistic]] or ritualistic purposes.Yamada 2019: 206Taniguchi 2006
[[File:Hinotodake Santyô.JPG|thumb|Stone rod at the summit of {{ill|Mt. Chōkaku|ja|丁岳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mt. Chōkaku|qq=}} on the border of {{ill|Akita Prefecture|ja|秋田県|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akita Prefecture|qq=}} and {{ill|Yamagata Prefecture|ja|山形県|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamagata Prefecture|qq=}}]]

{{Multiple issues | refimprove = March 2016 }} [[File:Sekibou.jpg|thumb|Stone rod ({{ill|Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties|ja|奈良県立橿原考古学研究所|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties|qq=}} Museum)]] [[File:Kitazawagawa Daisekibo.jpg|thumb|The largest stone rod in Japan, the {{ill|Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|ja|北沢大石棒|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|qq=}}]] ''''Stone rods''' (せきぼう, sekibō) are a type of polished [[stone tool]] from the [[Jōmon period]] in Japan. They are believed to represent [[phalluses]] and are associated with [[rituals]] and [[shamanistic practices]].Yamada 2019: 204
The largest known stone rod is the {{ill|Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|ja|北沢大石棒|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|qq=}} in [[:en:Sakuho, Nagano|Sakuho, Nagano]], measuring 223 cm in length and 25 cm in diameter.
== Overview == In a broad sense, "stone rods" encompass various rod-shaped stone artifacts, including [[stone swords]] and [[stone daggers]]. In a narrower sense, they often refer specifically to large stone rods.Tori 1924 These artifacts have been found throughout Japan, from [[Kyushu]] to [[Hokkaido]].
Phallic stone artifacts date back to the [[Upper Paleolithic]] period, such as the one found at the {{ill|Masukata Site|ja|升形遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Masukata Site|qq=}} in {{ill|Ōami-Shirasato, Chiba|ja|大羽白里市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōami-Shirasato, Chiba|qq=}}, dated to approximately 24,000 years ago.Harunari 1996 The specific type known as "large stone rods" emerged during the Middle Jōmon period in the [[Chūbu region]] and later spread eastward to areas such as the [[Kinki region]].Yamamoto 1979Okada 1960
Sites where stone rods were manufactured have been identified in eastern Japan, such as the {{ill|Shioya Kanesayama Shrine Site|ja|塩屋金清神社遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shioya Kanesayama Shrine Site|qq=}} and the {{ill|Shima Site|ja|島遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shima Site|qq=}} in [[:en:Gifu Prefecture|Gifu Prefecture]],Hayakawa & Hayashi 2000Miyoshi & Nagata 2012 as well as in western Japan, such as the {{ill|Mikuraoka Site|ja|見蔵岡遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikuraoka Site|qq=}} in [[:en:Hyōgo Prefecture|Hyōgo Prefecture]] and the {{ill|Kamizato Site|ja|上里遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamizato Site|qq=}} in [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]].Matsui, Oshita & Tamura 1997Takahashi & Kondo 2011 These findings suggest that the use of stone rods as ritual objects was adopted as a cultural practice across different regions.Oshita 2001
Some stone rods have been found near the [[hearths]] of dwellings, often showing signs of heat damage or discoloration. This indicates their use in fire-related [[rituals]]. Ritual practices involving stone rods are thought to symbolically represent the phases of male genital arousal, sexual activity, and post-ejaculatory flaccidity through actions such as striking, heating, and breaking the rods. The prevalence of broken stone rods in archaeological sites has led some researchers to attribute symbolic meanings to this practice, though others remain skeptical.Gotō 1999
Stone rods were also used as burial goods, suggesting multiple [[shamanistic]] or ritualistic purposes.Yamada 2019: 206Taniguchi 2006
[[File:Hinotodake Santyô.JPG|thumb|Stone rod at the summit of {{ill|Mt. Chōkaku|ja|丁岳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mt. Chōkaku|qq=}} on the border of [[:en:Akita Prefecture|Akita Prefecture]] and [[:en:Yamagata Prefecture|Yamagata Prefecture]]]]

Gotō, Shinsuke. 1999. "Research on Artifacts: Stone Rods, Stone Swords, and Stone Daggers." ''The Jōmon Period,'' Issue 10, Vol. 4. The Jōmon Period Cultural Research Society.

Yamamoto, Teruhisa. 1979. "Evolution of Rituals with Stone Rods (Parts 1 & 2)." ''Ancient Culture,'' Vol. 31, Issues 11–12. Association for Ancient Studies.

Miyoshi, Seichō & Nagata, Tomoya. 2012. ''The Shima and Shioya Kanesayama Sites.'' Hida City Board of Education.

Takahashi, Kiyoshi & Kondo, Nao. 2010. ''The Kamizato Site I: A Jōmon Late-Period Settlement Excavation.'' Kyoto City Archaeological Research Center Report No. 24.
Oshita, Akira. 2001. "Adoption and Transformation of Large Stone Rods in the Kinki Region (Parts 1 & 2)." ''Stone Ritual Implements in the Transition from Jōmon to Yayoi Periods III.'' Spring Research Lab, National Museum of Japanese History. == See Also ==

[[en:Sekibō]] [[sn:Ishibou]] [[ja:石棒]] [[pl:Sekibō]] [[ru:Сэкибо]] [[ru:Sekibo]] [[en:Ishibou]] [[sn:Sekibō]]
<!--Ishibou--><!--Ishibou--> [[Category:Sexuality and religion]] [[Category:Lithics]]

{{Multiple issues | refimprove = March 2016 }} [[File:Sekibou.jpg|thumb|Stone rod ({{ill|Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties|ja|奈良県立橿原考古学研究所|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties|qq=}} Museum)]] [[File:Kitazawagawa Daisekibo.jpg|thumb|The largest stone rod in Japan, the {{ill|Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|ja|北沢大石棒|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|qq=}}]] ''''Stone rods''' (せきぼう, sekibō) are a type of polished [[stone tool]] from the [[Jōmon period]] in Japan. They are believed to represent [[phalluses]] and are associated with [[rituals]] and [[shamanistic practices]].Yamada 2019: 204
The largest known stone rod is the {{ill|Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|ja|北沢大石棒|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|qq=}} in [[:en:Sakuho, Nagano|Sakuho, Nagano]], measuring 223 cm in length and 25 cm in diameter.
== Overview == In a broad sense, "stone rods" encompass various rod-shaped stone artifacts, including [[stone swords]] and [[stone daggers]]. In a narrower sense, they often refer specifically to large stone rods.Tori 1924 These artifacts have been found throughout Japan, from [[Kyushu]] to [[Hokkaido]].
Phallic stone artifacts date back to the [[Upper Paleolithic]] period, such as the one found at the {{ill|Masukata Site|ja|升形遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Masukata Site|qq=}} in {{ill|Ōami-Shirasato, Chiba|ja|大羽白里市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōami-Shirasato, Chiba|qq=}}, dated to approximately 24,000 years ago.Harunari 1996 The specific type known as "large stone rods" emerged during the Middle Jōmon period in the [[Chūbu region]] and later spread eastward to areas such as the [[Kinki region]].Yamamoto 1979Okada 1960
Sites where stone rods were manufactured have been identified in eastern Japan, such as the {{ill|Shioya Kanesayama Shrine Site|ja|塩屋金清神社遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shioya Kanesayama Shrine Site|qq=}} and the {{ill|Shima Site|ja|島遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shima Site|qq=}} in [[:en:Gifu Prefecture|Gifu Prefecture]],Hayakawa & Hayashi 2000Miyoshi & Nagata 2012 as well as in western Japan, such as the {{ill|Mikuraoka Site|ja|見蔵岡遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikuraoka Site|qq=}} in [[:en:Hyōgo Prefecture|Hyōgo Prefecture]] and the {{ill|Kamizato Site|ja|上里遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamizato Site|qq=}} in [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]].Matsui, Oshita & Tamura 1997Takahashi & Kondo 2011 These findings suggest that the use of stone rods as ritual objects was adopted as a cultural practice across different regions.Oshita 2001
Some stone rods have been found near the [[hearths]] of dwellings, often showing signs of heat damage or discoloration. This indicates their use in fire-related [[rituals]]. Ritual practices involving stone rods are thought to symbolically represent the phases of male genital arousal, sexual activity, and post-ejaculatory flaccidity through actions such as striking, heating, and breaking the rods. The prevalence of broken stone rods in archaeological sites has led some researchers to attribute symbolic meanings to this practice, though others remain skeptical.Gotō 1999
Stone rods were also used as burial goods, suggesting multiple [[shamanistic]] or ritualistic purposes.Yamada 2019: 206Taniguchi 2006
[[File:Hinotodake Santyô.JPG|thumb|Stone rod at the summit of {{ill|Mt. Chōkaku|ja|丁岳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mt. Chōkaku|qq=}} on the border of [[:en:Akita Prefecture|Akita Prefecture]] and [[:en:Yamagata Prefecture|Yamagata Prefecture]]]]

The largest known stone rod is the {{ill|Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|ja|北沢大石棒|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|qq=}} in [[:en:Sakuho, Nagano|Sakuho, Nagano]], measuring 223 cm in length and 25 cm in diameter.
== Overview == In a broad sense, "stone rods" encompass various rod-shaped stone artifacts, including [[stone swords]] and [[stone daggers]]. In a narrower sense, they often refer specifically to large stone rods.Tori 1924 These artifacts have been found throughout Japan, from [[Kyushu]] to [[Hokkaido]].
Phallic stone artifacts date back to the [[Upper Paleolithic]] period, such as the one found at the {{ill|Masukata Site|ja|升形遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Masukata Site|qq=}} in {{ill|Ōami-Shirasato, Chiba|ja|大羽白里市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōami-Shirasato, Chiba|qq=}}, dated to approximately 24,000 years ago.Harunari 1996 The specific type known as "large stone rods" emerged during the Middle Jōmon period in the [[Chūbu region]] and later spread eastward to areas such as the [[Kinki region]].Yamamoto 1979Okada 1960
Sites where stone rods were manufactured have been identified in eastern Japan, such as the {{ill|Shioya Kanesayama Shrine Site|ja|塩屋金清神社遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shioya Kanesayama Shrine Site|qq=}} and the {{ill|Shima Site|ja|島遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shima Site|qq=}} in [[:en:Gifu Prefecture|Gifu Prefecture]],Hayakawa & Hayashi 2000Miyoshi & Nagata 2012 as well as in western Japan, such as the {{ill|Mikuraoka Site|ja|見蔵岡遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikuraoka Site|qq=}} in [[:en:Hyōgo Prefecture|Hyōgo Prefecture]] and the {{ill|Kamizato Site|ja|上里遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamizato Site|qq=}} in [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]].Matsui, Oshita & Tamura 1997Takahashi & Kondo 2011 These findings suggest that the use of stone rods as ritual objects was adopted as a cultural practice across different regions.Oshita 2001
Some stone rods have been found near the [[hearths]] of dwellings, often showing signs of heat damage or discoloration. This indicates their use in fire-related [[rituals]]. Ritual practices involving stone rods are thought to symbolically represent the phases of male genital arousal, sexual activity, and post-ejaculatory flaccidity through actions such as striking, heating, and breaking the rods. The prevalence of broken stone rods in archaeological sites has led some researchers to attribute symbolic meanings to this practice, though others remain skeptical.Gotō 1999
Stone rods were also used as burial goods, suggesting multiple [[shamanistic]] or ritualistic purposes.Yamada 2019: 206Taniguchi 2006
[[File:Hinotodake Santyô.JPG|thumb|Stone rod at the summit of {{ill|Mt. Chōkaku|ja|丁岳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mt. Chōkaku|qq=}} on the border of [[:en:Akita Prefecture|Akita Prefecture]] and [[:en:Yamagata Prefecture|Yamagata Prefecture]]]]

Harunari, Hideō. 1996. "The Archaeology of Sexual Symbols." ''Bulletin of the National Museum of Japanese History,'' Issue 66.

Gotō, Shinsuke. 1999. "Research on Artifacts: Stone Rods, Stone Swords, and Stone Daggers." ''The Jōmon Period,'' Issue 10, Vol. 4. The Jōmon Period Cultural Research Society.
Taniguchi, Yasuhiro. 2006. "Stone Rods and Stone Plates: Contexts of Symbolic Reproductive Acts." ''Archaeology IV.'' Masato Anzai.
Yamamoto, Teruhisa. 1979. "Evolution of Rituals with Stone Rods (Parts 1 & 2)." ''Ancient Culture,'' Vol. 31, Issues 11–12. Association for Ancient Studies.

The prevalence of broken stone rods in archaeological sites has led some researchers to attribute symbolic meanings to this practice, though others remain skeptical.Gotō 1999

[[File:Hinotodake Santyô.JPG|thumb|Stone rod at the summit of {{ill|Mt. Chōkaku|ja|丁岳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mt. Chōkaku|qq=}} on the border of [[:en:Akita Prefecture|Akita Prefecture]] and [[:en:Yamagata Prefecture|Yamagata Prefecture]]]]

revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
{{Multiple issues | refimprove = March 2016 }} [[File:Sekibou.jpg|thumb|Stone rod ({{ill|Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties|ja|奈良県立橿原考古学研究所|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties|qq=}} Museum)]] [[File:Kitazawagawa Daisekibo.jpg|thumb|The largest stone rod in Japan, the {{ill|Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|ja|北沢大石棒|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|qq=}}]] ''''Stone rods''' (せきぼう, sekibō) are a type of polished [[stone tool]] from the [[Jōmon period]] in Japan. They are believed to represent [[phalluses]] and are associated with [[rituals]] and [[shamanistic practices]].Yamada 2019: 204
The largest known stone rod is the {{ill|Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|ja|北沢大石棒|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|qq=}} in [[:en:Sakuho, Nagano|Sakuho, Nagano]], measuring 223 cm in length and 25 cm in diameter.
== Overview == In a broad sense, "stone rods" encompass various rod-shaped stone artifacts, including [[stone swords]] and [[stone daggers]]. In a narrower sense, they often refer specifically to large stone rods.Tori 1924 These artifacts have been found throughout Japan, from [[Kyushu]] to [[Hokkaido]].
Phallic stone artifacts date back to the [[Upper Paleolithic]] period, such as the one found at the {{ill|Masukata Site|ja|升形遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Masukata Site|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Ōami-Shirasato, Chiba|ja|大羽白里市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōami-Shirasato, Chiba|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, dated to approximately 24,000 years ago.Harunari 1996 The specific type known as "large stone rods" emerged during the Middle Jōmon period in the [[:en:Chūbu region|Chūbu region]] and later spread eastward to areas such as the [[Kinki region]].Yamamoto 1979Okada 1960
Sites where stone rods were manufactured have been identified in eastern Japan, such as the {{ill|Shioya Kanesayama Shrine Site|ja|塩屋金清神社遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shioya Kanesayama Shrine Site|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and the {{ill|Shima Site|ja|島遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shima Site|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:Gifu Prefecture|Gifu Prefecture]],Hayakawa & Hayashi 2000Miyoshi & Nagata 2012 as well as in western Japan, such as the {{ill|Mikuraoka Site|ja|見蔵岡遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikuraoka Site|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:Hyōgo Prefecture|Hyōgo Prefecture]] and the {{ill|Kamizato Site|ja|上里遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamizato Site|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]].Matsui, Oshita & Tamura 1997Takahashi & Kondo 2011 These findings suggest that the use of stone rods as ritual objects was adopted as a cultural practice across different regions.Oshita 2001
Some stone rods have been found near the [[:en:hearths|hearths]] of dwellings, often showing signs of heat damage or discoloration. This indicates their use in fire-related [[rituals]]. Ritual practices involving stone rods are thought to symbolically represent the phases of male genital arousal, sexual activity, and post-ejaculatory flaccidity through actions such as striking, heating, and breaking the rods. The prevalence of broken stone rods in archaeological sites has led some researchers to attribute symbolic meanings to this practice, though others remain skeptical.Gotō 1999
Stone rods were also used as burial goods, suggesting multiple [[shamanistic]] or ritualistic purposes.Yamada 2019: 206Taniguchi 2006
[[File:Hinotodake Santyô.JPG|thumb|Stone rod at the summit of {{ill|Mt. Chōkaku|ja|丁岳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mt. Chōkaku|qq=}} on the border of [[:en:Akita Prefecture|Akita Prefecture]] and [[:en:Yamagata Prefecture|Yamagata Prefecture]]]]

[[Mishaguji]] — An ancient belief system originating in the Jōmon period, centered around stone rods and trees as sacred objects, prevalent in [[:en:Suwa, Nagano|Suwa]] and surrounding regions.
[[Snake deity]]

<!--Ishibou--><!--Ishibou--> [[Category:Sexuality and religion]] [[Category:Lithics]] [[Category:Festival Utensils]] [[Category:translated pages with valid en interwikis]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{Commons category|Sekibō}} <!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{Commons category|Sekibō}}

revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
{{Multiple issues | refimprove = March 2016 }} [[File:Sekibou.jpg|thumb|Stone rod ({{ill|Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties|ja|奈良県立橿原考古学研究所|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties|qq=}} Museum)]] [[File:Kitazawagawa Daisekibo.jpg|thumb|The largest stone rod in Japan, the {{ill|Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|ja|北沢大石棒|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|qq=}}]] ''''Stone rods''' (せきぼう, sekibō) are a type of polished [[stone tool]] from the [[Jōmon period]] in Japan. They are believed to represent [[phalluses]] and are associated with [[rituals]] and [[shamanistic practices]].Yamada 2019: 204
The largest known stone rod is the {{ill|Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|ja|北沢大石棒|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|qq=}} in [[:en:Sakuho, Nagano|Sakuho, Nagano]], measuring 223 cm in length and 25 cm in diameter.
== Overview == In a broad sense, "stone rods" encompass various rod-shaped stone artifacts, including [[stone swords]] and [[stone daggers]]. In a narrower sense, they often refer specifically to large stone rods.Tori 1924 These artifacts have been found throughout Japan, from [[Kyushu]] to [[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaidō]].
Phallic stone artifacts date back to the [[Upper Paleolithic]] period, such as the one found at the {{ill|Masukata Site|ja|升形遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Masukata Site|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Ōami-Shirasato, Chiba|ja|大羽白里市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōami-Shirasato, Chiba|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, dated to approximately 24,000 years ago.Harunari 1996 The specific type known as "large stone rods" emerged during the Middle Jōmon period in the [[:en:Chūbu region|Chūbu region]] and later spread eastward to areas such as the [[Kinki region]].Yamamoto 1979Okada 1960
Sites where stone rods were manufactured have been identified in eastern Japan, such as the {{ill|Shioya Kanesayama Shrine Site|ja|塩屋金清神社遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shioya Kanesayama Shrine Site|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and the {{ill|Shima Site|ja|島遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shima Site|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:Gifu Prefecture|Gifu Prefecture]],Hayakawa & Hayashi 2000Miyoshi & Nagata 2012 as well as in western Japan, such as the {{ill|Mikuraoka Site|ja|見蔵岡遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikuraoka Site|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:Hyōgo Prefecture|Hyōgo Prefecture]] and the {{ill|Kamizato Site|ja|上里遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamizato Site|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]].Matsui, Oshita & Tamura 1997Takahashi & Kondo 2011 These findings suggest that the use of stone rods as ritual objects was adopted as a cultural practice across different regions.Oshita 2001
Some stone rods have been found near the [[:en:hearths|hearths]] of dwellings, often showing signs of heat damage or discoloration. This indicates their use in fire-related [[rituals]]. Ritual practices involving stone rods are thought to symbolically represent the phases of male genital arousal, sexual activity, and post-ejaculatory flaccidity through actions such as striking, heating, and breaking the rods. The prevalence of broken stone rods in archaeological sites has led some researchers to attribute symbolic meanings to this practice, though others remain skeptical.Gotō 1999
Stone rods were also used as burial goods, suggesting multiple [[shamanistic]] or ritualistic purposes.Yamada 2019: 206Taniguchi 2006
[[File:Hinotodake Santyô.JPG|thumb|Stone rod at the summit of {{ill|Mt. Chōkaku|ja|丁岳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mt. Chōkaku|qq=}} on the border of [[:en:Akita Prefecture|Akita Prefecture]] and [[:en:Yamagata Prefecture|Yamagata Prefecture]]]]
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading ==
Tanaka, Taku & Sahara, Makoto (Eds.) 2002. ''Encyclopedia of Japanese Archaeology''. Sanseidō. pp. 487–488.
Tamura, Kōichi & Aida, Yoshimasa (Supervisors) 2004. ''Basic Terminology of Archaeological Exploration''. Yamakawa Publishing. p. 74.
Yamada, Yasuhiro. 2019. ''The History of the Jōmon Period''. Kodansha Modern Library.
Tori, Ryūzō. 1924. "Stone Rods and Stone Swords." ''Suwa History,'' Volume 1. Shinano Educational Association Suwa Branch.
Harunari, Hideō. 1996. "The Archaeology of Sexual Symbols." ''Bulletin of the National Museum of Japanese History,'' Issue 66.
Okada, Shigehiro. 1960. "Regional Development of Jōmon Culture in Kinki." ''The Jōmon Period'' (Japanese Archaeology II). Kawade Shobō Shinsha.
Gotō, Shinsuke. 1999. "Research on Artifacts: Stone Rods, Stone Swords, and Stone Daggers." ''The Jōmon Period,'' Issue 10, Vol. 4. The Jōmon Period Cultural Research Society.
Taniguchi, Yasuhiro. 2006. "Stone Rods and Stone Plates: Contexts of Symbolic Reproductive Acts." ''Archaeology IV.'' Masato Anzai.
Yamamoto, Teruhisa. 1979. "Evolution of Rituals with Stone Rods (Parts 1 & 2)." ''Ancient Culture,'' Vol. 31, Issues 11–12. Association for Ancient Studies.
Hayakawa, Shoichi & Hayashi, Naoki. 2000. ''Excavation Report of the Shioya Kanesayama Shrine Site (A Area).'' Gifu Prefecture & Miyagawa Village Board of Education.
Miyoshi, Seichō & Nagata, Tomoya. 2012. ''The Shima and Shioya Kanesayama Sites.'' Hida City Board of Education.
Matsui, Takayo; Oshita, Akira; Tamura, Seiichirō. 1997. ''Mikuraoka Site II.'' Takenochō Board of Education.
Takahashi, Kiyoshi & Kondo, Nao. 2010. ''The Kamizato Site I: A Jōmon Late-Period Settlement Excavation.'' Kyoto City Archaeological Research Center Report No. 24.
Oshita, Akira. 2001. "Adoption and Transformation of Large Stone Rods in the Kinki Region (Parts 1 & 2)." ''Stone Ritual Implements in the Transition from Jōmon to Yayoi Periods III.'' Spring Research Lab, National Museum of Japanese History. == See Also ==

The largest known stone rod is the {{ill|Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|ja|北沢大石棒|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|qq=}} in [[:en:Sakuho, Nagano|Sakuho, Nagano]], measuring 223 cm in length and 25 cm in diameter.
== Overview == In a broad sense, "stone rods" encompass various rod-shaped stone artifacts, including [[stone swords]] and [[stone daggers]]. In a narrower sense, they often refer specifically to large stone rods.Tori 1924 These artifacts have been found throughout Japan, from [[Kyushu]] to [[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaidō]].
Phallic stone artifacts date back to the [[:en:Upper Paleolithic|Upper Paleolithic]] period, such as the one found at the {{ill|Masukata Site|ja|升形遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Masukata Site|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Ōami-Shirasato, Chiba|ja|大羽白里市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōami-Shirasato, Chiba|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, dated to approximately 24,000 years ago.Harunari 1996 The specific type known as "large stone rods" emerged during the Middle Jōmon period in the [[:en:Chūbu region|Chūbu region]] and later spread eastward to areas such as the [[:en:Kinki region|Kinki region]].Yamamoto 1979Okada 1960
Sites where stone rods were manufactured have been identified in eastern Japan, such as the {{ill|Shioya Kanesayama Shrine Site|ja|塩屋金清神社遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shioya Kanesayama Shrine Site|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and the {{ill|Shima Site|ja|島遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shima Site|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:Gifu Prefecture|Gifu Prefecture]],Hayakawa & Hayashi 2000Miyoshi & Nagata 2012 as well as in western Japan, such as the {{ill|Mikuraoka Site|ja|見蔵岡遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikuraoka Site|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:Hyōgo Prefecture|Hyōgo Prefecture]] and the {{ill|Kamizato Site|ja|上里遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamizato Site|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]].Matsui, Oshita & Tamura 1997Takahashi & Kondo 2011 These findings suggest that the use of stone rods as ritual objects was adopted as a cultural practice across different regions.Oshita 2001
Some stone rods have been found near the [[:en:hearths|hearths]] of dwellings, often showing signs of heat damage or discoloration. This indicates their use in fire-related [[rituals]]. Ritual practices involving stone rods are thought to symbolically represent the phases of male genital arousal, sexual activity, and post-ejaculatory flaccidity through actions such as striking, heating, and breaking the rods. The prevalence of broken stone rods in archaeological sites has led some researchers to attribute symbolic meanings to this practice, though others remain skeptical.Gotō 1999
Stone rods were also used as burial goods, suggesting multiple [[shamanistic]] or ritualistic purposes.Yamada 2019: 206Taniguchi 2006
[[File:Hinotodake Santyô.JPG|thumb|Stone rod at the summit of {{ill|Mt. Chōkaku|ja|丁岳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mt. Chōkaku|qq=}} on the border of [[:en:Akita Prefecture|Akita Prefecture]] and [[:en:Yamagata Prefecture|Yamagata Prefecture]]]]
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading ==

Tamura, Kōichi & Aida, Yoshimasa (Supervisors) 2004. ''Basic Terminology of Archaeological Exploration''. Yamakawa Publishing. p. 74.
Yamada, Yasuhiro. 2019. ''The History of the Jōmon Period''. Kodansha Modern Library.
Tori, Ryūzō. 1924. "Stone Rods and Stone Swords." ''Suwa History,'' Volume 1. Shinano Educational Association Suwa Branch.
Harunari, Hideō. 1996. "The Archaeology of Sexual Symbols." ''Bulletin of the National Museum of Japanese History,'' Issue 66.
Okada, Shigehiro. 1960. "Regional Development of Jōmon Culture in Kinki." ''The Jōmon Period'' (Japanese Archaeology II). Kawade Shobō Shinsha.
Gotō, Shinsuke. 1999. "Research on Artifacts: Stone Rods, Stone Swords, and Stone Daggers." ''The Jōmon Period,'' Issue 10, Vol. 4. The Jōmon Period Cultural Research Society.
Taniguchi, Yasuhiro. 2006. "Stone Rods and Stone Plates: Contexts of Symbolic Reproductive Acts." ''Archaeology IV.'' Masato Anzai.
Yamamoto, Teruhisa. 1979. "Evolution of Rituals with Stone Rods (Parts 1 & 2)." ''Ancient Culture,'' Vol. 31, Issues 11–12. Association for Ancient Studies.
Hayakawa, Shoichi & Hayashi, Naoki. 2000. ''Excavation Report of the Shioya Kanesayama Shrine Site (A Area).'' Gifu Prefecture & Miyagawa Village Board of Education.
Miyoshi, Seichō & Nagata, Tomoya. 2012. ''The Shima and Shioya Kanesayama Sites.'' Hida City Board of Education.
Matsui, Takayo; Oshita, Akira; Tamura, Seiichirō. 1997. ''Mikuraoka Site II.'' Takenochō Board of Education.
Takahashi, Kiyoshi & Kondo, Nao. 2010. ''The Kamizato Site I: A Jōmon Late-Period Settlement Excavation.'' Kyoto City Archaeological Research Center Report No. 24.
Oshita, Akira. 2001. "Adoption and Transformation of Large Stone Rods in the Kinki Region (Parts 1 & 2)." ''Stone Ritual Implements in the Transition from Jōmon to Yayoi Periods III.'' Spring Research Lab, National Museum of Japanese History. == See Also ==

The prevalence of broken stone rods in archaeological sites has led some researchers to attribute symbolic meanings to this practice, though others remain skeptical.Gotō 1999

In a narrower sense, they often refer specifically to large stone rods.Tori 1924 These artifacts have been found throughout Japan, from [[Kyushu]] to [[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaidō]].
Phallic stone artifacts date back to the [[:en:Upper Paleolithic|Upper Paleolithic]] period, such as the one found at the {{ill|Masukata Site|ja|升形遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Masukata Site|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Ōami-Shirasato, Chiba|ja|大羽白里市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōami-Shirasato, Chiba|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, dated to approximately 24,000 years ago.Harunari 1996 The specific type known as "large stone rods" emerged during the Middle Jōmon period in the [[:en:Chūbu region|Chūbu region]] and later spread eastward to areas such as the [[:en:Kinki region|Kinki region]].Yamamoto 1979Okada 1960
Sites where stone rods were manufactured have been identified in eastern Japan, such as the {{ill|Shioya Kanesayama Shrine Site|ja|塩屋金清神社遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shioya Kanesayama Shrine Site|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and the {{ill|Shima Site|ja|島遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shima Site|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:Gifu Prefecture|Gifu Prefecture]],Hayakawa & Hayashi 2000Miyoshi & Nagata 2012 as well as in western Japan, such as the {{ill|Mikuraoka Site|ja|見蔵岡遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikuraoka Site|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:Hyōgo Prefecture|Hyōgo Prefecture]] and the {{ill|Kamizato Site|ja|上里遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamizato Site|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]].Matsui, Oshita & Tamura 1997Takahashi & Kondo 2011 These findings suggest that the use of stone rods as ritual objects was adopted as a cultural practice across different regions.Oshita 2001
Some stone rods have been found near the [[:en:hearths|hearths]] of dwellings, often showing signs of heat damage or discoloration. This indicates their use in fire-related [[:en:Rituals|Rituals]]. Ritual practices involving stone rods are thought to symbolically represent the phases of male genital arousal, sexual activity, and post-ejaculatory flaccidity through actions such as striking, heating, and breaking the rods. The prevalence of broken stone rods in archaeological sites has led some researchers to attribute symbolic meanings to this practice, though others remain skeptical.Gotō 1999
Stone rods were also used as burial goods, suggesting multiple [[shamanistic]] or ritualistic purposes.Yamada 2019: 206Taniguchi 2006
[[File:Hinotodake Santyô.JPG|thumb|Stone rod at the summit of {{ill|Mt. Chōkaku|ja|丁岳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mt. Chōkaku|qq=}} on the border of [[:en:Akita Prefecture|Akita Prefecture]] and [[:en:Yamagata Prefecture|Yamagata Prefecture]]]]
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading ==

Yamada, Yasuhiro. 2019. ''The History of the Jōmon Period''. Kodansha Modern Library.
Tori, Ryūzō. 1924. "Stone Rods and Stone Swords." ''Suwa History,'' Volume 1. Shinano Educational Association Suwa Branch.
Harunari, Hideō. 1996. "The Archaeology of Sexual Symbols." ''Bulletin of the National Museum of Japanese History,'' Issue 66.
Okada, Shigehiro. 1960. "Regional Development of Jōmon Culture in Kinki." ''The Jōmon Period'' (Japanese Archaeology II). Kawade Shobō Shinsha.
Gotō, Shinsuke. 1999. "Research on Artifacts: Stone Rods, Stone Swords, and Stone Daggers." ''The Jōmon Period,'' Issue 10, Vol. 4. The Jōmon Period Cultural Research Society.
Taniguchi, Yasuhiro. 2006. "Stone Rods and Stone Plates: Contexts of Symbolic Reproductive Acts." ''Archaeology IV.'' Masato Anzai.
Yamamoto, Teruhisa. 1979. "Evolution of Rituals with Stone Rods (Parts 1 & 2)." ''Ancient Culture,'' Vol. 31, Issues 11–12. Association for Ancient Studies.
Hayakawa, Shoichi & Hayashi, Naoki. 2000. ''Excavation Report of the Shioya Kanesayama Shrine Site (A Area).'' Gifu Prefecture & Miyagawa Village Board of Education.
Miyoshi, Seichō & Nagata, Tomoya. 2012. ''The Shima and Shioya Kanesayama Sites.'' Hida City Board of Education.
Matsui, Takayo; Oshita, Akira; Tamura, Seiichirō. 1997. ''Mikuraoka Site II.'' Takenochō Board of Education.
Takahashi, Kiyoshi & Kondo, Nao. 2010. ''The Kamizato Site I: A Jōmon Late-Period Settlement Excavation.'' Kyoto City Archaeological Research Center Report No. 24.
Oshita, Akira. 2001. "Adoption and Transformation of Large Stone Rods in the Kinki Region (Parts 1 & 2)." ''Stone Ritual Implements in the Transition from Jōmon to Yayoi Periods III.'' Spring Research Lab, National Museum of Japanese History. == See Also ==

{{Multiple issues | refimprove = March 2016 }} [[File:Sekibou.jpg|thumb|Stone rod ({{ill|Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties|ja|奈良県立橿原考古学研究所|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties|qq=}} Museum)]] [[File:Kitazawagawa Daisekibo.jpg|thumb|The largest stone rod in Japan, the {{ill|Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|ja|北沢大石棒|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|qq=}}]] ''''Stone rods''' (せきぼう, sekibō) are a type of polished [[stone tool]] from the [[Jōmon period]] in Japan. They are believed to represent [[phalluses]] and are associated with [[:en:Rituals|Rituals]] and [[shamanistic practices]].Yamada 2019: 204
The largest known stone rod is the {{ill|Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|ja|北沢大石棒|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|qq=}} in [[:en:Sakuho, Nagano|Sakuho, Nagano]], measuring 223 cm in length and 25 cm in diameter.
== Overview == In a broad sense, "stone rods" encompass various rod-shaped stone artifacts, including [[stone swords]] and [[stone daggers]]. In a narrower sense, they often refer specifically to large stone rods.Tori 1924 These artifacts have been found throughout Japan, from [[Kyushu]] to [[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaidō]].
Phallic stone artifacts date back to the [[:en:Upper Paleolithic|Upper Paleolithic]] period, such as the one found at the {{ill|Masukata Site|ja|升形遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Masukata Site|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Ōami-Shirasato, Chiba|ja|大羽白里市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōami-Shirasato, Chiba|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, dated to approximately 24,000 years ago.Harunari 1996 The specific type known as "large stone rods" emerged during the Middle Jōmon period in the [[:en:Chūbu region|Chūbu region]] and later spread eastward to areas such as the [[:en:Kinki region|Kinki region]].Yamamoto 1979Okada 1960
Sites where stone rods were manufactured have been identified in eastern Japan, such as the {{ill|Shioya Kanesayama Shrine Site|ja|塩屋金清神社遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shioya Kanesayama Shrine Site|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and the {{ill|Shima Site|ja|島遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shima Site|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:Gifu Prefecture|Gifu Prefecture]],Hayakawa & Hayashi 2000Miyoshi & Nagata 2012 as well as in western Japan, such as the {{ill|Mikuraoka Site|ja|見蔵岡遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikuraoka Site|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:Hyōgo Prefecture|Hyōgo Prefecture]] and the {{ill|Kamizato Site|ja|上里遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamizato Site|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]].Matsui, Oshita & Tamura 1997Takahashi & Kondo 2011 These findings suggest that the use of stone rods as ritual objects was adopted as a cultural practice across different regions.Oshita 2001
Some stone rods have been found near the [[:en:hearths|hearths]] of dwellings, often showing signs of heat damage or discoloration. This indicates their use in fire-related [[:en:Rituals|Rituals]]. Ritual practices involving stone rods are thought to symbolically represent the phases of male genital arousal, sexual activity, and post-ejaculatory flaccidity through actions such as striking, heating, and breaking the rods. The prevalence of broken stone rods in archaeological sites has led some researchers to attribute symbolic meanings to this practice, though others remain skeptical.Gotō 1999
Stone rods were also used as burial goods, suggesting multiple [[:en:shamanistic|shamanistic]] or ritualistic purposes.Yamada 2019: 206Taniguchi 2006
[[File:Hinotodake Santyô.JPG|thumb|Stone rod at the summit of {{ill|Mt. Chōkaku|ja|丁岳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mt. Chōkaku|qq=}} on the border of [[:en:Akita Prefecture|Akita Prefecture]] and [[:en:Yamagata Prefecture|Yamagata Prefecture]]]]

Phallic stone artifacts date back to the [[:en:Upper Paleolithic|Upper Paleolithic]] period, such as the one found at the {{ill|Masukata Site|ja|升形遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Masukata Site|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Ōami-Shirasato, Chiba|ja|大羽白里市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōami-Shirasato, Chiba|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, dated to approximately 24,000 years ago.Harunari 1996 The specific type known as "large stone rods" emerged during the Middle Jōmon period in the [[:en:Chūbu region|Chūbu region]] and later spread eastward to areas such as the [[:en:Kinki region|Kinki region]].Yamamoto 1979Okada 1960
Sites where stone rods were manufactured have been identified in eastern Japan, such as the {{ill|Shioya Kanesayama Shrine Site|ja|塩屋金清神社遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shioya Kanesayama Shrine Site|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and the {{ill|Shima Site|ja|島遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shima Site|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:Gifu Prefecture|Gifu Prefecture]],Hayakawa & Hayashi 2000Miyoshi & Nagata 2012 as well as in western Japan, such as the {{ill|Mikuraoka Site|ja|見蔵岡遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikuraoka Site|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:Hyōgo Prefecture|Hyōgo Prefecture]] and the {{ill|Kamizato Site|ja|上里遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamizato Site|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]].Matsui, Oshita & Tamura 1997Takahashi & Kondo 2011 These findings suggest that the use of stone rods as ritual objects was adopted as a cultural practice across different regions.Oshita 2001
Some stone rods have been found near the [[:en:hearths|hearths]] of dwellings, often showing signs of heat damage or discoloration. This indicates their use in fire-related [[:en:Rituals|Rituals]]. Ritual practices involving stone rods are thought to symbolically represent the phases of male genital arousal, sexual activity, and post-ejaculatory flaccidity through actions such as striking, heating, and breaking the rods. The prevalence of broken stone rods in archaeological sites has led some researchers to attribute symbolic meanings to this practice, though others remain skeptical.Gotō 1999
Stone rods were also used as burial goods, suggesting multiple [[:en:shamanistic|shamanistic]] or ritualistic purposes.Yamada 2019: 206Taniguchi 2006
[[File:Hinotodake Santyô.JPG|thumb|Stone rod at the summit of {{ill|Mt. Chōkaku|ja|丁岳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mt. Chōkaku|qq=}} on the border of [[:en:Akita Prefecture|Akita Prefecture]] and [[:en:Yamagata Prefecture|Yamagata Prefecture]]]]

Harunari, Hideō. 1996. "The Archaeology of Sexual Symbols." ''Bulletin of the National Museum of Japanese History,'' Issue 66.
Okada, Shigehiro. 1960. "Regional Development of Jōmon Culture in Kinki." ''The Jōmon Period'' (Japanese Archaeology II). Kawade Shobō Shinsha.
Gotō, Shinsuke. 1999. "Research on Artifacts: Stone Rods, Stone Swords, and Stone Daggers." ''The Jōmon Period,'' Issue 10, Vol. 4. The Jōmon Period Cultural Research Society.
Taniguchi, Yasuhiro. 2006. "Stone Rods and Stone Plates: Contexts of Symbolic Reproductive Acts." ''Archaeology IV.'' Masato Anzai.

Oshita, Akira. 2001. "Adoption and Transformation of Large Stone Rods in the Kinki Region (Parts 1 & 2)." ''Stone Ritual Implements in the Transition from Jōmon to Yayoi Periods III.'' Spring Research Lab, National Museum of Japanese History. == See Also ==
