{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q17994957]] Wikidata name: sekibō [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:09, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Multiple issues | refimprove = March 2016 }} [[File:Sekibou.jpg|thumb|Stone rod ({{ill|Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties|ja|奈良県立橿原考古学研究所|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties|qq=}} Museum)]] [[File:Kitazawagawa Daisekibo.jpg|thumb|The largest stone rod in Japan, the {{ill|Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|ja|北沢大石棒|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|qq=}}]] ''''Stone rods''' (せきぼう, sekibō) are a type of polished [[:en:Stone tool|Stone tool]] from the [[Jōmon period]] in Japan. They are believed to represent [[phalluses]] and are associated with [[:en:Rituals|Rituals]] and [[shamanistic practices]].Yamada 2019: 204
The largest known stone rod is the {{ill|Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|ja|北沢大石棒|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|qq=}} in [[:en:Sakuho, Nagano|Sakuho, Nagano]], measuring 223 cm in length and 25 cm in diameter.
== Overview == In a broad sense, "stone rods" encompass various rod-shaped stone artifacts, including [[stone swords]] and [[stone daggers]]. In a narrower sense, they often refer specifically to large stone rods.Tori 1924 These artifacts have been found throughout Japan, from [[Kyushu]] to [[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaidō]].
Phallic stone artifacts date back to the [[:en:Upper Paleolithic|Upper Paleolithic]] period, such as the one found at the {{ill|Masukata Site|ja|升形遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Masukata Site|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Ōami-Shirasato, Chiba|ja|大羽白里市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōami-Shirasato, Chiba|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, dated to approximately 24,000 years ago.Harunari 1996 The specific type known as "large stone rods" emerged during the Middle Jōmon period in the [[:en:Chūbu region|Chūbu region]] and later spread eastward to areas such as the [[:en:Kinki region|Kinki region]].Yamamoto 1979Okada 1960
Sites where stone rods were manufactured have been identified in eastern Japan, such as the {{ill|Shioya Kanesayama Shrine Site|ja|塩屋金清神社遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shioya Kanesayama Shrine Site|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and the {{ill|Shima Site|ja|島遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shima Site|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:Gifu Prefecture|Gifu Prefecture]],Hayakawa & Hayashi 2000Miyoshi & Nagata 2012 as well as in western Japan, such as the {{ill|Mikuraoka Site|ja|見蔵岡遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikuraoka Site|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:Hyōgo Prefecture|Hyōgo Prefecture]] and the {{ill|Kamizato Site|ja|上里遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamizato Site|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]].Matsui, Oshita & Tamura 1997Takahashi & Kondo 2011 These findings suggest that the use of stone rods as ritual objects was adopted as a cultural practice across different regions.Oshita 2001
Some stone rods have been found near the [[:en:hearths|hearths]] of dwellings, often showing signs of heat damage or discoloration. This indicates their use in fire-related [[:en:Rituals|Rituals]]. Ritual practices involving stone rods are thought to symbolically represent the phases of male genital arousal, sexual activity, and post-ejaculatory flaccidity through actions such as striking, heating, and breaking the rods. The prevalence of broken stone rods in archaeological sites has led some researchers to attribute symbolic meanings to this practice, though others remain skeptical.Gotō 1999
Stone rods were also used as burial goods, suggesting multiple [[:en:shamanistic|shamanistic]] or ritualistic purposes.Yamada 2019: 206Taniguchi 2006
[[File:Hinotodake Santyô.JPG|thumb|Stone rod at the summit of {{ill|Mt. Chōkaku|ja|丁岳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mt. Chōkaku|qq=}} on the border of [[:en:Akita Prefecture|Akita Prefecture]] and [[:en:Yamagata Prefecture|Yamagata Prefecture]]]]

Yamamoto, Teruhisa. 1979. "Evolution of Rituals with Stone Rods (Parts 1 & 2)." ''Ancient Culture,'' Vol. 31, Issues 11–12. Association for Ancient Studies.
Hayakawa, Shoichi & Hayashi, Naoki. 2000. ''Excavation Report of the Shioya Kanesayama Shrine Site (A Area).'' Gifu Prefecture & Miyagawa Village Board of Education.

In a narrower sense, they often refer specifically to large stone rods.Tori 1924 These artifacts have been found throughout Japan, from [[Kyushu]] to [[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaidō]].
Phallic stone artifacts date back to the [[:en:Upper Paleolithic|Upper Paleolithic]] period, such as the one found at the {{ill|Masukata Site|ja|升形遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Masukata Site|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Ōami-Shirasato, Chiba|ja|大羽白里市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōami-Shirasato, Chiba|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, dated to approximately 24,000 years ago.Harunari 1996 The specific type known as "large stone rods" emerged during the Middle Jōmon period in the [[:en:Chūbu region|Chūbu region]] and later spread eastward to areas such as the [[:en:Kinki region|Kinki region]].Yamamoto 1979Okada 1960
Sites where stone rods were manufactured have been identified in eastern Japan, such as the {{ill|Shioya Kanesayama Shrine Site|ja|塩屋金清神社遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shioya Kanesayama Shrine Site|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and the {{ill|Shima Site|ja|島遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shima Site|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:Gifu Prefecture|Gifu Prefecture]],Hayakawa & Hayashi 2000Miyoshi & Nagata 2012 as well as in western Japan, such as the {{ill|Mikuraoka Site|ja|見蔵岡遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikuraoka Site|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:Hyōgo Prefecture|Hyōgo Prefecture]] and the {{ill|Kamizato Site|ja|上里遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamizato Site|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]].Matsui, Oshita & Tamura 1997Takahashi & Kondo 2011 These findings suggest that the use of stone rods as ritual objects was adopted as a cultural practice across different regions.Oshita 2001
Some stone rods have been found near the [[:en:hearths|hearths]] of dwellings, often showing signs of heat damage or discoloration. This indicates their use in fire-related [[:en:Rituals|Rituals]]. Ritual practices involving stone rods are thought to symbolically represent the phases of male genital arousal, sexual activity, and post-ejaculatory flaccidity through actions such as striking, heating, and breaking the rods. The prevalence of broken stone rods in archaeological sites has led some researchers to attribute symbolic meanings to this practice, though others remain skeptical.Gotō 1999
Stone rods were also used as burial goods, suggesting multiple [[:en:shamanistic|shamanistic]] or ritualistic purposes.Yamada 2019: 206Taniguchi 2006
[[File:Hinotodake Santyô.JPG|thumb|Stone rod at the summit of {{ill|Mt. Chōkaku|ja|丁岳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mt. Chōkaku|qq=|1=Hinoto Dake|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18338992}} on the border of [[:en:Akita Prefecture|Akita Prefecture]] and [[:en:Yamagata Prefecture|Yamagata Prefecture]]]]
== References == {{Reflist}}

Miyoshi, Seichō & Nagata, Tomoya. 2012. ''The Shima and Shioya Kanesayama Sites.'' Hida City Board of Education.

Oshita, Akira. 2001. "Adoption and Transformation of Large Stone Rods in the Kinki Region (Parts 1 & 2)." ''Stone Ritual Implements in the Transition from Jōmon to Yayoi Periods III.'' Spring Research Lab, National Museum of Japanese History. == See Also ==

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q17994957]] Wikidata name: sekibō [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:09, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Multiple issues | refimprove = March 2016 }} [[File:Sekibou.jpg|thumb|Stone rod ({{ill|Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties|ja|奈良県立橿原考古学研究所|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties|qq=|1=Archaeological Institute of Kashihara, Nara Prefecture|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11445218}} Museum)]] [[File:Kitazawagawa Daisekibo.jpg|thumb|The largest stone rod in Japan, the {{ill|Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|ja|北沢大石棒|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|qq=|1=Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18457535}}]] ''''Stone rods''' (せきぼう, sekibō) are a type of polished [[:en:Stone tool|Stone tool]] from the [[Jōmon period]] in Japan. They are believed to represent [[phalluses]] and are associated with [[:en:Rituals|Rituals]] and [[shamanistic practices]].Yamada 2019: 204
The largest known stone rod is the {{ill|Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|ja|北沢大石棒|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|qq=|1=Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18457535}} in [[:en:Sakuho, Nagano|Sakuho, Nagano]], measuring 223 cm in length and 25 cm in diameter.
== Overview == In a broad sense, "stone rods" encompass various rod-shaped stone artifacts, including [[stone swords]] and [[stone daggers]]. In a narrower sense, they often refer specifically to large stone rods.Tori 1924 These artifacts have been found throughout Japan, from [[Kyushu]] to [[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaidō]].
Phallic stone artifacts date back to the [[:en:Upper Paleolithic|Upper Paleolithic]] period, such as the one found at the {{ill|Masukata Site|ja|升形遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Masukata Site|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Ōami-Shirasato, Chiba|ja|大羽白里市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōami-Shirasato, Chiba|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, dated to approximately 24,000 years ago.Harunari 1996 The specific type known as "large stone rods" emerged during the Middle Jōmon period in the [[:en:Chūbu region|Chūbu region]] and later spread eastward to areas such as the [[:en:Kinki region|Kinki region]].Yamamoto 1979Okada 1960
Sites where stone rods were manufactured have been identified in eastern Japan, such as the {{ill|Shioya Kanesayama Shrine Site|ja|塩屋金清神社遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shioya Kanesayama Shrine Site|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and the {{ill|Shima Site|ja|島遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shima Site|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:Gifu Prefecture|Gifu Prefecture]],Hayakawa & Hayashi 2000Miyoshi & Nagata 2012 as well as in western Japan, such as the {{ill|Mikuraoka Site|ja|見蔵岡遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikuraoka Site|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:Hyōgo Prefecture|Hyōgo Prefecture]] and the {{ill|Kamizato Site|ja|上里遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamizato Site|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]].Matsui, Oshita & Tamura 1997Takahashi & Kondo 2011 These findings suggest that the use of stone rods as ritual objects was adopted as a cultural practice across different regions.Oshita 2001
Some stone rods have been found near the [[:en:hearths|hearths]] of dwellings, often showing signs of heat damage or discoloration. This indicates their use in fire-related [[:en:Rituals|Rituals]]. Ritual practices involving stone rods are thought to symbolically represent the phases of male genital arousal, sexual activity, and post-ejaculatory flaccidity through actions such as striking, heating, and breaking the rods. The prevalence of broken stone rods in archaeological sites has led some researchers to attribute symbolic meanings to this practice, though others remain skeptical.Gotō 1999
Stone rods were also used as burial goods, suggesting multiple [[:en:shamanistic|shamanistic]] or ritualistic purposes.Yamada 2019: 206Taniguchi 2006
[[File:Hinotodake Santyô.JPG|thumb|Stone rod at the summit of {{ill|Mt. Chōkaku|ja|丁岳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mt. Chōkaku|qq=|1=Hinoto Dake|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18338992}} on the border of [[:en:Akita Prefecture|Akita Prefecture]] and [[:en:Yamagata Prefecture|Yamagata Prefecture]]]]

The largest known stone rod is the {{ill|Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|ja|北沢大石棒|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|qq=|1=Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18457535}} in [[:en:Sakuho, Nagano|Sakuho, Nagano]], measuring 223 cm in length and 25 cm in diameter.
== Overview == In a broad sense, "stone rods" encompass various rod-shaped stone artifacts, including [[stone swords]] and [[stone daggers]]. In a narrower sense, they often refer specifically to large stone rods.Tori 1924 These artifacts have been found throughout Japan, from [[Kyushu]] to [[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaidō]].
Phallic stone artifacts date back to the [[:en:Upper Paleolithic|Upper Paleolithic]] period, such as the one found at the {{ill|Masukata Site|ja|升形遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Masukata Site|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Ōami-Shirasato, Chiba|ja|大羽白里市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōami-Shirasato, Chiba|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, dated to approximately 24,000 years ago.Harunari 1996 The specific type known as "large stone rods" emerged during the Middle Jōmon period in the [[:en:Chūbu region|Chūbu region]] and later spread eastward to areas such as the [[:en:Kinki region|Kinki region]].Yamamoto 1979Okada 1960
Sites where stone rods were manufactured have been identified in eastern Japan, such as the {{ill|Shioya Kanesayama Shrine Site|ja|塩屋金清神社遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shioya Kanesayama Shrine Site|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and the {{ill|Shima Site|ja|島遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shima Site|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:Gifu Prefecture|Gifu Prefecture]],Hayakawa & Hayashi 2000Miyoshi & Nagata 2012 as well as in western Japan, such as the {{ill|Mikuraoka Site|ja|見蔵岡遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikuraoka Site|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:Hyōgo Prefecture|Hyōgo Prefecture]] and the {{ill|Kamizato Site|ja|上里遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamizato Site|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]].Matsui, Oshita & Tamura 1997Takahashi & Kondo 2011 These findings suggest that the use of stone rods as ritual objects was adopted as a cultural practice across different regions.Oshita 2001
Some stone rods have been found near the [[:en:hearths|hearths]] of dwellings, often showing signs of heat damage or discoloration. This indicates their use in fire-related [[:en:Rituals|Rituals]]. Ritual practices involving stone rods are thought to symbolically represent the phases of male genital arousal, sexual activity, and post-ejaculatory flaccidity through actions such as striking, heating, and breaking the rods. The prevalence of broken stone rods in archaeological sites has led some researchers to attribute symbolic meanings to this practice, though others remain skeptical.Gotō 1999
Stone rods were also used as burial goods, suggesting multiple [[:en:shamanistic|shamanistic]] or ritualistic purposes.Yamada 2019: 206Taniguchi 2006
[[File:Hinotodake Santyô.JPG|thumb|Stone rod at the summit of {{ill|Mt. Chōkaku|ja|丁岳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mt. Chōkaku|qq=|1=Hinoto Dake|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18338992}} on the border of [[:en:Akita Prefecture|Akita Prefecture]] and [[:en:Yamagata Prefecture|Yamagata Prefecture]]]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Sekibō]][[en:Sekibō]][[ja:石棒]][[pl:Sekibō]][[ru:Сэкибо]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q17994957]] Wikidata name: sekibō [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:09, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Multiple issues | refimprove = March 2016 }} [[File:Sekibou.jpg|thumb|Stone rod ({{ill|Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties|ja|奈良県立橿原考古学研究所|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties|qq=|1=Archaeological Institute of Kashihara, Nara Prefecture|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11445218}} Museum)]] [[File:Kitazawagawa Daisekibo.jpg|thumb|The largest stone rod in Japan, the {{ill|Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|ja|北沢大石棒|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|qq=|1=Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18457535}}]] ''''Stone rods''' (せきぼう, sekibō) are a type of polished [[:en:Stone tool|Stone tool]] from the [[Jōmon period]] in Japan. <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Sekibō]][[en:Sekibō]][[ja:石棒]][[pl:Sekibō]][[ru:Сэкибо]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q17994957]] Wikidata name: sekibō [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:09, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Multiple issues | refimprove = March 2016 }} [[File:Sekibou.jpg|thumb|Stone rod ({{ill|Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties|ja|奈良県立橿原考古学研究所|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties|1=Archaeological Institute of Kashihara, Nara Prefecture|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11445218}} Museum)]] [[File:Kitazawagawa Daisekibo.jpg|thumb|The largest stone rod in Japan, the {{ill|Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|ja|北沢大石棒|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|1=Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18457535}}]] ''''Stone rods''' (せきぼう, sekibō) are a type of polished [[:en:Stone tool|Stone tool]] from the [[Jōmon period]] in Japan. They are believed to represent [[phalluses]] and are associated with [[:en:Rituals|Rituals]] and [[shamanistic practices]].Yamada 2019: 204
The largest known stone rod is the {{ill|Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|ja|北沢大石棒|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|1=Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18457535}} in [[:en:Sakuho, Nagano|Sakuho, Nagano]], measuring 223 cm in length and 25 cm in diameter.
== Overview == In a broad sense, "stone rods" encompass various rod-shaped stone artifacts, including [[stone swords]] and [[stone daggers]]. In a narrower sense, they often refer specifically to large stone rods.Tori 1924 These artifacts have been found throughout Japan, from [[Kyushu]] to [[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaidō]].
Phallic stone artifacts date back to the [[:en:Upper Paleolithic|Upper Paleolithic]] period, such as the one found at the {{ill|Masukata Site|ja|升形遺跡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Masukata Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Ōami-Shirasato, Chiba|ja|大羽白里市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōami-Shirasato, Chiba|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, dated to approximately 24,000 years ago.Harunari 1996 The specific type known as "large stone rods" emerged during the Middle Jōmon period in the [[:en:Chūbu region|Chūbu region]] and later spread eastward to areas such as the [[:en:Kinki region|Kinki region]].Yamamoto 1979Okada 1960
Sites where stone rods were manufactured have been identified in eastern Japan, such as the {{ill|Shioya Kanesayama Shrine Site|ja|塩屋金清神社遺跡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shioya Kanesayama Shrine Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and the {{ill|Shima Site|ja|島遺跡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shima Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:Gifu Prefecture|Gifu Prefecture]],Hayakawa & Hayashi 2000Miyoshi & Nagata 2012 as well as in western Japan, such as the {{ill|Mikuraoka Site|ja|見蔵岡遺跡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikuraoka Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:Hyōgo Prefecture|Hyōgo Prefecture]] and the {{ill|Kamizato Site|ja|上里遺跡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamizato Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]].Matsui, Oshita & Tamura 1997Takahashi & Kondo 2011 These findings suggest that the use of stone rods as ritual objects was adopted as a cultural practice across different regions.Oshita 2001
Some stone rods have been found near the [[:en:hearths|hearths]] of dwellings, often showing signs of heat damage or discoloration. This indicates their use in fire-related [[:en:Rituals|Rituals]]. Ritual practices involving stone rods are thought to symbolically represent the phases of male genital arousal, sexual activity, and post-ejaculatory flaccidity through actions such as striking, heating, and breaking the rods. The prevalence of broken stone rods in archaeological sites has led some researchers to attribute symbolic meanings to this practice, though others remain skeptical.Gotō 1999
Stone rods were also used as burial goods, suggesting multiple [[:en:shamanistic|shamanistic]] or ritualistic purposes.Yamada 2019: 206Taniguchi 2006
[[File:Hinotodake Santyô.JPG|thumb|Stone rod at the summit of {{ill|Mt. Chōkaku|ja|丁岳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mt. Chōkaku|1=Hinoto Dake|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18338992}} on the border of [[:en:Akita Prefecture|Akita Prefecture]] and [[:en:Yamagata Prefecture|Yamagata Prefecture]]]]
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading ==
Tanaka, Taku & Sahara, Makoto (Eds.) 2002. ''Encyclopedia of Japanese Archaeology''. Sanseidō. pp. 487–488.
Tamura, Kōichi & Aida, Yoshimasa (Supervisors) 2004. ''Basic Terminology of Archaeological Exploration''. Yamakawa Publishing. p. 74.
Yamada, Yasuhiro. 2019. ''The History of the Jōmon Period''. Kodansha Modern Library.
Tori, Ryūzō. 1924. "Stone Rods and Stone Swords." ''Suwa History,'' Volume 1. Shinano Educational Association Suwa Branch.
Harunari, Hideō. 1996. "The Archaeology of Sexual Symbols." ''Bulletin of the National Museum of Japanese History,'' Issue 66.
Okada, Shigehiro. 1960. "Regional Development of Jōmon Culture in Kinki." ''The Jōmon Period'' (Japanese Archaeology II). Kawade Shobō Shinsha.
Gotō, Shinsuke. 1999. "Research on Artifacts: Stone Rods, Stone Swords, and Stone Daggers." ''The Jōmon Period,'' Issue 10, Vol. 4. The Jōmon Period Cultural Research Society.
Taniguchi, Yasuhiro. 2006. "Stone Rods and Stone Plates: Contexts of Symbolic Reproductive Acts." ''Archaeology IV.'' Masato Anzai.
Yamamoto, Teruhisa. 1979. "Evolution of Rituals with Stone Rods (Parts 1 & 2)." ''Ancient Culture,'' Vol. 31, Issues 11–12. Association for Ancient Studies.
Hayakawa, Shoichi & Hayashi, Naoki. 2000. ''Excavation Report of the Shioya Kanesayama Shrine Site (A Area).'' Gifu Prefecture & Miyagawa Village Board of Education.
Miyoshi, Seichō & Nagata, Tomoya. 2012. ''The Shima and Shioya Kanesayama Sites.'' Hida City Board of Education.
Matsui, Takayo; Oshita, Akira; Tamura, Seiichirō. 1997. ''Mikuraoka Site II.'' Takenochō Board of Education.
Takahashi, Kiyoshi & Kondo, Nao. 2010. ''The Kamizato Site I: A Jōmon Late-Period Settlement Excavation.'' Kyoto City Archaeological Research Center Report No. 24.
Oshita, Akira. 2001. "Adoption and Transformation of Large Stone Rods in the Kinki Region (Parts 1 & 2)." ''Stone Ritual Implements in the Transition from Jōmon to Yayoi Periods III.'' Spring Research Lab, National Museum of Japanese History. == See Also ==

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Sekibō]][[en:Sekibō]][[ja:石棒]][[pl:Sekibō]][[ru:Сэкибо]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q17994957]] Wikidata name: sekibō [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:09, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Multiple issues | refimprove = March 2016 }} [[File:Sekibou.jpg|thumb|Stone rod ({{ill|Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties|ja|奈良県立橿原考古学研究所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties|1=Archaeological Institute of Kashihara, Nara Prefecture|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11445218}} Museum)]] [[File:Kitazawagawa Daisekibo.jpg|thumb|The largest stone rod in Japan, the {{ill|Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|ja|北沢大石棒|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|1=Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18457535}}]] ''''Stone rods''' (せきぼう, sekibō) are a type of polished [[:en:Stone tool|Stone tool]] from the [[Jōmon period]] in Japan. They are believed to represent [[phalluses]] and are associated with [[:en:Rituals|Rituals]] and [[shamanistic practices]].Yamada 2019: 204
The largest known stone rod is the {{ill|Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|ja|北沢大石棒|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|1=Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18457535}} in [[:en:Sakuho, Nagano|Sakuho, Nagano]], measuring 223 cm in length and 25 cm in diameter.
== Overview == In a broad sense, "stone rods" encompass various rod-shaped stone artifacts, including [[stone swords]] and [[stone daggers]]. In a narrower sense, they often refer specifically to large stone rods.Tori 1924 These artifacts have been found throughout Japan, from [[Kyushu]] to [[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaidō]].
Phallic stone artifacts date back to the [[:en:Upper Paleolithic|Upper Paleolithic]] period, such as the one found at the {{ill|Masukata Site|ja|升形遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Masukata Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Ōami-Shirasato, Chiba|ja|大羽白里市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōami-Shirasato, Chiba|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, dated to approximately 24,000 years ago.Harunari 1996 The specific type known as "large stone rods" emerged during the Middle Jōmon period in the [[:en:Chūbu region|Chūbu region]] and later spread eastward to areas such as the [[:en:Kinki region|Kinki region]].Yamamoto 1979Okada 1960
Sites where stone rods were manufactured have been identified in eastern Japan, such as the {{ill|Shioya Kanesayama Shrine Site|ja|塩屋金清神社遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shioya Kanesayama Shrine Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and the {{ill|Shima Site|ja|島遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shima Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:Gifu Prefecture|Gifu Prefecture]],Hayakawa & Hayashi 2000Miyoshi & Nagata 2012 as well as in western Japan, such as the {{ill|Mikuraoka Site|ja|見蔵岡遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikuraoka Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:Hyōgo Prefecture|Hyōgo Prefecture]] and the {{ill|Kamizato Site|ja|上里遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamizato Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]].Matsui, Oshita & Tamura 1997Takahashi & Kondo 2011 These findings suggest that the use of stone rods as ritual objects was adopted as a cultural practice across different regions.Oshita 2001
Some stone rods have been found near the [[:en:hearths|hearths]] of dwellings, often showing signs of heat damage or discoloration. This indicates their use in fire-related [[:en:Rituals|Rituals]]. Ritual practices involving stone rods are thought to symbolically represent the phases of male genital arousal, sexual activity, and post-ejaculatory flaccidity through actions such as striking, heating, and breaking the rods. The prevalence of broken stone rods in archaeological sites has led some researchers to attribute symbolic meanings to this practice, though others remain skeptical.Gotō 1999
Stone rods were also used as burial goods, suggesting multiple [[:en:shamanistic|shamanistic]] or ritualistic purposes.Yamada 2019: 206Taniguchi 2006
[[File:Hinotodake Santyô.JPG|thumb|Stone rod at the summit of {{ill|Mt. Chōkaku|ja|丁岳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mt. Chōkaku|1=Hinoto Dake|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18338992}} on the border of [[:en:Akita Prefecture|Akita Prefecture]] and [[:en:Yamagata Prefecture|Yamagata Prefecture]]]]
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading ==
Tanaka, Taku & Sahara, Makoto (Eds.) 2002. ''Encyclopedia of Japanese Archaeology''. Sanseidō. pp. 487–488.
Tamura, Kōichi & Aida, Yoshimasa (Supervisors) 2004. ''Basic Terminology of Archaeological Exploration''. Yamakawa Publishing. p. 74.
Yamada, Yasuhiro. 2019. ''The History of the Jōmon Period''. Kodansha Modern Library.
Tori, Ryūzō. 1924. "Stone Rods and Stone Swords." ''Suwa History,'' Volume 1. Shinano Educational Association Suwa Branch.
Harunari, Hideō. 1996. "The Archaeology of Sexual Symbols." ''Bulletin of the National Museum of Japanese History,'' Issue 66.
Okada, Shigehiro. 1960. "Regional Development of Jōmon Culture in Kinki." ''The Jōmon Period'' (Japanese Archaeology II). Kawade Shobō Shinsha.
Gotō, Shinsuke. 1999. "Research on Artifacts: Stone Rods, Stone Swords, and Stone Daggers." ''The Jōmon Period,'' Issue 10, Vol. 4. The Jōmon Period Cultural Research Society.
Taniguchi, Yasuhiro. 2006. "Stone Rods and Stone Plates: Contexts of Symbolic Reproductive Acts." ''Archaeology IV.'' Masato Anzai.
Yamamoto, Teruhisa. 1979. "Evolution of Rituals with Stone Rods (Parts 1 & 2)." ''Ancient Culture,'' Vol. 31, Issues 11–12. Association for Ancient Studies.
Hayakawa, Shoichi & Hayashi, Naoki. 2000. ''Excavation Report of the Shioya Kanesayama Shrine Site (A Area).'' Gifu Prefecture & Miyagawa Village Board of Education.
Miyoshi, Seichō & Nagata, Tomoya. 2012. ''The Shima and Shioya Kanesayama Sites.'' Hida City Board of Education.
Matsui, Takayo; Oshita, Akira; Tamura, Seiichirō. 1997. ''Mikuraoka Site II.'' Takenochō Board of Education.
Takahashi, Kiyoshi & Kondo, Nao. 2010. ''The Kamizato Site I: A Jōmon Late-Period Settlement Excavation.'' Kyoto City Archaeological Research Center Report No. 24.
Oshita, Akira. 2001. "Adoption and Transformation of Large Stone Rods in the Kinki Region (Parts 1 & 2)." ''Stone Ritual Implements in the Transition from Jōmon to Yayoi Periods III.'' Spring Research Lab, National Museum of Japanese History. == See Also ==
[[Stone sword]]
[[:en:Stone tools|Stone tools]]
[[:en:Kin|Kin]]
[[:en:Kōjin|Kōjin]]
[[:en:Vajra|Vajra]]
[[Mara Stone|Phallic stone of Asuka]]
[[Mishaguji]] — An ancient belief system originating in the Jōmon period, centered around stone rods and trees as sacred objects, prevalent in [[:en:Suwa, Nagano|Suwa]] and surrounding regions.
[[:en:Snake deity|Snake deity]]
== External Links ==
[http://www.tamagawa.ac.jp/museum/archive/2001/119_1.html Stone rods at Tamagawa University Education Museum]
[http://www.city.kunitachi.tokyo.jp/dbps_data/material/localhost/100000/101000/pdf/2012.07.10midorikawahigasiiseki.pdf Stone rods discovered at the Midorikawa East Site (PDF) – Kunitachi City]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Sekibō]][[en:Sekibō]][[ja:石棒]][[pl:Sekibō]][[ru:Сэкибо]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q17994957]] Wikidata name: sekibō [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:09, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Multiple issues | refimprove = March 2016 }} [[File:Sekibou.jpg|thumb|Stone rod ({{ill|Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties|ja|奈良県立橿原考古学研究所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties|1=Archaeological Institute of Kashihara, Nara Prefecture|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11445218}} Museum)]] [[File:Kitazawagawa Daisekibo.jpg|thumb|The largest stone rod in Japan, the {{ill|Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|ja|北沢大石棒|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|1=Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18457535}}]] ''''Stone rods''' (せきぼう, sekibō) are a type of polished [[:en:Stone tool|Stone tool]] from the [[Jōmon period]] in Japan. They are believed to represent [[phalluses]] and are associated with [[:en:Rituals|Rituals]] and [[shamanistic practices]].Yamada 2019: 204
The largest known stone rod is the {{ill|Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|ja|北沢大石棒|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|1=Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18457535}} in [[:en:Sakuho, Nagano|Sakuho, Nagano]], measuring 223 cm in length and 25 cm in diameter.
== Overview == In a broad sense, "stone rods" encompass various rod-shaped stone artifacts, including [[stone swords]] and [[stone daggers]]. In a narrower sense, they often refer specifically to large stone rods.Tori 1924 These artifacts have been found throughout Japan, from [[Kyushu]] to [[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaidō]].
Phallic stone artifacts date back to the [[:en:Upper Paleolithic|Upper Paleolithic]] period, such as the one found at the {{ill|Masukata Site|ja|升形遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Masukata Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Ōami-Shirasato, Chiba|ja|大羽白里市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōami-Shirasato, Chiba|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, dated to approximately 24,000 years ago.Harunari 1996 The specific type known as "large stone rods" emerged during the Middle Jōmon period in the [[:en:Chūbu region|Chūbu region]] and later spread eastward to areas such as the [[:en:Kinki region|Kinki region]].Yamamoto 1979Okada 1960
Sites where stone rods were manufactured have been identified in eastern Japan, such as the {{ill|Shioya Kanesayama Shrine Site|ja|塩屋金清神社遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shioya Kanesayama Shrine Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and the {{ill|Shima Site|ja|島遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shima Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:Gifu Prefecture|Gifu Prefecture]],Hayakawa & Hayashi 2000Miyoshi & Nagata 2012 as well as in western Japan, such as the {{ill|Mikuraoka Site|ja|見蔵岡遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikuraoka Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:Hyōgo Prefecture|Hyōgo Prefecture]] and the {{ill|Kamizato Site|ja|上里遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamizato Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]].Matsui, Oshita & Tamura 1997Takahashi & Kondo 2011 These findings suggest that the use of stone rods as ritual objects was adopted as a cultural practice across different regions.Oshita 2001
Some stone rods have been found near the [[:en:hearths|hearths]] of dwellings, often showing signs of heat damage or discoloration. This indicates their use in fire-related [[:en:Rituals|Rituals]]. Ritual practices involving stone rods are thought to symbolically represent the phases of male genital arousal, sexual activity, and post-ejaculatory flaccidity through actions such as striking, heating, and breaking the rods. The prevalence of broken stone rods in archaeological sites has led some researchers to attribute symbolic meanings to this practice, though others remain skeptical.Gotō 1999
Stone rods were also used as burial goods, suggesting multiple [[:en:shamanistic|shamanistic]] or ritualistic purposes.Yamada 2019: 206Taniguchi 2006
[[File:Hinotodake Santyô.JPG|thumb|Stone rod at the summit of {{ill|Mt. Chōkaku|ja|丁岳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mt. Chōkaku|1=Hinoto Dake|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18338992}} on the border of [[:en:Akita Prefecture|Akita Prefecture]] and [[:en:Yamagata Prefecture|Yamagata Prefecture]]]]
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading ==
Tanaka, Taku & Sahara, Makoto (Eds.) 2002. ''Encyclopedia of Japanese Archaeology''. Sanseidō. pp. 487–488.
Tamura, Kōichi & Aida, Yoshimasa (Supervisors) 2004. ''Basic Terminology of Archaeological Exploration''. Yamakawa Publishing. p. 74.
Yamada, Yasuhiro. 2019. ''The History of the Jōmon Period''. Kodansha Modern Library.
Tori, Ryūzō. 1924. "Stone Rods and Stone Swords." ''Suwa History,'' Volume 1. Shinano Educational Association Suwa Branch.
Harunari, Hideō. 1996. "The Archaeology of Sexual Symbols." ''Bulletin of the National Museum of Japanese History,'' Issue 66.
Okada, Shigehiro. 1960. "Regional Development of Jōmon Culture in Kinki." ''The Jōmon Period'' (Japanese Archaeology II). Kawade Shobō Shinsha.
Gotō, Shinsuke. 1999. "Research on Artifacts: Stone Rods, Stone Swords, and Stone Daggers." ''The Jōmon Period,'' Issue 10, Vol. 4. The Jōmon Period Cultural Research Society.
Taniguchi, Yasuhiro. 2006. "Stone Rods and Stone Plates: Contexts of Symbolic Reproductive Acts." ''Archaeology IV.'' Masato Anzai.
Yamamoto, Teruhisa. 1979. "Evolution of Rituals with Stone Rods (Parts 1 & 2)." ''Ancient Culture,'' Vol. 31, Issues 11–12. Association for Ancient Studies.
Hayakawa, Shoichi & Hayashi, Naoki. 2000. ''Excavation Report of the Shioya Kanesayama Shrine Site (A Area).'' Gifu Prefecture & Miyagawa Village Board of Education.
Miyoshi, Seichō & Nagata, Tomoya. 2012. ''The Shima and Shioya Kanesayama Sites.'' Hida City Board of Education.
Matsui, Takayo; Oshita, Akira; Tamura, Seiichirō. 1997. ''Mikuraoka Site II.'' Takenochō Board of Education.
Takahashi, Kiyoshi & Kondo, Nao. 2010. ''The Kamizato Site I: A Jōmon Late-Period Settlement Excavation.'' Kyoto City Archaeological Research Center Report No. 24.
Oshita, Akira. 2001. "Adoption and Transformation of Large Stone Rods in the Kinki Region (Parts 1 & 2)." ''Stone Ritual Implements in the Transition from Jōmon to Yayoi Periods III.'' Spring Research Lab, National Museum of Japanese History. == See Also ==

[[Mishaguji]] — An ancient belief system originating in the Jōmon period, centered around stone rods and trees as sacred objects, prevalent in [[:en:Suwa, Nagano|Suwa]] and surrounding regions.
[[:en:Snake deity|Snake deity]]

<!--Ishibou--><!--Ishibou--> [[Category:Sexuality and religion]] [[Category:Lithics]] [[Category:Festival Utensils]] [[Category:translated pages with valid en interwikis]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{Commons category|Sekibō}} <!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{Commons category|Sekibō}} {{wikidata link|Q17994957}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Sekibō]][[en:Sekibō]][[ja:石棒]][[pl:Sekibō]][[ru:Сэкибо]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Sekibō]][[en:Sekibō]][[ja:石棒]][[pl:Sekibō]][[ru:Сэкибо]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q17994957]] Wikidata name: sekibō [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:09, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Multiple issues | refimprove = March 2016 }} [[File:Sekibou.jpg|thumb|Stone rod ({{ill|Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties|ja|奈良県立橿原考古学研究所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties|1=Archaeological Institute of Kashihara, Nara Prefecture|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11445218}} Museum)]] [[File:Kitazawagawa Daisekibo.jpg|thumb|The largest stone rod in Japan, the {{ill|Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|ja|北沢大石棒|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|1=Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18457535}}]] ''''Stone rods''' (せきぼう, sekibō) are a type of polished [[:en:Stone tool|Stone tool]] from the [[Jōmon period]] in Japan. They are believed to represent [[phalluses]] and are associated with [[:en:Rituals|Rituals]] and [[shamanistic practices]].Yamada 2019: 204
The largest known stone rod is the {{ill|Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|ja|北沢大石棒|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|1=Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18457535}} in [[:en:Sakuho, Nagano|Sakuho, Nagano]], measuring 223 cm in length and 25 cm in diameter.
== Overview == In a broad sense, "stone rods" encompass various rod-shaped stone artifacts, including [[stone swords]] and [[stone daggers]]. In a narrower sense, they often refer specifically to large stone rods.Tori 1924 These artifacts have been found throughout Japan, from [[Kyushu]] to [[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaidō]].
Phallic stone artifacts date back to the [[:en:Upper Paleolithic|Upper Paleolithic]] period, such as the one found at the {{ill|Masukata Site|ja|升形遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Masukata Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Ōami-Shirasato, Chiba|ja|大羽白里市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōami-Shirasato, Chiba|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, dated to approximately 24,000 years ago.Harunari 1996 The specific type known as "large stone rods" emerged during the Middle Jōmon period in the [[:en:Chūbu region|Chūbu region]] and later spread eastward to areas such as the [[:en:Kinki region|Kinki region]].Yamamoto 1979Okada 1960
Sites where stone rods were manufactured have been identified in eastern Japan, such as the {{ill|Shioya Kanesayama Shrine Site|ja|塩屋金清神社遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shioya Kanesayama Shrine Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and the {{ill|Shima Site|ja|島遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shima Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:Gifu Prefecture|Gifu Prefecture]],Hayakawa & Hayashi 2000Miyoshi & Nagata 2012 as well as in western Japan, such as the {{ill|Mikuraoka Site|ja|見蔵岡遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikuraoka Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:Hyōgo Prefecture|Hyōgo Prefecture]] and the {{ill|Kamizato Site|ja|上里遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamizato Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]].Matsui, Oshita & Tamura 1997Takahashi & Kondo 2011 These findings suggest that the use of stone rods as ritual objects was adopted as a cultural practice across different regions.Oshita 2001
Some stone rods have been found near the [[:en:hearths|hearths]] of dwellings, often showing signs of heat damage or discoloration. This indicates their use in fire-related [[:en:Rituals|Rituals]]. Ritual practices involving stone rods are thought to symbolically represent the phases of male genital arousal, sexual activity, and post-ejaculatory flaccidity through actions such as striking, heating, and breaking the rods. The prevalence of broken stone rods in archaeological sites has led some researchers to attribute symbolic meanings to this practice, though others remain skeptical.Gotō 1999
Stone rods were also used as burial goods, suggesting multiple [[:en:shamanistic|shamanistic]] or ritualistic purposes.Yamada 2019: 206Taniguchi 2006
[[File:Hinotodake Santyô.JPG|thumb|Stone rod at the summit of {{ill|Mt. Chōkaku|ja|丁岳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mt. Chōkaku|1=Hinoto Dake|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18338992}} on the border of [[:en:Akita Prefecture|Akita Prefecture]] and [[:en:Yamagata Prefecture|Yamagata Prefecture]]]]
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading ==
Tanaka, Taku & Sahara, Makoto (Eds.) 2002. ''Encyclopedia of Japanese Archaeology''. Sanseidō. pp. 487–488.
Tamura, Kōichi & Aida, Yoshimasa (Supervisors) 2004. ''Basic Terminology of Archaeological Exploration''. Yamakawa Publishing. p. 74.
Yamada, Yasuhiro. 2019. ''The History of the Jōmon Period''. Kodansha Modern Library.
Tori, Ryūzō. 1924. "Stone Rods and Stone Swords." ''Suwa History,'' Volume 1. Shinano Educational Association Suwa Branch.
Harunari, Hideō. 1996. "The Archaeology of Sexual Symbols." ''Bulletin of the National Museum of Japanese History,'' Issue 66.
Okada, Shigehiro. 1960. "Regional Development of Jōmon Culture in Kinki." ''The Jōmon Period'' (Japanese Archaeology II). Kawade Shobō Shinsha.
Gotō, Shinsuke. 1999. "Research on Artifacts: Stone Rods, Stone Swords, and Stone Daggers." ''The Jōmon Period,'' Issue 10, Vol. 4. The Jōmon Period Cultural Research Society.
Taniguchi, Yasuhiro. 2006. "Stone Rods and Stone Plates: Contexts of Symbolic Reproductive Acts." ''Archaeology IV.'' Masato Anzai.
Yamamoto, Teruhisa. 1979. "Evolution of Rituals with Stone Rods (Parts 1 & 2)." ''Ancient Culture,'' Vol. 31, Issues 11–12. Association for Ancient Studies.
Hayakawa, Shoichi & Hayashi, Naoki. 2000. ''Excavation Report of the Shioya Kanesayama Shrine Site (A Area).'' Gifu Prefecture & Miyagawa Village Board of Education.
Miyoshi, Seichō & Nagata, Tomoya. 2012. ''The Shima and Shioya Kanesayama Sites.'' Hida City Board of Education.
Matsui, Takayo; Oshita, Akira; Tamura, Seiichirō. 1997. ''Mikuraoka Site II.'' Takenochō Board of Education.
Takahashi, Kiyoshi & Kondo, Nao. 2010. ''The Kamizato Site I: A Jōmon Late-Period Settlement Excavation.'' Kyoto City Archaeological Research Center Report No. 24.
Oshita, Akira. 2001. "Adoption and Transformation of Large Stone Rods in the Kinki Region (Parts 1 & 2)." ''Stone Ritual Implements in the Transition from Jōmon to Yayoi Periods III.'' Spring Research Lab, National Museum of Japanese History. == See Also ==

[[Mishaguji]] — An ancient belief system originating in the Jōmon period, centered around stone rods and trees as sacred objects, prevalent in [[:en:Suwa, Nagano|Suwa]] and surrounding regions.

[[en:Sekibō]]
[[ja:石棒]] [[pl:Sekibō]] [[ru:Сэкибо]] [[ru:Sekibo]]
<!--Ishibou--><!--Ishibou-->
<!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{Commons category|Sekibō}} <!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{Commons category|Sekibō}} {{wikidata link|Q17994957}} [[Category:Archaeological artifacts]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Festival Utensils]] [[Category:Jōmon period]] [[Category:Lithics]] [[Category:Sexuality and religion]] [[Category:Suwa faith]] [[Category:translated pages with valid en interwikis]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Sekibō]][[en:Sekibō]][[ja:石棒]][[pl:Sekibō]][[ru:Сэкибо]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Sekibō]][[en:Sekibō]][[ja:石棒]][[pl:Sekibō]][[ru:Сэкибо]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Sekibō]][[en:Sekibō]][[ja:石棒]][[pl:Sekibō]][[ru:Сэкибо]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q17994957]] Wikidata name: sekibō [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:09, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Multiple issues | refimprove = March 2016 }} [[File:Sekibou.jpg|thumb|Stone rod ({{ill|Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties|ja|奈良県立橿原考古学研究所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties|1=Archaeological Institute of Kashihara, Nara Prefecture|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11445218}} Museum)]] [[File:Kitazawagawa Daisekibo.jpg|thumb|The largest stone rod in Japan, the {{ill|Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|ja|北沢大石棒|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|1=Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18457535}}]] ''''Stone rods''' (せきぼう, sekibō) are a type of polished [[:en:Stone tool|Stone tool]] from the [[Jōmon period]] in Japan. They are believed to represent [[phalluses]] and are associated with [[:en:Rituals|Rituals]] and [[shamanistic practices]].Yamada 2019: 204
The largest known stone rod is the {{ill|Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|ja|北沢大石棒|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|1=Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18457535}} in [[:en:Sakuho, Nagano|Sakuho, Nagano]], measuring 223 cm in length and 25 cm in diameter.
== Overview == In a broad sense, "stone rods" encompass various rod-shaped stone artifacts, including [[stone swords]] and [[stone daggers]]. In a narrower sense, they often refer specifically to large stone rods.Tori 1924 These artifacts have been found throughout Japan, from [[Kyushu]] to [[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaidō]].
Phallic stone artifacts date back to the [[:en:Upper Paleolithic|Upper Paleolithic]] period, such as the one found at the {{ill|Masukata Site|ja|升形遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Masukata Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Ōami-Shirasato, Chiba|ja|大羽白里市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōami-Shirasato, Chiba|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, dated to approximately 24,000 years ago.Harunari 1996 The specific type known as "large stone rods" emerged during the Middle Jōmon period in the [[:en:Chūbu region|Chūbu region]] and later spread eastward to areas such as the [[:en:Kinki region|Kinki region]].Yamamoto 1979Okada 1960
Sites where stone rods were manufactured have been identified in eastern Japan, such as the {{ill|Shioya Kanesayama Shrine Site|ja|塩屋金清神社遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shioya Kanesayama Shrine Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and the {{ill|Shima Site|ja|島遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shima Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:Gifu Prefecture|Gifu Prefecture]],Hayakawa & Hayashi 2000Miyoshi & Nagata 2012 as well as in western Japan, such as the {{ill|Mikuraoka Site|ja|見蔵岡遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikuraoka Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:Hyōgo Prefecture|Hyōgo Prefecture]] and the {{ill|Kamizato Site|ja|上里遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamizato Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]].Matsui, Oshita & Tamura 1997Takahashi & Kondo 2011 These findings suggest that the use of stone rods as ritual objects was adopted as a cultural practice across different regions.Oshita 2001
Some stone rods have been found near the [[:en:hearths|hearths]] of dwellings, often showing signs of heat damage or discoloration. This indicates their use in fire-related [[:en:Rituals|Rituals]]. Ritual practices involving stone rods are thought to symbolically represent the phases of male genital arousal, sexual activity, and post-ejaculatory flaccidity through actions such as striking, heating, and breaking the rods. The prevalence of broken stone rods in archaeological sites has led some researchers to attribute symbolic meanings to this practice, though others remain skeptical.Gotō 1999
Stone rods were also used as burial goods, suggesting multiple [[:en:shamanistic|shamanistic]] or ritualistic purposes.Yamada 2019: 206Taniguchi 2006
[[File:Hinotodake Santyô.JPG|thumb|Stone rod at the summit of {{ill|Mt. Chōkaku|ja|丁岳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mt. Chōkaku|1=Hinoto Dake|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18338992}} on the border of [[:en:Akita Prefecture|Akita Prefecture]] and [[:en:Yamagata Prefecture|Yamagata Prefecture]]]]

Okada, Shigehiro. 1960. "Regional Development of Jōmon Culture in Kinki." ''The Jōmon Period'' (Japanese Archaeology II). Kawade Shobō Shinsha.
Gotō, Shinsuke. 1999. "Research on Artifacts: Stone Rods, Stone Swords, and Stone Daggers." ''The Jōmon Period,'' Issue 10, Vol. 4. The Jōmon Period Cultural Research Society.
Taniguchi, Yasuhiro. 2006. "Stone Rods and Stone Plates: Contexts of Symbolic Reproductive Acts." ''Archaeology IV.'' Masato Anzai.

Oshita, Akira. 2001. "Adoption and Transformation of Large Stone Rods in the Kinki Region (Parts 1 & 2)." ''Stone Ritual Implements in the Transition from Jōmon to Yayoi Periods III.'' Spring Research Lab, National Museum of Japanese History. == See Also ==

[[:en:Snake deity|Snake deity]]
== External Links ==
[http://www.tamagawa.ac.jp/museum/archive/2001/119_1.html Stone rods at Tamagawa University Education Museum]
[http://www.city.kunitachi.tokyo.jp/dbps_data/material/localhost/100000/101000/pdf/2012.07.10midorikawahigasiiseki.pdf Stone rods discovered at the Midorikawa East Site (PDF) – Kunitachi City]
{{Japan-hist-stub}} ==References== {{reflist}} {{Suwa Faith}}

[[en:Sekibō]]
[[ja:石棒]] [[pl:Sekibō]] [[ru:Сэкибо]] [[ru:Sekibo]]
<!--Ishibou--><!--Ishibou--> <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{Commons category|Sekibō}} <!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{Commons category|Sekibō}} {{wikidata link|Q17994957}} [[Category:Archaeological artifacts]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Festival Utensils]] [[Category:Jōmon period]] [[Category:Lithics]] [[Category:Sexuality and religion]] [[Category:Suwa faith]] [[Category:translated pages with valid en interwikis]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Sekibō]][[en:Sekibō]][[ja:石棒]][[pl:Sekibō]][[ru:Сэкибо]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Sekibō]][[en:Sekibō]][[ja:石棒]][[pl:Sekibō]][[ru:Сэкибо]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Sekibō]][[en:Sekibō]][[ja:石棒]][[pl:Sekibō]][[ru:Сэкибо]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q17994957]] Wikidata name: sekibō [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:09, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Multiple issues | refimprove = March 2016 }} [[File:Sekibou.jpg|thumb|Stone rod ({{ill|Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties|ja|奈良県立橿原考古学研究所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties|1=Archaeological Institute of Kashihara, Nara Prefecture|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11445218}} Museum)]] [[File:Kitazawagawa Daisekibo.jpg|thumb|The largest stone rod in Japan, the {{ill|Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|ja|北沢大石棒|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|1=Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18457535}}]] ''''Stone rods''' (せきぼう, sekibō) are a type of polished [[:en:Stone tool|Stone tool]] from the [[Jōmon period]] in Japan. They are believed to represent [[phalluses]] and are associated with [[:en:Rituals|Rituals]] and [[shamanistic practices]].Yamada 2019: 204
The largest known stone rod is the {{ill|Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|ja|北沢大石棒|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|1=Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18457535}} in [[:en:Sakuho, Nagano|Sakuho, Nagano]], measuring 223 cm in length and 25 cm in diameter.
== Overview == In a broad sense, "stone rods" encompass various rod-shaped stone artifacts, including [[stone swords]] and [[stone daggers]]. In a narrower sense, they often refer specifically to large stone rods.Tori 1924 These artifacts have been found throughout Japan, from [[Kyushu]] to [[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaidō]].
Phallic stone artifacts date back to the [[:en:Upper Paleolithic|Upper Paleolithic]] period, such as the one found at the {{ill|Masukata Site|ja|升形遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Masukata Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Ōami-Shirasato, Chiba|ja|大羽白里市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōami-Shirasato, Chiba|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, dated to approximately 24,000 years ago.Harunari 1996 The specific type known as "large stone rods" emerged during the Middle Jōmon period in the [[:en:Chūbu region|Chūbu region]] and later spread eastward to areas such as the [[:en:Kinki region|Kinki region]].Yamamoto 1979Okada 1960
Sites where stone rods were manufactured have been identified in eastern Japan, such as the {{ill|Shioya Kanesayama Shrine Site|ja|塩屋金清神社遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shioya Kanesayama Shrine Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and the {{ill|Shima Site|ja|島遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shima Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:Gifu Prefecture|Gifu Prefecture]],Hayakawa & Hayashi 2000Miyoshi & Nagata 2012 as well as in western Japan, such as the {{ill|Mikuraoka Site|ja|見蔵岡遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikuraoka Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:Hyōgo Prefecture|Hyōgo Prefecture]] and the {{ill|Kamizato Site|ja|上里遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamizato Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]].Matsui, Oshita & Tamura 1997Takahashi & Kondo 2011 These findings suggest that the use of stone rods as ritual objects was adopted as a cultural practice across different regions.Oshita 2001
Some stone rods have been found near the [[:en:hearths|hearths]] of dwellings, often showing signs of heat damage or discoloration. This indicates their use in fire-related [[:en:Rituals|Rituals]]. Ritual practices involving stone rods are thought to symbolically represent the phases of male genital arousal, sexual activity, and post-ejaculatory flaccidity through actions such as striking, heating, and breaking the rods. The prevalence of broken stone rods in archaeological sites has led some researchers to attribute symbolic meanings to this practice, though others remain skeptical.Gotō 1999
Stone rods were also used as burial goods, suggesting multiple [[:en:shamanistic|shamanistic]] or ritualistic purposes.Yamada 2019: 206Taniguchi 2006
[[File:Hinotodake Santyô.JPG|thumb|Stone rod at the summit of {{ill|Mt. Chōkaku|ja|丁岳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mt. Chōkaku|1=Hinoto Dake|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18338992}} on the border of [[:en:Akita Prefecture|Akita Prefecture]] and [[:en:Yamagata Prefecture|Yamagata Prefecture]]]]
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading ==
Tanaka, Taku & Sahara, Makoto (Eds.) 2002. ''Encyclopedia of Japanese Archaeology''. Sanseidō. pp. 487–488.
Tamura, Kōichi & Aida, Yoshimasa (Supervisors) 2004. ''Basic Terminology of Archaeological Exploration''. Yamakawa Publishing. p. 74.
Yamada, Yasuhiro. 2019. ''The History of the Jōmon Period''. Kodansha Modern Library.
Tori, Ryūzō. 1924. "Stone Rods and Stone Swords." ''Suwa History,'' Volume 1. Shinano Educational Association Suwa Branch.
Harunari, Hideō. 1996. "The Archaeology of Sexual Symbols." ''Bulletin of the National Museum of Japanese History,'' Issue 66.
Okada, Shigehiro. 1960. "Regional Development of Jōmon Culture in Kinki." ''The Jōmon Period'' (Japanese Archaeology II). Kawade Shobō Shinsha.
Gotō, Shinsuke. 1999. "Research on Artifacts: Stone Rods, Stone Swords, and Stone Daggers." ''The Jōmon Period,'' Issue 10, Vol. 4. The Jōmon Period Cultural Research Society.
Taniguchi, Yasuhiro. 2006. "Stone Rods and Stone Plates: Contexts of Symbolic Reproductive Acts." ''Archaeology IV.'' Masato Anzai.
Yamamoto, Teruhisa. 1979. "Evolution of Rituals with Stone Rods (Parts 1 & 2)." ''Ancient Culture,'' Vol. 31, Issues 11–12. Association for Ancient Studies.
Hayakawa, Shoichi & Hayashi, Naoki. 2000. ''Excavation Report of the Shioya Kanesayama Shrine Site (A Area).'' Gifu Prefecture & Miyagawa Village Board of Education.
Miyoshi, Seichō & Nagata, Tomoya. 2012. ''The Shima and Shioya Kanesayama Sites.'' Hida City Board of Education.
Matsui, Takayo; Oshita, Akira; Tamura, Seiichirō. 1997. ''Mikuraoka Site II.'' Takenochō Board of Education.
Takahashi, Kiyoshi & Kondo, Nao. 2010. ''The Kamizato Site I: A Jōmon Late-Period Settlement Excavation.'' Kyoto City Archaeological Research Center Report No. 24.
Oshita, Akira. 2001. "Adoption and Transformation of Large Stone Rods in the Kinki Region (Parts 1 & 2)." ''Stone Ritual Implements in the Transition from Jōmon to Yayoi Periods III.'' Spring Research Lab, National Museum of Japanese History. == See Also ==

Gotō, Shinsuke. 1999. "Research on Artifacts: Stone Rods, Stone Swords, and Stone Daggers." ''The Jōmon Period,'' Issue 10, Vol. 4. The Jōmon Period Cultural Research Society.
Taniguchi, Yasuhiro. 2006. "Stone Rods and Stone Plates: Contexts of Symbolic Reproductive Acts." ''Archaeology IV.'' Masato Anzai.
Yamamoto, Teruhisa. 1979. "Evolution of Rituals with Stone Rods (Parts 1 & 2)." ''Ancient Culture,'' Vol. 31, Issues 11–12. Association for Ancient Studies.
Hayakawa, Shoichi & Hayashi, Naoki. 2000. ''Excavation Report of the Shioya Kanesayama Shrine Site (A Area).'' Gifu Prefecture & Miyagawa Village Board of Education.
Miyoshi, Seichō & Nagata, Tomoya. 2012. ''The Shima and Shioya Kanesayama Sites.'' Hida City Board of Education.
Matsui, Takayo; Oshita, Akira; Tamura, Seiichirō. 1997. ''Mikuraoka Site II.'' Takenochō Board of Education.
Takahashi, Kiyoshi & Kondo, Nao. 2010. ''The Kamizato Site I: A Jōmon Late-Period Settlement Excavation.'' Kyoto City Archaeological Research Center Report No. 24.
Oshita, Akira. 2001. "Adoption and Transformation of Large Stone Rods in the Kinki Region (Parts 1 & 2)." ''Stone Ritual Implements in the Transition from Jōmon to Yayoi Periods III.'' Spring Research Lab, National Museum of Japanese History. == See Also ==

<!--Ishibou--><!--Ishibou--> <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{Commons category|Sekibō}} <!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{Commons category|Sekibō}} {{wikidata link|Q17994957}} [[Category:Archaeological artifacts]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Festival Utensils]] [[Category:Jōmon period]] [[Category:Lithics]] [[Category:Sexuality and religion]] [[Category:Suwa faith]] [[Category:translated pages with valid en interwikis]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Sekibō]][[en:Sekibō]][[ja:石棒]][[pl:Sekibō]][[ru:Сэкибо]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Sekibō]][[en:Sekibō]][[ja:石棒]][[pl:Sekibō]][[ru:Сэкибо]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Sekibō]][[en:Sekibō]][[ja:石棒]][[pl:Sekibō]][[ru:Сэкибо]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q17994957]] Wikidata name: sekibō [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:09, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Multiple issues | refimprove = March 2016 }} [[File:Sekibou.jpg|thumb|Stone rod ({{ill|Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties|ja|奈良県立橿原考古学研究所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties|1=Archaeological Institute of Kashihara, Nara Prefecture|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11445218}} Museum)]] [[File:Kitazawagawa Daisekibo.jpg|thumb|The largest stone rod in Japan, the {{ill|Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|ja|北沢大石棒|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|1=Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18457535}}]] ''''Stone rods''' (せきぼう, sekibō) are a type of polished {{ill|Stone tool|en|Stone tool|WD=Q479257|lt=Stone tool}} from the [[Jōmon period]] in Japan. They are believed to represent [[phalluses]] and are associated with [[:en:Rituals|Rituals]] and [[shamanistic practices]].Yamada 2019: 204
The largest known stone rod is the {{ill|Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|ja|北沢大石棒|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|1=Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18457535}} in {{ill|Sakuho, Nagano|en|Sakuho, Nagano|WD=Q1203700|lt=Sakuho, Nagano}}, measuring 223 cm in length and 25 cm in diameter.
== Overview == In a broad sense, "stone rods" encompass various rod-shaped stone artifacts, including [[stone swords]] and [[stone daggers]]. In a narrower sense, they often refer specifically to large stone rods.Tori 1924 These artifacts have been found throughout Japan, from [[Kyushu]] to [[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaidō]].
Phallic stone artifacts date back to the {{ill|Upper Paleolithic|en|Upper Paleolithic|WD=Q479505|lt=Upper Paleolithic}} period, such as the one found at the {{ill|Masukata Site|ja|升形遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Masukata Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Ōami-Shirasato, Chiba|ja|大羽白里市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōami-Shirasato, Chiba|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, dated to approximately 24,000 years ago.Harunari 1996 The specific type known as "large stone rods" emerged during the Middle Jōmon period in the {{ill|Chūbu region|en|Chūbu region|WD=Q134638|lt=Chūbu region}} and later spread eastward to areas such as the [[:en:Kinki region|Kinki region]].Yamamoto 1979Okada 1960
Sites where stone rods were manufactured have been identified in eastern Japan, such as the {{ill|Shioya Kanesayama Shrine Site|ja|塩屋金清神社遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shioya Kanesayama Shrine Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and the {{ill|Shima Site|ja|島遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shima Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Gifu Prefecture|en|Gifu Prefecture|WD=Q131277|lt=Gifu Prefecture}},Hayakawa & Hayashi 2000Miyoshi & Nagata 2012 as well as in western Japan, such as the {{ill|Mikuraoka Site|ja|見蔵岡遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikuraoka Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|en|Hyōgo Prefecture|WD=Q130290|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture}} and the {{ill|Kamizato Site|ja|上里遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamizato Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|en|Kyoto Prefecture|WD=Q120730|lt=Kyoto Prefecture}}.Matsui, Oshita & Tamura 1997Takahashi & Kondo 2011 These findings suggest that the use of stone rods as ritual objects was adopted as a cultural practice across different regions.Oshita 2001
Some stone rods have been found near the [[:en:hearths|hearths]] of dwellings, often showing signs of heat damage or discoloration. This indicates their use in fire-related [[:en:Rituals|Rituals]]. Ritual practices involving stone rods are thought to symbolically represent the phases of male genital arousal, sexual activity, and post-ejaculatory flaccidity through actions such as striking, heating, and breaking the rods. The prevalence of broken stone rods in archaeological sites has led some researchers to attribute symbolic meanings to this practice, though others remain skeptical.Gotō 1999
Stone rods were also used as burial goods, suggesting multiple [[:en:shamanistic|shamanistic]] or ritualistic purposes.Yamada 2019: 206Taniguchi 2006
[[File:Hinotodake Santyô.JPG|thumb|Stone rod at the summit of {{ill|Mt. Chōkaku|ja|丁岳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mt. Chōkaku|1=Hinoto Dake|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18338992}} on the border of {{ill|Akita Prefecture|en|Akita Prefecture|WD=Q81863|lt=Akita Prefecture}} and {{ill|Yamagata Prefecture|en|Yamagata Prefecture|WD=Q125863|lt=Yamagata Prefecture}}]]

In a narrower sense, they often refer specifically to large stone rods.Tori 1924 These artifacts have been found throughout Japan, from [[Kyushu]] to [[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaidō]].
Phallic stone artifacts date back to the {{ill|Upper Paleolithic|en|Upper Paleolithic|WD=Q479505|lt=Upper Paleolithic}} period, such as the one found at the {{ill|Masukata Site|ja|升形遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Masukata Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Ōami-Shirasato, Chiba|ja|大羽白里市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōami-Shirasato, Chiba|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, dated to approximately 24,000 years ago.Harunari 1996 The specific type known as "large stone rods" emerged during the Middle Jōmon period in the {{ill|Chūbu region|en|Chūbu region|WD=Q134638|lt=Chūbu region}} and later spread eastward to areas such as the [[:en:Kinki region|Kinki region]].Yamamoto 1979Okada 1960
Sites where stone rods were manufactured have been identified in eastern Japan, such as the {{ill|Shioya Kanesayama Shrine Site|ja|塩屋金清神社遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shioya Kanesayama Shrine Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and the {{ill|Shima Site|ja|島遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shima Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Gifu Prefecture|en|Gifu Prefecture|WD=Q131277|lt=Gifu Prefecture}},Hayakawa & Hayashi 2000Miyoshi & Nagata 2012 as well as in western Japan, such as the {{ill|Mikuraoka Site|ja|見蔵岡遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikuraoka Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|en|Hyōgo Prefecture|WD=Q130290|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture}} and the {{ill|Kamizato Site|ja|上里遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamizato Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|en|Kyoto Prefecture|WD=Q120730|lt=Kyoto Prefecture}}.Matsui, Oshita & Tamura 1997Takahashi & Kondo 2011 These findings suggest that the use of stone rods as ritual objects was adopted as a cultural practice across different regions.Oshita 2001
Some stone rods have been found near the [[:en:hearths|hearths]] of dwellings, often showing signs of heat damage or discoloration. This indicates their use in fire-related [[:en:Rituals|Rituals]]. Ritual practices involving stone rods are thought to symbolically represent the phases of male genital arousal, sexual activity, and post-ejaculatory flaccidity through actions such as striking, heating, and breaking the rods. The prevalence of broken stone rods in archaeological sites has led some researchers to attribute symbolic meanings to this practice, though others remain skeptical.Gotō 1999
Stone rods were also used as burial goods, suggesting multiple [[:en:shamanistic|shamanistic]] or ritualistic purposes.Yamada 2019: 206Taniguchi 2006
[[File:Hinotodake Santyô.JPG|thumb|Stone rod at the summit of {{ill|Mt. Chōkaku|ja|丁岳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mt. Chōkaku|1=Hinoto Dake|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18338992}} on the border of {{ill|Akita Prefecture|en|Akita Prefecture|WD=Q81863|lt=Akita Prefecture}} and {{ill|Yamagata Prefecture|en|Yamagata Prefecture|WD=Q125863|lt=Yamagata Prefecture}}]]

Hayakawa, Shoichi & Hayashi, Naoki. 2000. ''Excavation Report of the Shioya Kanesayama Shrine Site (A Area).'' Gifu Prefecture & Miyagawa Village Board of Education.
Miyoshi, Seichō & Nagata, Tomoya. 2012. ''The Shima and Shioya Kanesayama Sites.'' Hida City Board of Education.

Takahashi, Kiyoshi & Kondo, Nao. 2010. ''The Kamizato Site I: A Jōmon Late-Period Settlement Excavation.'' Kyoto City Archaeological Research Center Report No. 24.
Oshita, Akira. 2001. "Adoption and Transformation of Large Stone Rods in the Kinki Region (Parts 1 & 2)." ''Stone Ritual Implements in the Transition from Jōmon to Yayoi Periods III.'' Spring Research Lab, National Museum of Japanese History. == See Also ==

{{Multiple issues | refimprove = March 2016 }} [[File:Sekibou.jpg|thumb|Stone rod ({{ill|Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties|ja|奈良県立橿原考古学研究所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties|1=Kashihara Archaeological Institute|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11445218}} Museum)]] [[File:Kitazawagawa Daisekibo.jpg|thumb|The largest stone rod in Japan, the {{ill|Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|ja|北沢大石棒|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|1=Great Sekibō of Kitazawa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18457535}}]] ''''Stone rods''' (せきぼう, sekibō) are a type of polished {{ill|Stone tool|en|Stone tool|WD=Q479257|lt=Stone tool|1=Stone tool}} from the [[Jōmon period]] in Japan. They are believed to represent [[phalluses]] and are associated with [[:en:Rituals|Rituals]] and [[shamanistic practices]].Yamada 2019: 204
The largest known stone rod is the {{ill|Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|ja|北沢大石棒|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|1=Great Sekibō of Kitazawa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18457535}} in {{ill|Sakuho, Nagano|en|Sakuho, Nagano|WD=Q1203700|lt=Sakuho, Nagano|1=Sakuho, Nagano}}, measuring 223 cm in length and 25 cm in diameter.
== Overview == In a broad sense, "stone rods" encompass various rod-shaped stone artifacts, including [[stone swords]] and [[stone daggers]]. In a narrower sense, they often refer specifically to large stone rods.Tori 1924 These artifacts have been found throughout Japan, from [[Kyushu]] to [[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaidō]].
Phallic stone artifacts date back to the {{ill|Upper Paleolithic|en|Upper Paleolithic|WD=Q479505|lt=Upper Paleolithic|1=Upper Paleolithic}} period, such as the one found at the {{ill|Masukata Site|ja|升形遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Masukata Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Ōami-Shirasato, Chiba|ja|大羽白里市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōami-Shirasato, Chiba|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, dated to approximately 24,000 years ago.Harunari 1996 The specific type known as "large stone rods" emerged during the Middle Jōmon period in the {{ill|Chūbu region|en|Chūbu region|WD=Q134638|lt=Chūbu region|1=Chūbu region}} and later spread eastward to areas such as the [[:en:Kinki region|Kinki region]].Yamamoto 1979Okada 1960
Sites where stone rods were manufactured have been identified in eastern Japan, such as the {{ill|Shioya Kanesayama Shrine Site|ja|塩屋金清神社遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shioya Kanesayama Shrine Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and the {{ill|Shima Site|ja|島遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shima Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Gifu Prefecture|en|Gifu Prefecture|WD=Q131277|lt=Gifu Prefecture|1=Gifu Prefecture}},Hayakawa & Hayashi 2000Miyoshi & Nagata 2012 as well as in western Japan, such as the {{ill|Mikuraoka Site|ja|見蔵岡遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikuraoka Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|en|Hyōgo Prefecture|WD=Q130290|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}} and the {{ill|Kamizato Site|ja|上里遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamizato Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|en|Kyoto Prefecture|WD=Q120730|lt=Kyoto Prefecture|1=Kyoto Prefecture}}.Matsui, Oshita & Tamura 1997Takahashi & Kondo 2011 These findings suggest that the use of stone rods as ritual objects was adopted as a cultural practice across different regions.Oshita 2001
Some stone rods have been found near the [[:en:hearths|hearths]] of dwellings, often showing signs of heat damage or discoloration. This indicates their use in fire-related [[:en:Rituals|Rituals]]. Ritual practices involving stone rods are thought to symbolically represent the phases of male genital arousal, sexual activity, and post-ejaculatory flaccidity through actions such as striking, heating, and breaking the rods. The prevalence of broken stone rods in archaeological sites has led some researchers to attribute symbolic meanings to this practice, though others remain skeptical.Gotō 1999
Stone rods were also used as burial goods, suggesting multiple [[:en:shamanistic|shamanistic]] or ritualistic purposes.Yamada 2019: 206Taniguchi 2006
[[File:Hinotodake Santyô.JPG|thumb|Stone rod at the summit of {{ill|Mt. Chōkaku|ja|丁岳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mt. Chōkaku|1=Hinoto Dake|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18338992}} on the border of {{ill|Akita Prefecture|en|Akita Prefecture|WD=Q81863|lt=Akita Prefecture|1=Akita Prefecture}} and {{ill|Yamagata Prefecture|en|Yamagata Prefecture|WD=Q125863|lt=Yamagata Prefecture|1=Yamagata Prefecture}}]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Sekibō]][[en:Sekibō]][[ja:石棒]][[pl:Sekibō]][[ru:Сэкибо]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Sekibō]][[en:Sekibō]][[ja:石棒]][[pl:Sekibō]][[ru:Сэкибо]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[Category:sn_crud|TESTcommons:Category:Sekibō]][[en:Sekibō]][[ja:石棒]][[pl:Sekibō]][[ru:Сэкибо]]
{{Multiple issues | refimprove = March 2016 }} [[File:Sekibou.jpg|thumb|Stone rod ({{ill|Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties|ja|奈良県立橿原考古学研究所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties|1=Kashihara Archaeological Institute|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11445218}} Museum)]] [[File:Kitazawagawa Daisekibo.jpg|thumb|The largest stone rod in Japan, the {{ill|Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|ja|北沢大石棒|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|1=Great Sekibō of Kitazawa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18457535}}]] ''''Stone rods''' (せきぼう, sekibō) are a type of polished {{ill|Stone tool|en|Stone tool|WD=Q479257|lt=Stone tool|1=Stone tool}} from the [[Jōmon period]] in Japan. They are believed to represent [[phalluses]] and are associated with [[:en:Rituals|Rituals]] and [[shamanistic practices]].Yamada 2019: 204
The largest known stone rod is the {{ill|Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|ja|北沢大石棒|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitazawa Great Stone Rod|1=Great Sekibō of Kitazawa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18457535}} in {{ill|Sakuho, Nagano|en|Sakuho, Nagano|WD=Q1203700|lt=Sakuho, Nagano|1=Sakuho, Nagano}}, measuring 223 cm in length and 25 cm in diameter.
== Overview == In a broad sense, "stone rods" encompass various rod-shaped stone artifacts, including [[stone swords]] and [[stone daggers]]. In a narrower sense, they often refer specifically to large stone rods.Tori 1924 These artifacts have been found throughout Japan, from [[Kyushu]] to [[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaidō]].
Phallic stone artifacts date back to the {{ill|Upper Paleolithic|en|Upper Paleolithic|WD=Q479505|lt=Upper Paleolithic|1=Upper Paleolithic}} period, such as the one found at the {{ill|Masukata Site|ja|升形遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Masukata Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Ōami-Shirasato, Chiba|ja|大羽白里市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōami-Shirasato, Chiba|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, dated to approximately 24,000 years ago.Harunari 1996 The specific type known as "large stone rods" emerged during the Middle Jōmon period in the {{ill|Chūbu region|en|Chūbu region|WD=Q134638|lt=Chūbu region|1=Chūbu region}} and later spread eastward to areas such as the [[:en:Kinki region|Kinki region]].Yamamoto 1979Okada 1960
Sites where stone rods were manufactured have been identified in eastern Japan, such as the {{ill|Shioya Kanesayama Shrine Site|ja|塩屋金清神社遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shioya Kanesayama Shrine Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and the {{ill|Shima Site|ja|島遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shima Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Gifu Prefecture|en|Gifu Prefecture|WD=Q131277|lt=Gifu Prefecture|1=Gifu Prefecture}},Hayakawa & Hayashi 2000Miyoshi & Nagata 2012 as well as in western Japan, such as the {{ill|Mikuraoka Site|ja|見蔵岡遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikuraoka Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|en|Hyōgo Prefecture|WD=Q130290|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}} and the {{ill|Kamizato Site|ja|上里遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamizato Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|en|Kyoto Prefecture|WD=Q120730|lt=Kyoto Prefecture|1=Kyoto Prefecture}}.Matsui, Oshita & Tamura 1997Takahashi & Kondo 2011 These findings suggest that the use of stone rods as ritual objects was adopted as a cultural practice across different regions.Oshita 2001
Some stone rods have been found near the [[:en:hearths|hearths]] of dwellings, often showing signs of heat damage or discoloration. This indicates their use in fire-related [[:en:Rituals|Rituals]]. Ritual practices involving stone rods are thought to symbolically represent the phases of male genital arousal, sexual activity, and post-ejaculatory flaccidity through actions such as striking, heating, and breaking the rods. The prevalence of broken stone rods in archaeological sites has led some researchers to attribute symbolic meanings to this practice, though others remain skeptical.Gotō 1999
Stone rods were also used as burial goods, suggesting multiple [[:en:shamanistic|shamanistic]] or ritualistic purposes.Yamada 2019: 206Taniguchi 2006
[[File:Hinotodake Santyô.JPG|thumb|Stone rod at the summit of {{ill|Mt. Chōkaku|ja|丁岳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mt. Chōkaku|1=Hinoto Dake|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18338992}} on the border of {{ill|Akita Prefecture|en|Akita Prefecture|WD=Q81863|lt=Akita Prefecture|1=Akita Prefecture}} and {{ill|Yamagata Prefecture|en|Yamagata Prefecture|WD=Q125863|lt=Yamagata Prefecture|1=Yamagata Prefecture}}]]

== Further Reading ==
Tanaka, Taku & Sahara, Makoto (Eds.) 2002. ''Encyclopedia of Japanese Archaeology''. Sanseidō. pp. 487–488.
Tamura, Kōichi & Aida, Yoshimasa (Supervisors) 2004. ''Basic Terminology of Archaeological Exploration''. Yamakawa Publishing. p. 74.
Yamada, Yasuhiro. 2019. ''The History of the Jōmon Period''. Kodansha Modern Library.
Tori, Ryūzō. 1924. "Stone Rods and Stone Swords." ''Suwa History,'' Volume 1. Shinano Educational Association Suwa Branch.
Harunari, Hideō. 1996. "The Archaeology of Sexual Symbols." ''Bulletin of the National Museum of Japanese History,'' Issue 66.
Okada, Shigehiro. 1960. "Regional Development of Jōmon Culture in Kinki." ''The Jōmon Period'' (Japanese Archaeology II). Kawade Shobō Shinsha.
Gotō, Shinsuke. 1999. "Research on Artifacts: Stone Rods, Stone Swords, and Stone Daggers." ''The Jōmon Period,'' Issue 10, Vol. 4. The Jōmon Period Cultural Research Society.
Taniguchi, Yasuhiro. 2006. "Stone Rods and Stone Plates: Contexts of Symbolic Reproductive Acts." ''Archaeology IV.'' Masato Anzai.
Yamamoto, Teruhisa. 1979. "Evolution of Rituals with Stone Rods (Parts 1 & 2)." ''Ancient Culture,'' Vol. 31, Issues 11–12. Association for Ancient Studies.
Hayakawa, Shoichi & Hayashi, Naoki. 2000. ''Excavation Report of the Shioya Kanesayama Shrine Site (A Area).'' Gifu Prefecture & Miyagawa Village Board of Education.
Miyoshi, Seichō & Nagata, Tomoya. 2012. ''The Shima and Shioya Kanesayama Sites.'' Hida City Board of Education.
Matsui, Takayo; Oshita, Akira; Tamura, Seiichirō. 1997. ''Mikuraoka Site II.'' Takenochō Board of Education.
Takahashi, Kiyoshi & Kondo, Nao. 2010. ''The Kamizato Site I: A Jōmon Late-Period Settlement Excavation.'' Kyoto City Archaeological Research Center Report No. 24.
Oshita, Akira. 2001. "Adoption and Transformation of Large Stone Rods in the Kinki Region (Parts 1 & 2)." ''Stone Ritual Implements in the Transition from Jōmon to Yayoi Periods III.'' Spring Research Lab, National Museum of Japanese History. == See Also ==
